Research pipeline
session.dispose()
candidates.text:
### `session.dispose()`

Prune the trunk and set it to null.

```typescript
await session.dispose();
```

<Note>
Session does NOT own the `SessionContext` or `BranchStore`. You create those separately and pass them in. `dispose()` only prunes the trunk. Clean up `ctx` and `store` yourself when shutting down.
</Note>

candidates.heading: What's not on this page (and where it lives)
candidates.text:
## What's not on this page (and where it lives)

The concept spine deliberately stops at *names and relationships*. Everything below is mechanism — read it when you're building or debugging the corresponding piece:

| Mechanism | Lives in |
| --- | --- |
| Tick phases (SPAWN+EXTEND · PRODUCE · COMMIT · SETTLE · DISPATCH) | [Concurrency](/under-the-hood/concurrency) |
| KV pressure, recovery, force-kill | [KV pressure](/under-the-hood/kv-pressure) |
| Branch fork / prefill / prune mechanics | [Branch lifecycle](/under-the-hood/branch-lifecycle) |
| How the Spine is rendered (catalog, boundary marker, tool-selection rule) | [Continuous-context spine](/under-the-hood/continuous-context-spine) |
| GBNF grammar, tool ordering, eager vs lazy | [Grammar & ordering](/under-the-hood/grammar-and-ordering) |
| Reranker scoring (logit-diff, floor) | [Sources & retrieval](/build-an-app/sources-and-retrieval) |
| App registry scope model, factory/teardown | [Lifecycle & registry](/build-an-app/lifecycle-and-registry) |
| The App protocol (boundary marker, intro, catalog format) | [What is an App](/build-an-app/what-is-an-app#the-app-protocol) |

candidates.heading: Make it yours
candidates.text:
## Make it yours

The whole orchestration is one parameter — `orchestrate`. Drop in any of these to reshape the harness:

```typescript
// Independent topics — fan out, no spine extension. Each agent runs on the same GPU.
orchestrate: parallel(topics.map(t => ({ content: t, systemPrompt: RESEARCH_PROMPT, seed })))

// A landscape pass that informs N parallel domain agents
orchestrate: fanout(landscapeStep, domainSteps)

// Arbitrary dependency graph — research two topics in parallel, compare, synth
orchestrate: dag([
  { id: 'research_A', task: { content: topicA, systemPrompt: RESEARCH_PROMPT } },
  { id: 'research_B', task: { content: topicB, systemPrompt: RESEARCH_PROMPT } },
  { id: 'compare',    dependsOn: ['research_A', 'research_B'],
    task: { content: 'Compare A vs B', systemPrompt: COMPARE_PROMPT } },
  { id: 'synth',      dependsOn: ['compare'],
    task: { content: 'Synthesize',     systemPrompt: SYNTH_PROMPT } },
])
```

The `dag` shape is what `examples/compare/` uses — a real harness that compares two subjects across multiple axes in 250 LOC. Read it after this quick-start.

candidates.heading: v0.2
candidates.text:
## v0.2

- **`spawnAgents()` removed.** Pool orchestration moved to a callback interface. Use `agentPool({ orchestrate: ... })` or `useAgentPool({ orchestrate: ... })` instead. The `orchestrate` argument accepts an `Orchestrator = (ctx: PoolContext) => Operation<void>` — typically one of the built-in combinators (`parallel`, `chain`, `fanout`, `dag`, `dagWithEvents`).
- **`PoolContext` introduced** as the orchestrator-facing API: `spine`, `spawn()`, `waitFor()`, `extendSpine()`, `canFit()`. Custom orchestrators are now ~10–30 line implementations of the `Orchestrator` shape.

candidates.heading: Required
candidates.text:
#### Required

- **`orchestrate`** — what shape (`parallel` / `chain` / `fanout` / `dag` / custom). Mandatory; no default makes sense.
- **`tools`** — what data access. Optional in the type but practically required (otherwise use `useAgent`).
- **`terminalTool`** — name string for the "I'm done" tool (e.g. `'report'`). Without it, agents exit via free-text-stop or recovery extraction — both heavier and less clean. Practically required for any harness with non-trivial tool use.

candidates.heading: The building blocks
candidates.text:
## The building blocks

Three primitives implement the work inside a stage:

<CardGroup cols={3}>
  <Card title="agent()" icon="wand-magic-sparkles">
    Single agent. Plan decomposition (with `schema`), JSON extraction, evaluation. Returns an Agent with result and raw output.
  </Card>
  <Card title="agentPool()" icon="users">
    Multi-agent pool with tools and an `orchestrate` argument that picks how the pool's agents relate (parallel / chain / fanout / dag).
  </Card>
  <Card title="diverge()" icon="code-branch">
    Multi-branch perplexity selection. Fork N attempts, generate in parallel, select the lowest-perplexity output. Verification and confidence checking.
  </Card>
</CardGroup>

`reduce()` is a stage-level combinator (covered in [Multi-source pipelines](#multi-source-pipelines-with-reduce) below) — it threads accumulated state across stages rather than describing relationships within one pool.

candidates.heading: Parallel agents: agentPool
Next 5 minutes
candidates.text:
## Next 5 minutes

<CardGroup cols={2}>
  <Card title="Run it on a cron" icon="clock">
    Wrap the `main()` in a node-cron entry. The harness is stateless per-run; each invocation starts fresh.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Add a follow-up across days" icon="arrows-rotate" href="/build-a-harness/sessions">
    Wrap in a `Session` and call `commitTurn(query, answer)` after the pool. Tomorrow's run forks from the trunk and reads yesterday's digest via attention — "give me the new news since yesterday's digest" works without re-prompting.
  </Card>
  <Card title="DAG: compare two topics" icon="diagram-project" href="/examples/compare">
    `examples/compare/` runs `research → compare-axes → synth` as a 6-node DAG. Same `agentPool` call, different `orchestrate` argument.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Recursive delegation" icon="code-branch" href="/build-a-harness/agents-and-pools">
    Add a `DelegateTool` to the `tools` array. Agents can call it to spawn parallel sub-agents that inherit the calling agent's full KV via warm-path forking — useful when one of your stories warrants a deeper dive.
  </Card>
</CardGroup>

candidates.heading: Orchestrators
candidates.text:
## Orchestrators

A pool's `orchestrate` argument selects how its agents relate. The SDK ships five composable shapes plus an escape hatch — pick the simplest one that fits your dependency pattern.

<CardGroup cols={2}>
  <Card title="parallel(specs)" icon="grip-lines-vertical">
    Fan-out. All agents fork from the spine concurrently. Use when sub-tasks are independent.
  </Card>
  <Card title="chain(items, toStep)" icon="link">
    Sequential. Each step extends the spine before the next forks — later agents see earlier findings via KV attention. Use when later tasks build on earlier ones.
  </Card>
  <Card title="fanout(landscape, domains)" icon="diagram-project">
    Landscape pass + N domain agents from the post-landscape root. Use when one shared scouting step should anchor several specialized investigations.
  </Card>
  <Card title="dag(nodes)" icon="circle-nodes">
    Arbitrary acyclic graph with multi-parent dependencies. Lazy spawn on dependency resolution; independent nodes run in parallel. Use when the dependency shape isn't a chain or pure fan-out.
  </Card>
  <Card title="dagWithEvents(nodes, emit)" icon="bell">
    `dag` with a progress-event hook for TUI / telemetry. Same semantics, plus emit-on-spawn / emit-on-complete callbacks.
  </Card>
  <Card title="(custom)" icon="code">
    `Orchestrator = (ctx: PoolContext) => Operation<void>`. The built-ins are 10-30 line implementations of this. Write your own when none of the above fits.
  </Card>
</CardGroup>

A worked DAG example (compare two sources, write up the diff) lives in [`examples/compare`](/examples/compare) and is forked / reshaped step-by-step in the [custom-pipeline guide](/guides/custom-pipeline).

candidates.heading: v0.1
candidates.text:
## v0.1

- **`Source` decoupled from orchestration.** Sources now expose data-access tools and chunks (`tools`, `bind`, `getChunks`); the harness drives orchestration via `agentPool()`. A source no longer owns prompts, recursion shape, or policy.
- **`Agent` stabilized as first-class API.** Internal `AgentState` shape is no longer exported; introspect via the `Agent` class (`status`, `result`, `branch`, `traceBuffer`, `currentTool`).
- **`useAgent` / `useAgentPool` introduced** as the dual resource layers; `agent()` is a convenience alias for `useAgent()` with N=1.

candidates.heading: Offering HDK-equivalent services
candidates.text:
### Offering HDK-equivalent services

- A hosted orchestration service that exposes HDK-compatible APIs as a
  substitute for the HDK runtime
- A managed-inference product that wraps HDK and resells it as your own
  inference platform

candidates.heading: Scope
candidates.text:
### Scope

Recovery is per-pool. In chain/parallel/DAG pipelines, each pool runs its own recovery independently. For chain orchestration, recovered findings land on `agent.result` and the chain orchestrator's `extendSpine` call carries them to the spine — subsequent tasks inherit prior recovered findings via KV, same as voluntary returns.

candidates.heading: 4. SETTLE deferral (tool result gating)
ToS revisions
candidates.text:
## ToS revisions

This document is versioned (`v1`, `v2`, …). Each version is identified
by the `tosVersion` field in your publisher record. When Lloyal Labs
revises the ToS in a way that materially changes publisher obligations,
the version increments and your existing registration's `tosVersion`
becomes stale. The publish CLI will refuse to submit until you
re-attest by running `harness.dev publishers register` again.

Editorial or clarifying changes that don't change publisher obligations
keep the same `tosVersion`.

candidates.heading:
title: Pipelines
description: Composing generator stages into multi-step workflows.
candidates.text:
title: Pipelines
description: Composing generator stages into multi-step workflows.
---


A pipeline is a sequence of generator stages. Each stage `yield*`s into a framework primitive — `agent`, `agentPool`, `diverge`. The output of one stage feeds the next.

Composition is code-first: stages are TypeScript generators with Effection scopes, wired together with `yield*`. There is no separate pipeline DSL or visual builder. Within a single `agentPool` call, structured **orchestrators** — `parallel`, `chain`, `fanout`, `dag`, `dagWithEvents`, `reduce` — describe how that pool's agents relate to one another (fan-out, sequential, multi-parent dependency graphs). They're optional building blocks; the contract is one function — `Orchestrator = (ctx: PoolContext) => Operation<void>` — and the built-ins are 10–30 line implementations of it.

candidates.heading: See also
candidates.text:
## See also

- [Mixed-role pools](/under-the-hood/mixed-role-pools) — the shared-catalog convention for pools with multiple roles
- [Continuous-context spine](/under-the-hood/continuous-context-spine) — how `extendSpine` and prefix sharing compose
- [Concurrency](/under-the-hood/concurrency) — the five-phase tick loop and DAG orchestration
- [Sources & retrieval](/build-an-app/sources-and-retrieval) — the `Source<TCtx, TChunk>` contract
- [Bridges](/build-a-harness/bridges) — multi-source pipelines
- [Compare](/examples/compare) — a worked DAG harness using these patterns

candidates.heading: How cloud APIs and orchestration frameworks handle tools
candidates.text:
### How cloud APIs and orchestration frameworks handle tools

To understand the value of prefix sharing, it helps to see what happens without it.

**Cloud APIs rebuild per request.** Both the Anthropic Messages API and OpenAI Chat Completions API require the full `tools` array on every request. Each API call re-processes all tool schemas as input tokens. The Anthropic API charges input tokens for the tool definitions plus a fixed system prompt overhead (~346 tokens for Claude Opus/Sonnet). In a 6-turn tool-using conversation with 5 tools, the tool schemas are re-processed 6 times.

Anthropic's prompt caching mitigates this: marking the last tool with `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }` caches the tool definitions server-side. Subsequent requests with identical tools get cache hits at 10% of the input token price. However, prompt caching is a billing optimization — the tokens still count toward context length, and the model still attends to them on every turn. The KV computation may be accelerated server-side, but the client has no control over or visibility into this.

**MCP adds a discovery layer, not a caching layer.** MCP (Model Context Protocol) provides a `tools/list` RPC for discovering available tools from servers. The client receives tool schemas, then includes them in LLM API requests — which are standard API calls with the full tool array. MCP itself does not cache tool schemas in the model's context or share them across calls. The caching (if any) depends on the underlying API provider's prompt caching support.

**Orchestration frameworks rebuild the prompt per step.** Frameworks like LangChain's AgentExecutor and LangGraph construct the full prompt (system + tools + conversation history) on every agent step, then send it as a single API call. Each step re-serializes and re-sends the complete tool list. There is no cross-step KV sharing — each API call is independent.

candidates.heading: Conditional stages
1. Prefill gate (ctx.spawn)
Reasoning Modes
candidates.text:
## Reasoning Modes

For the Research stage specifically, two orchestration shapes are common:

- **Sequential (chain)** — each task forks `querySpine` at its current extended position; on report, `extendSpine` writes the curated `[user, asst]` turn onto `querySpine`; the next task sees prior findings via KV attention. Worker prompts can adapt on `taskIndex > 0` to target gaps and named entities from earlier findings rather than re-investigating. Synth runs as a post-pool `useAgent` that forks the fully-extended `querySpine`.

- **Parallel (flat)** — all tasks fork `querySpine` simultaneously; no spine extension; each agent runs independently with sibling-task awareness in its prompt. Findings are concatenated into the synth's user message as text. Synth runs as a `useAgent` but consumes findings via text injection, not via attention.

Pick sequential when later tasks should genuinely build on earlier ones. Pick parallel when sub-questions are independent and time-budget headroom is plentiful.

candidates.heading: How they nest
candidates.text:
## How they nest

```
Harness                                       ← what you ship
 │
 ├─ Registry ─→ App ─→ Source · Tools · skill.eta
 │              App ─→ Source · Tools · skill.eta
 │              …
 │
 ├─ Session ─→ trunk (KV that survives across queries)
 │
 └─ Pool ─→ Spine (catalog + intro + tool schemas, decoded once)
            ├─ Agent (a branch with intent)
            ├─ Agent
            └─ Agent
```

Read top-down: a **Harness** runs Apps (via the Registry), holds a Session, and spawns Pools. Each Pool runs Agents on a Spine. Apps contribute Sources + Tools + a skill into the runtime. The Session's trunk is the KV that survives across turns.

<Note>
Two of the seven concepts cover *who builds what*. A **Harness** is what you ship to users — orchestration, UI, persistence. An **App** is a packaged capability a harness loads. Same person can wear both hats on day one; the protocol scales when third parties start shipping Apps for harnesses they didn't write.
</Note>

---

candidates.heading: What is actually restricted?
candidates.text:
## What is actually restricted?

The restriction is narrow and specific: **don't become the replacement
platform vendor**. Concretely:

- **AWS / Google / Microsoft launching "Bedrock Managed HDK" or "Vertex HDK"
  as a hosted runtime service** — restricted ❌
- **A competitor publishing "OpenHDK" as a forked harness runtime under a
  different name** — restricted ❌
- **A clean-room reimplementation of the HDK runtime in Python / Rust / Go
  intended as a drop-in replacement** — restricted ❌ (Competing Use doesn't
  require forking source code — a reimplementation that competes is the
  same problem)
- **Launching `apps.competitor.com` as an alternative HDK App distribution
  channel** — restricted ❌
- **Offering "managed HDK hosting" or "HDK-as-a-Service" as a competing
  SaaS** — restricted ❌
- **A hosted orchestration service exposing HDK-compatible APIs as a
  substitute for the HDK runtime** — restricted ❌

Notice the pattern: every restricted scenario is "become the platform
vendor," not "build products with HDK." If your project doesn't compete
directly with the HDK runtime or its distribution channel, FSL doesn't
affect you.

candidates.heading: What the server validates
candidates.text:
## What the server validates

The Worker performs these checks before quarantining a submission. Failures return `400` with a structured error code:

| Error code | Cause |
|---|---|
| `no-publisher-account` | Your identity has no publisher record. Run [`publishers register`](/cli/publishers) first. |
| `account-suspended` | Your publisher account is suspended. Email `support@lloyal.ai`. |
| `tos-version-stale` | Your `tosVersion` is older than the current ToS. Re-attest via `publishers register --yes`. |
| `rate-limit-exceeded` | You've hit the per-identity rate limit (currently 10 publishes / 24h). Response includes `Retry-After`. |
| `invalid-name` | The catalog name doesn't match `<publisher-handle>/<app-name>`. |
| `name-handle-mismatch` | The handle prefix in `app.json.name` doesn't match your publisher record. |
| `invalid-version` | `package.json.version` is not a valid semver. |
| `size-mismatch` | The tarball's actual byte size doesn't match the manifest stub's claim (very rare; usually a packaging bug). |
| `importName-required` | `package.json.name` is missing. |
| `importName-mismatch` | The `package.json.name` inside the tarball doesn't match the manifest stub's `importName` (someone is lying — refuse). |
| `version-package-json-mismatch` | The `package.json.version` inside the tarball doesn't match the manifest stub's `version` (same — refuse). |
| `tarball-inspect-failed` | The Worker couldn't extract `package/package.json` from the tarball (invalid gzip, not a valid npm-pack output, etc.). |

candidates.heading: onSettleReject — stall-break fallback
candidates.text:
## `onSettleReject` — stall-break fallback

Called by the **SETTLE stall-breaker** when deferred tool results have no active siblings to free KV (everyone is `awaiting_tool` or idle). The policy decides per deferred item: nudge the agent with a compact error payload (replacing the oversized result), or drop the agent and let recovery extract.

```typescript
type SettleAction =
  | { type: 'nudge'; message: string }
  | { type: 'idle'; reason: IdleReason };
```

`DefaultAgentPolicy.onSettleReject` returns `{type: 'nudge'}` when `terminalToolName` is configured and the agent has made at least one tool call. The nudge message includes the token budget (same format as PRODUCE-phase nudges):

```
Tool result too large for remaining KV. Report your findings now within 1100 words.
```

The budget is `pressure.remaining - pressure.hardLimit` — tokens available before the kill threshold fires. If the nudge payload itself doesn't fit in the current headroom, the pool drops the agent with `pool:agentDrop reason=pressure_settle_reject` and recovery takes over. If the hook is absent (no `onSettleReject` implemented), the stall-breaker falls back to the legacy path: `pool:agentDrop reason=settle_stall_break`.

In normal (non-stalled) operation, SETTLE just defers oversized items without consulting the policy — siblings freeing KV (parallel orchestration) or the spine growing (chain) restores headroom on subsequent ticks. `onSettleReject` fires only at the last-resort moment when no alternatives remain.

candidates.heading: The stack
candidates.text:
## The stack

```
Your application
    ↓
Apps (defineApp + manifest)         ← what third parties build (corpus, web, custom)
    ↓
@lloyal-labs/rig                     Registry, retrieval primitives, harness building blocks
    ↓
@lloyal-labs/lloyal-agents           Continuous Context agent orchestration, authGuard
    ↓
@lloyal-labs/sdk                     Branches, sessions, KV cache, reranker
    ↓
@lloyal-labs/lloyal.node             Node-native binding (JS ↔ C++)
    ↓
liblloyal                            C++ core — KV machinery, sampler, GPU integration
                                     ─ goes wherever C++ runs ─
```

Four npm packages over a portable C++ core, with **Apps** as the new top-of-stack capability layer. One install. The whole stack ships with your app.

- **[Apps](/build-an-app/what-is-an-app)** — Source + Tools + skill + manifest, wired with `defineApp`. The unit a third party authors and a harness loads.
- **[Tools](/build-an-app/tools)** — anything an agent can call: databases, APIs, filesystems, web search, services. You define the interface; the model dispatches uniformly. `protected: true` puts a tool behind `authGuard`.
- **[Agent Pools](/build-a-harness/agents-and-pools)** — parallel agents on shared KV. Orchestrators (`parallel` / `chain` / `fanout` / `dag`) reshape the harness without changing the call.
- **[Sources](/build-an-app/sources-and-retrieval)** — any data backend: web, corpus, vector store, database. Wrapped in Apps; full RIG stack inherited.

candidates.heading: Built to embed
candidates.text:
### Built to embed

Single-process isn't a developer-convenience claim — it's a **shipping** claim, and it has two layers.

The HDK itself is a Node program. It goes wherever a Node runtime goes:

- **Inside Electron** — desktop apps with Node embedded. The harness ships as part of the app bundle; end users install one binary.
- **Inside a CLI** — `npm install -g your-cli`. Users get the agent layer with the rest of the install. No external services to stand up.
- **Inside an existing Node service** — drop a worker into a backend you already run. No separate inference deployment, no extra HTTP hop in the request path.
- **On edge / serverless runtimes** — anywhere a Node runtime executes. The whole agent loop runs inline.

Underneath, the inference runtime, KV machinery, sampler, and GPU integration are powered by **[liblloyal](https://lloyal-ai.github.io/liblloyal/)** — the C++ core. The Node binding (`@lloyal-labs/lloyal.node`) is one front-end on top of liblloyal; the core itself runs anywhere a C++ toolchain plus a compute backend exist:

- **Desktop OSes** — macOS (Apple Silicon + Intel, Metal), Linux (x86_64 + ARM via CUDA / Vulkan / ROCm / CPU), Windows (CUDA / Vulkan / CPU). Already shipped via the Node binding.
- **Mobile** — iOS (Swift / Obj-C ↔ C++ interop, Metal acceleration), Android (NDK, Vulkan / OpenCL acceleration). Consumer phones have the RAM and GPU for 1–4B-class models.
- **Browser via WebAssembly** — Emscripten compiles liblloyal to WASM; WebGPU exposes accelerator access. Constrained by per-tab memory and the WebGPU rollout.
- **Game engines** — Unreal (native C++), Unity (native plugin), Godot (GDExtension), proprietary engines. Engine ↔ liblloyal becomes a function call.
- **Other language ecosystems via FFI** — Python (`pybind11`), Rust (FFI), Go (`cgo`), Swift (native interop), Kotlin/JNI, .NET (P/Invoke).
- **Native server processes** — pure C++ services, daemons, edge containers without a JavaScript runtime in the picture.

Today, only the Node binding ships as part of the HDK. Other bindings have the same shape as `lloyal.node` adapted for the host language; some are in the pipeline, others are community territory. The architecture isn't bounded by Node — today's HDK distribution surface is.

The typical multi-process stack can't go any of those places without dragging its inference server, vector DB, and orchestration along. At that point you're shipping a distributed system, not a feature.

candidates.heading:
title: Agents
description: Parallel agents on shared GPU — the Agent class, agent pool, policy, and spine.
candidates.text:
title: Agents
description: Parallel agents on shared GPU — the Agent class, agent pool, policy, and spine.
---


Three agents. One GPU. Shared prefix.

```
Spine (system prompt + tool schemas): decoded once
  ├── Agent 0: fork → investigate question A
  ├── Agent 1: fork → investigate question B
  └── Agent 2: fork → investigate question C
```

Every agent inherits the root's KV cache at zero cost — an O(1) metadata tag, not a copy. Each agent then generates independently, calls tools, and reports findings. The pool manages the entire lifecycle.

candidates.heading: What is an Agent?
candidates.text:
## What is an Agent?

An agent is a **branch with intent**. A Branch is a forkable KV cache sequence — it stores every token the model has seen and generated. An Agent adds: a task to accomplish, a policy for how to accomplish it, and a record of what it has done.

The branch is the ground truth. The agent is the interpretation layer that gives meaning to what's in the branch and makes decisions about what to do next.

```typescript
import { Agent } from "@lloyal-labs/lloyal-agents";

// agent and agentPool return Agents.
// After completion, each agent exposes:
result.result          // what the agent discovered (or null)
result.rawOutput       // raw model output from the final turn
result.toolCallCount   // how many tools it called
result.tokenCount      // how many tokens it generated
result.nestedResults   // results from recursive sub-agents
result.ppl             // model-level perplexity at completion
```

candidates.heading: Status model
candidates.text:
### Status model

Every agent transitions through a lifecycle:

```
idle → active → awaiting_tool → active → ... → idle → disposed
```

- **idle** — created but not yet generating, OR finished but branch still alive (extraction window)
- **active** — generating tokens between stop boundaries
- **awaiting_tool** — tool call parsed, waiting for result in SETTLE
- **disposed** — branch pruned, agent no longer usable

The pool enforces valid transitions. An agent cannot go backward or skip states.

candidates.heading: Findings
candidates.text:
### Findings

Agents produce results through a single write path with provenance tracking:

- `voluntary_return` — agent voluntarily called the terminal tool (e.g. `report()`)
- `free_text` — agent emitted prose without a tool call
- `recovery` — extracted post-idle via the recovery path (`recoverInline`)
- `nudge` — agent returned after a nudge injection
- `tool_error` — tool threw, error captured as result

candidates.heading: Tool history
candidates.text:
### Tool history

Each agent records metadata for every tool invocation — tool name, arguments, result token count, context available after settlement. The [Agent Policy](#agent-policy) reads this history for informed lifecycle decisions.

candidates.heading: Create a harness
candidates.text:
## Create a harness

```bash
npx harness.dev <name> [--dir <path>]
```

Generates a new harness in a directory named `<name>` in your current working directory (or in `<path>` if `--dir` is given). `<name>` must match `[a-z][a-z0-9_-]{1,63}`.

```bash
npx harness.dev my-research-harness
cd my-research-harness
npm install
# edit harness.json — point model.path at a local .gguf
npm run dev "What was the Cuban missile crisis?"
```

The generated harness ships with the `lloyal/wikipedia` reference app preinstalled (via signed-channel canonical URL) so it answers a research question on first run with no extra setup beyond a local model file.

The generated harness includes:

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `package.json` | Standard npm metadata + scripts (`dev`, `build`, `start`). Depends on `@lloyal-labs/lloyal-agents`, `@lloyal-labs/sdk`, `@lloyal-labs/rig`, `@lloyal-labs/lloyal.node`, `effection`, and `@lloyal-labs/wikipedia-app` (pinned to the signed-channel canonical tarball URL). |
| `harness.json` | Per-harness config — model path and context size. |
| `bin/run.js` | Node shim that requires `dist/main.js`. |
| `src/main.ts` | Boot — parses argv, loads the model, builds the `AppRegistry`, enables the Wikipedia app, runs the query. |
| `src/harness.ts` | The orchestrator — `runQuery` plans the query into tasks via `PlanTool`, fans them out as parallel agents via `agentPool` + `parallel`, then reduces the reports into an answer via a synth `useAgent` call. The harness IS the orchestrator. |
| `prompts/plan.eta` | Planner prompt (Eta-templated). |
| `prompts/synth.eta` | Synthesizer prompt. |
| `LICENSE` | Apache 2.0. |
| `tsconfig.json` | Standard TS config (CommonJS, ES2022). |

The harness is yours — extend it freely. Add the corpus reference app with `npx harness.dev install lloyal/corpus`, or scaffold your own app with `npx harness.dev app`. When you're ready to ship a derivative harness, the same Apache 2.0 license applies.

candidates.heading: onRecovery — recovery extraction
candidates.text:
## `onRecovery` — recovery extraction

Called inline when the trailing stop kills an agent during PRODUCE (or at pool close for agents that ended idle without result). The policy decides whether to extract findings, and renders its extraction prompt with the live pressure snapshot so the model knows its budget.

```typescript
type RecoveryAction =
  | { type: 'extract'; prompt: { system: string; user: string } }
  | { type: 'skip' };

onRecovery?(agent: Agent, pressure: ContextPressure): RecoveryAction;
```

`DefaultAgentPolicy` checks the confabulation guard before returning `extract`:
- `agent.tokenCount >= minTokens` (default 100) — agent generated enough context
- `agent.toolCallCount >= minToolCalls` (default 2) — agent did real work

Agents that barely ran would produce fabricated findings — the guard prevents this.

**Budget-aware prompt rendering.** The policy computes `budget = max(50, pressure.remaining - prefillOverhead - batchBuffer)` and renders the prompt's eta template with that value. Prompt authors use `<%= it.budget %>` where they want the budget surfaced:

```eta
You are a research reporter. The agent has been terminated due to budget constraints.

Call the report tool with the most important findings. Prioritize:
1. Direct quotes and specific evidence
2. Key conclusions with source URLs
3. Supporting context (only if budget allows)

IMPORTANT: You have approximately <%= it.budget %> words for your report. Be concise.
---
Report your findings.
```

Prompts with no eta tags render unchanged (backward compatible). Policies that author prompts via `DefaultAgentPolicyOpts.recovery.prompt` can use the full eta feature set (conditionals, loops) for richer templating.

The pool owns the extraction grammar (`{ "result": "..." }` JSON schema). The policy owns the prompt and the decision.

**Recovery diagnostics are always visible.** After every non-skip recovery attempt, the pool emits one of:

- `pool:recoveryReturn` — JSON parse succeeded, findings captured.
- `pool:recoveryFailed` — parse failed; event carries `reason` (`parse_error`, `no_result_field`, `scope_error`) + `outputExcerpt` (first 200 chars of what was generated) so ops can diagnose without re-running.

No silent recovery failures — every recovery prefill has a matching diagnostic.

<Tip>
Recovery runs inline on each pressure-kill using the `hardLimit` reserve as a parallel report budget. `pruneOnReturn: true` (default) maximizes sibling headroom after each recovered or voluntary return.
</Tip>

candidates.heading: Model selection
candidates.text:
## Model selection

lloyal-agents requires an instruction-tuned model with native tool-calling support. The runtime uses `formatChat()` to embed tool schemas into the system prompt and `parseChatOutput()` to extract structured tool calls from generation output. Models without tool support will fail at agent setup with:

```
Error: Model does not support tool calling. Please use a model with native tool support
```

Confirmed compatible model families:

| Family | Notes |
|--------|-------|
| Qwen 3 | Recommended. Strong tool-call compliance, good depth-first behavior |
| Llama 3.x | Works well with instruction-tuned variants |
| Mistral | Tool-calling variants supported |

The reference pipeline uses `Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507` quantized to Q4_K_M. Larger models improve reasoning depth (more hypothesis-driven tool calls, better synthesis) but require proportionally more KV memory.

candidates.heading: Per-role system prompts
candidates.text:
## Per-role system prompts

Each `DAGNode`'s `task.systemPrompt` is the role assignment for that agent. In compare, per-role prompts live in `prompts/research-web.eta`, `compare.eta`, `synthesize.eta` etc., and each opens by stating which subset of tools is in scope for that role. The agent then forks from a shared spine that holds the full tool catalog, but only uses what its role authorizes.

When you adopt the App protocol, this convention moves up a level: each App ships its own `skill.eta` describing how an agent should use that App's tools, and the per-role prompt collapses into "use App `X`". See [What is an App](/build-an-app/what-is-an-app#skill) for the protocol.

candidates.heading: Internal enterprise use
candidates.text:
### Internal enterprise use

- A bank runs HDK internally to power their intelligent search and document
  understanding for compliance use
- A government agency deploys HDK on-premise for sovereign AI use cases
- A research org uses HDK across hundreds of internal applications

candidates.heading: createAppRegistry
Is this OSI-approved open source?
candidates.text:
## Is this OSI-approved open source?

**No, and we want to be honest about that.** FSL is not OSI-approved
because the OSI definition of open source (clause 6, "No Discrimination
Against Fields of Endeavor") does not permit restrictions on specific
use cases. FSL restricts Competing Use. That restriction takes it out of
strict OSI compliance.

FSL falls under the [Fair Source](https://fair.io) classification —
source-available licenses that are explicitly developer-friendly:
commercial use permitted, free redistribution, eventual open-source
conversion. Fair Source is a more developer-friendly framing than the
generic "source-available" label, which has been tainted by the
SSPL / Elastic / MongoDB relicensing trauma cycles.

What this means practically:

- **You can read the source.** ✅
- **You can modify it.** ✅
- **You can sell products built with it.** ✅
- **You can redistribute it (with the same FSL terms).** ✅
- **It will be Apache 2.0 in two years.** ✅
- Some enterprise procurement policies that strictly require OSI-approved
  licenses will require an exception for this. We're working on making
  that exception easy to grant.

candidates.heading: Writing a custom policy
Recursive Agents
candidates.text:
## Recursive Agents

Tools can spawn sub-agents. A tool's `execute()` method returns an Effection [`Operation<unknown>`][ops], which means it can [`yield*`][ops] into any framework primitive — `agentPool`, `useAgentPool`, `agent`, `diverge`. The inner pipeline shares the same GPU compute, KV cache, and event channel as the outer pool. No separate process. No serialization boundary.

candidates.heading:
title: Pipelines
description: Composing generator stages into multi-step workflows.
candidates.text:
title: Pipelines
description: Composing generator stages into multi-step workflows.
---


A pipeline is a sequence of generator stages. Each stage `yield*`s into a framework primitive — `agent`, `agentPool`, `diverge`. The output of one stage feeds the next.

Composition is code-first: stages are TypeScript generators with Effection scopes, wired together with `yield*`. There is no separate pipeline DSL or visual builder. Within a single `agentPool` call, structured **orchestrators** — `parallel`, `chain`, `fanout`, `dag`, `dagWithEvents`, `reduce` — describe how that pool's agents relate to one another (fan-out, sequential, multi-parent dependency graphs). They're optional building blocks; the contract is one function — `Orchestrator = (ctx: PoolContext) => Operation<void>` — and the built-ins are 10–30 line implementations of it.

candidates.heading: Why
candidates.text:
### Why

The internal vocabulary echoed the model's tool-call mental model when it should have described what was happening. "Report" carries a domain assumption (research findings); "return" describes the *semantic* (function exit with value) and works for any agent regardless of what it produces. The split between voluntary and recovery makes the framework honest about *how* a value was obtained — both paths produce `agent.result` but only one is the model's own work.

candidates.heading:
title: "Rerank Architecture"
description: "How the cross-encoder reranker composes Branch + BranchStore primitives over a warm trunk, and where BM25 first-stage fits."
candidates.text:
title: "Rerank Architecture"
description: "How the cross-encoder reranker composes Branch + BranchStore primitives over a warm trunk, and where BM25 first-stage fits."
---

Rerank is the cross-encoder that admits task-relevant context into every agent's KV cache. It's HDK's most load-bearing capability: every search tool result, every corpus probe, every coverage check goes through it. The architecture below is the post-3.0 shape — composed in the SDK over the same `Branch` + `BranchStore` primitives that power `useAgent`, `withSpine`, and `agentPool`. The framework's "agents are branches of a live KV cache" framing extends literally to rerank.

candidates.heading: Behavior change: voluntary vs recovery split
candidates.text:
### Behavior change: voluntary vs recovery split

Previously `agent:report` fired for BOTH voluntary completion (via `handleReport`) AND recovery extraction (via `recoverInline`). Consumers couldn't tell apart "agent voluntarily produced this value" from "framework salvaged a value from a killed agent."

After the rename:

- **`agent:return`** — fires only when the model voluntarily called the terminal tool and `handleReturn` ran. `ResultSource` = `'voluntary_return'`.
- **`agent:recovered`** — fires only when `recoverInline` extracted findings via grammar-constrained generation after the agent was killed. `ResultSource` = `'recovery'`.
- Both still populate `agent.result` and fire `agent:done` for lifecycle.

**Consumer migration:** code that previously listened for `agent:report` to capture any agent's value should subscribe to BOTH `agent:return` and `agent:recovered` (or to `agent:done` and read `agent.result` directly). The lloyal-sdk examples and reasoning.run use case-fall-through (`case 'agent:return': case 'agent:recovered': { ... }`) to preserve the prior union semantics.

candidates.heading: Event Channel
candidates.text:
### Event Channel

Inner agent events flow through the same `Events` channel. The TUI or event collector sees `agent:spawn`, `agent:produce`, `agent:tool_call`, `agent:tool_result`, `agent:done` from both outer and inner agents. The `parentAgentId` field on `agent:spawn` distinguishes inner from outer — inner agents have a `parentAgentId` that matches an inner pool's root branch handle, not the outer spine.

candidates.heading: Events
dagWithEvents — orchestrator with TUI hooks
Problem: Agents not using tools
candidates.text:
## Problem: Agents not using tools

**Symptom:** Agents generate text but never call any tools. They produce raw text output and are marked done without tool calls.

**Diagnosis steps:**

1. Check `prompt:format` events with `role: 'agentSuffix'`. The `tools` field must contain the JSON-serialized tool schemas. If `tools` is missing or empty, the model has no awareness of available tools.

```jsonl
{"type":"prompt:format","role":"agentSuffix","tools":"[{\"type\":\"function\",...}]",...}
```

2. Check `agent:turn` events. If `parsedToolCalls` is an empty array, the model generated text but no tool-call syntax:

```jsonl
{"type":"agent:turn","agentId":5,"parsedContent":"The answer is...","parsedToolCalls":[]}
```

3. Verify the model supports tool calling. If the model's chat template does not handle tools, `formatChatSync` will produce `CHAT_FORMAT_CONTENT_ONLY` or `CHAT_FORMAT_GENERIC`, and agent setup will throw. If you catch/suppress this error, agents will run without tool awareness.

**Common causes:**
- Missing `tools` on the pool: `agentPool({ orchestrate, tools: [], ... })` — empty array means no tools available to the agent
- Wrong model: base model instead of instruction-tuned, or instruction-tuned without tool-call training
- Empty tools array: `tools: []` means no tools available to the agent

candidates.heading: Read a trace
candidates.text:
## Read a trace

Trace files are newline-delimited JSON. Use Python one-liners:

<Tabs>
  <Tab title="Agent trajectory">
```bash
cat trace.jsonl | python3 -c "
import json, sys
for e in (json.loads(l) for l in sys.stdin):
    if e['type'] == 'tool:dispatch' and e['agentId'] == 3:
        print(f\"{e['tool']}({json.dumps(e['args'])[:80]}) [{e['toolIndex']}/{e['toolkitSize']}]\")
"
```
  </Tab>
  <Tab title="KV pressure timeline">
```bash
cat trace.jsonl | python3 -c "
import json, sys
for e in (json.loads(l) for l in sys.stdin):
    if e['type'] == 'pool:open':
        print(f\"OPEN agents={e['agentCount']} headroom={e['pressure']['headroom']}\")
    if e['type'] == 'pool:agentDrop':
        print(f\"DROP agent={e['agentId']} reason={e['reason']}\")
"
```
  </Tab>
  <Tab title="Prefill budget">
```bash
cat trace.jsonl | python3 -c "
import json, sys
for e in (json.loads(l) for l in sys.stdin):
    if e['type'] == 'tool:result':
        print(f\"agent={e['agentId']} {e['tool']:15s} prefill={e['prefillTokenCount']:>5d}\")
"
```
  </Tab>
</Tabs>

candidates.heading: Shared mode at a glance
candidates.text:
## Shared mode at a glance

The framework knob that makes mixed-role pools work is called **shared mode**. It's a binary the wrapper toggles based on whether `agentPool` (or `withSpine`) was given a `systemPrompt`:

| Mode | Activation | Spine header KV | Per-agent suffix | `extendSpine` writes to |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Shared** | `systemPrompt` set | `[system + tool schemas]` prefilled once at the inner spine | `[role-assign system, user-task]` — no tools | inner spine (pruned at `withSpine` exit) |
| **Non-shared** | `systemPrompt` omitted | turn separator only | `[system + tools + user]` — full prefix per spawn | warm parent (persists past pool exit) |

**Use shared mode when** the pool has mixed roles drawing from one tool set (a DAG with research + compare + synth nodes), or a long chain re-emits the same tools per step. **Avoid it when** the pool's findings need to be visible to a later, separate `useAgent` that forks the same parent — the extensions would be pruned with the inner spine. See [Continuous-context spine](/under-the-hood/continuous-context-spine) for the spine-persistence consequences and [Concurrency](/under-the-hood/concurrency#spinefmt--shared-mode) for the `SpineFmt` mechanism.

candidates.heading:
title: "Build a Custom Tool"
description: "Create tools for agents — extend Tool<TArgs>, handle async work, build recursive tools, and pass to agentPool()."
candidates.text:
title: "Build a Custom Tool"
description: "Create tools for agents — extend Tool<TArgs>, handle async work, build recursive tools, and pass to agentPool()."
---

This guide walks through creating tools for agents to use during generation. Tools extend the `Tool<TArgs>` base class and are passed directly to `agent()` or `agentPool()`.

candidates.heading: Spine compatibility
candidates.text:
## Spine compatibility

Putting the catalog on the harness's outer `withSpine` (rather than on `agentPool` itself) is load-bearing for any harness whose synth runs *post-pool* via a separate `useAgent`. `agentPool({ systemPrompt })` would create a *nested* `withSpine` whose root is pruned at pool exit — chain extensions land on that transient inner spine, and a post-pool `useAgent({ parent: querySpine })` would fork an empty branch.

When the catalog lives on the harness's outer `withSpine`, `agentPool` runs in non-shared mode internally; `extendSpine` writes onto the outer querySpine; synth's `useAgent` forks the same root and sees the chain extensions via attention. See [Continuous-context spine](/under-the-hood/continuous-context-spine) for the spine mechanics.

For pools whose synth runs as a *node inside the same `agentPool`* (a DAG with synth as the terminal node), this distinction doesn't matter — synth's branch forks from the same spineRoot the chain extensions land on.

candidates.heading: Renames
candidates.text:
### Renames

- **`agent:report` event → `agent:return` event.** Fires only for voluntary completion (the model called the terminal tool). See behavior split below.
- **NEW event `agent:recovered`** (no predecessor — split out from the prior conflated `agent:report`). Fires when recovery extraction salvaged findings from a killed agent.
- **`{ type: 'report' }` policy action → `{ type: 'return' }`.**
- **`{ type: 'free_text_report' }` policy action → `{ type: 'free_text_return' }`.**
- **`handleReport(...)` → `handleReturn(...)`** + **`handleFreeTextReport(...)` → `handleFreeTextReturn(...)`** (framework-internal handlers).
- **`pruneOnReport: boolean` option → `pruneOnReturn: boolean`** (on `AgentPoolOptions`, `CreateAgentPoolOpts`, `useAgent`).
- **`'report_tool'` ResultSource literal → `'voluntary_return'`.**
- **`agent.reportResult(value, source)` setter → `agent.setResult(value, source)`.** "Return" describes the act; "result" stays as the noun for the data.
- **`pool:recoveryReport` trace event → `pool:recoveryReturn`.**
- **`terminalTool: string` option field → `terminalToolName: string`.** It's a name pointer into the toolkit (matched against `parsedToolCall.name`), not a Tool instance. The rename makes the field's semantic explicit.
- **`PolicyConfig.terminalTool` and `DefaultAgentPolicyOpts.terminalTool` → `terminalToolName`.**
- **`DefaultAgentPolicyOpts.minToolCallsBeforeReport` → `minToolCallsBeforeReturn`.**
- **`IdleReason: 'reported'` → `'returned'`.**

candidates.heading: What about the lloyal stack — what's under FSL and what's not?
candidates.text:
## What about the lloyal stack — what's under FSL and what's not?

| Component | License | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `liblloyal` (C++ engine) | FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 | Native primitives the runtime is built on |
| `lloyal-node` (N-API binding) | FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 | The binding that lets Effection drive llama.cpp |
| `@lloyal-labs/lloyal-agents` | FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 | Runtime framework |
| `@lloyal-labs/lloyal-sdk` | FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 | Runtime framework |
| `@lloyal-labs/rig` | FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 | Runtime framework — holds the App protocol |
| `@lloyal-labs/corpus`, `@lloyal-labs/web` | FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 | Reference Apps shipped in-tree |
| **`@lloyal-labs/harness-cli` (the `harness.dev` CLI)** | **Apache 2.0** | Scaffolder — unrestricted for scaffolding new harnesses and Apps |
| **`hdk-create-app`** (when shipped) | **Apache 2.0** | Scaffolder — same as above |
| `llama.cpp` (vendored dependency) | MIT (unchanged) | External upstream library; we don't relicense their code |

candidates.heading: v0.4 (2026-05-16)
candidates.text:
## v0.4 (2026-05-16)

Agent termination vocabulary renamed from "report" to "return" — framework-internal only. **The model-facing protocol is unchanged**: the model still emits standard `<tool_call>{"name": "report", "arguments": {"result": "..."}}</tool_call>` syntax via the same chat-parser pipeline. The rename touches the framework's *internal* names for what was happening: an agent emitting a terminal value is a *return*, not a *report*. The new vocabulary is domain-neutral (works for code-generation, planning, classification agents — not just research) and matches the function-call execution model the runtime already follows.

Ships in the same npm release as v0.3 (no version bump between the two; both renames consolidate into `@lloyal-labs/lloyal-agents@2.1.0`, `@lloyal-labs/sdk@2.1.0`, `@lloyal-labs/rig@2.1.0`).

candidates.heading: What stayed the same
candidates.text:
### What stayed the same

- **`reportTool` exported from `@lloyal-labs/rig`** — research-domain helper. The harness imports it and assigns `terminalToolName: 'report'`. The string `'report'` as a tool name is the harness's choice; other domains can choose `'submit_code'`, `'plan'`, etc.
- **The model-facing protocol.** The model still sees a tool named whatever the harness chooses (typically `'report'`), still emits standard `<tool_call>...</tool_call>` syntax via llama.cpp's native chat parser. Nothing about generation behavior changes.
- **`agent.result`, `agent.resultSource`** field names — the noun stays; only the act renames.
- **`agent:tool_call` event** — still fires for terminal-tool calls (the model emitted a parsed tool call from its POV; the interception happens at the policy layer, not the event layer).
- **`agent:done`** — lifecycle event, always fires.
- **`'free_text'`, `'recovery'`, `'tool_error'`, `'nudge'`** ResultSource literals — note: `'scratchpad'` was renamed to `'recovery'` in v0.5 alongside the dead-surface cleanup.

candidates.heading: Generators instead of async/await
useAgent and useAgentPool
agent cleanup
Problem: Early termination (agents report too soon)
candidates.text:
## Problem: Early termination (agents report too soon)

**Symptom:** Agents call 0-1 non-terminal tools, then immediately call `report()` with shallow findings.

**Diagnosis steps:**

1. Check `tool:dispatch` events for each agent. Look at the `toolIndex` and `toolkitSize` fields:

```jsonl
{"type":"tool:dispatch","agentId":5,"tool":"report","toolIndex":4,"toolkitSize":5,...}
```

If `toolIndex` equals `toolkitSize - 1` (the tool is last in the array), check whether it is the terminal tool. Terminal tools at the end of the toolkit array trigger LLM recency bias.

2. Inspect the `pool:open` event's `pressure` field. If `headroom` is very low at pool start, agents may be immediately pressure-cut after their first tool call:

```jsonl
{"type":"pool:open","agentCount":3,"pressure":{"remaining":2048,"softLimit":1024,"headroom":1024}}
```

3. Check `pool:agentDrop` events. If `reason` is `pressure_softcut`, the agent wanted a non-terminal tool but headroom was negative:

```jsonl
{"type":"pool:agentDrop","agentId":5,"reason":"pressure_softcut"}
```

**Common causes:**
- **Tool ordering:** `report` or terminal tool is last in the `tools` array. Fix by placing it before any tool that shares a name prefix. See [Custom Tool](./custom-tool.md) for the ordering rule.
- **Prompt quality:** System prompt does not motivate thorough investigation. Agents will call `report()` early if the prompt says "answer the question" rather than "investigate thoroughly using available tools."
- **Large prior tool results:** Earlier agents consumed most of the KV budget, leaving later agents pressure-constrained from their first turn.

candidates.heading: Trace event reference
candidates.text:
## Trace event reference

Key event types from `packages/agents/src/trace-types.ts`:

| Event | When | Key fields |
|-------|------|------------|
| `prompt:format` | System prompt or agent suffix tokenized | `promptText`, `taskContent`, `tokenCount`, `tools`, `role` |
| `branch:create` | Branch forked or created | `branchHandle`, `parentHandle`, `role` |
| `branch:prefill` | Tokens injected into a branch | `branchHandle`, `tokenCount`, `role` |
| `pool:open` | Agent pool starts | `agentCount`, `taskSuffixTokens`, `pressure` |
| `pool:agentDrop` | Agent killed by pressure or turn limit | `agentId`, `reason` |
| `pool:close` | Agent pool ends | `agents[]` (each with `tokenCount`, `toolCallCount`, `result`) |
| `agent:turn` | Agent completes a generation segment | `rawOutput`, `parsedToolCalls` |
| `tool:dispatch` | Tool call starts | `tool`, `toolIndex`, `toolkitSize`, `args` |
| `tool:result` | Tool call returns | `result`, `prefillTokenCount`, `durationMs` |
| `generate:start` | Single-branch generation begins | `hasGrammar`, `hasParent`, `role` |
| `generate:end` | Single-branch generation ends | `output`, `tokenCount`, `parsed` |

candidates.heading: Problem: Synthesis ignores research findings
candidates.text:
## Problem: Synthesis ignores research findings

**Symptom:** The synthesis output does not reference research findings. It produces generic answers or hallucinates claims not present in the research.

**Diagnosis steps:**

1. Check the `prompt:format` event for the synthesis agent (look for the synthesis system prompt). The `taskContent` field should contain the full research findings:

```jsonl
{"type":"prompt:format","role":"agentSuffix","taskContent":"## corpus research\n\n### Agent 1\n..."}
```

If `taskContent` is empty or contains `(none)`, the research phase produced no findings.

2. Check the synthesis task content template. The harness fills `{{agentFindings}}` and `{{sourcePassages}}`:

```typescript
const content = SYNTHESIZE.user
  .replace('{{agentFindings}}', agentFindings || '(none)')
  .replace('{{sourcePassages}}', sourcePassages || '(none)')
  .replace('{{query}}', query);
```

If all research agents were dropped by pressure, both `agentFindings` and `sourcePassages` will be `(none)`.

3. Check if the synthesis agent has grounding tools. Look at `tool:dispatch` events from the synthesis pool. If the synthesis pool has only the `report` tool, the agent cannot independently verify claims:

```jsonl
{"type":"pool:open","agentCount":1}
{"type":"tool:dispatch","tool":"report","toolIndex":0,"toolkitSize":1}
```

A synthesis agent with grounding tools (search, read_file, grep, query) can make 4-6 verification calls before reporting. One with only `report` must rely entirely on the content in its prompt.

**Solutions:**
- Ensure research agents produced results: check earlier `pool:close` events for non-null `result` in the agents array
- Give synthesis grounding tools when conflicts detected: `tools: [...source.tools, reportTool]`
- Increase reranker passage count for more source material in the synthesis prompt

candidates.heading: Measuring prefix savings
Depth
candidates.text:
## Depth

Nothing in the framework limits recursion to two levels. An inner tool can itself spawn sub-agents that spawn sub-agents. Each level creates its own `withSpine` + `useAgentPool` scope, registers its own `ensure()` callbacks, and participates in the same `BranchStore`. On teardown, Effection halts children when the parent scope exits — ["it is impossible for this task to outlive its parent"][spawn]. The guarantees compose at every depth.

candidates.heading: Direct grammar use with agent()
Single agent: agent
What does NOT change
candidates.text:
## What does NOT change

Tarball bytes are signed exactly as you submitted them. Lloyal Labs does not modify your code. If a revision is needed, you submit a new version with a new semver.

The signature is over the tarball bytes — anyone can verify integrity by fetching the tarball + manifest from `apps.lloyal.ai` and Ed25519-verifying against the framework-vendored `LLOYAL_PLATFORM_KEY_2026_Q2` public key.

candidates.heading: v0.1
candidates.text:
## v0.1

- **`Source` decoupled from orchestration.** Sources now expose data-access tools and chunks (`tools`, `bind`, `getChunks`); the harness drives orchestration via `agentPool()`. A source no longer owns prompts, recursion shape, or policy.
- **`Agent` stabilized as first-class API.** Internal `AgentState` shape is no longer exported; introspect via the `Agent` class (`status`, `result`, `branch`, `traceBuffer`, `currentTool`).
- **`useAgent` / `useAgentPool` introduced** as the dual resource layers; `agent()` is a convenience alias for `useAgent()` with N=1.

candidates.heading: Spine toolkit unification
Why Bridges Exist
candidates.text:
## Why Bridges Exist

Without a bridge, source N+1 re-investigates everything source N already covered. The web source searches for the same topics the corpus source already established. You waste KV budget on redundant retrieval.

A bridge solves this by extracting what was found, what is incomplete, and what is missing. The next source targets gaps instead of re-covering established ground.

title: Your first App
