- [Continuous-context spine](/under-the-hood/continuous-context-spine) — how `extendSpine` and prefix sharing compose
- [Concurrency](/under-the-hood/concurrency) — the five-phase tick loop and DAG orchestration
- [Sources & retrieval](/build-an-app/sources-and-retrieval) — the `Source<TCtx, TChunk>` contract
- [Bridges](/build-a-harness/bridges) — multi-source pipelines
- [Compare](/examples/compare) — a worked DAG harness using these patterns

candidates.heading: How cloud APIs and orchestration frameworks handle tools
candidates.text:
### How cloud APIs and orchestration frameworks handle tools

To understand the value of prefix sharing, it helps to see what happens without it.

**Cloud APIs rebuild per request.** Both the Anthropic Messages API and OpenAI Chat Completions API require the full `tools` array on every request. Each API call re-processes all tool schemas as input tokens. The Anthropic API charges input tokens for the tool definitions plus a fixed system prompt overhead (~346 tokens for Claude Opus/Sonnet). In a 6-turn tool-using conversation with 5 tools, the tool schemas are re-processed 6 times.

Anthropic's prompt caching mitigates this: marking the last tool with `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }` caches the tool definitions server-side. Subsequent requests with identical tools get cache hits at 10% of the input token price. However, prompt caching is a billing optimization — the tokens still count toward context length, and the model still attends to them on every turn. The KV computation may be accelerated server-side, but the client has no control over or visibility into this.

**MCP adds a discovery layer, not a caching layer.** MCP (Model Context Protocol) provides a `tools/list` RPC for discovering available tools from servers. The client receives tool schemas, then includes them in LLM API requests — which are standard API calls with the full tool array. MCP itself does not cache tool schemas in the model's context or share them across calls. The caching (if any) depends on the underlying API provider's prompt caching support.

**Orchestration frameworks rebuild the prompt per step.** Frameworks like LangChain's AgentExecutor and LangGraph construct the full prompt (system + tools + conversation history) on every agent step, then send it as a single API call. Each step re-serializes and re-sends the complete tool list. There is no cross-step KV sharing — each API call is independent.

candidates.heading: The hardLimit >= nBatch invariant
candidates.text:
## The `hardLimit >= nBatch` invariant

`hardLimit` isn't an arbitrary safety margin — it's the **parallel report budget** for the recovery path. When `pressure.critical` fires and the kill proceeds, `recoverInline` prefills its extraction prompt and runs a single-agent produce/commit loop *inside* the reserved `hardLimit` cells. If that reserve is smaller than the context's `nBatch`, the first recovery decode fails with `llama_decode: failed to find a memory slot for batch of size N` — native OOM.

The pool enforces this at `useAgentPool` entry. If `hardLimit < nBatch`, the pool throws:

```
useAgentPool: Invariant Violation — hardLimit (128) must be >= nBatch (512).
Recovery reserves hardLimit cells for its own decode; if smaller than nBatch,
the next batch allocation will OOM. Increase policy.budget.context.hardLimit
to at least 512.
```

This is a compile-time/startup check, not a runtime surprise. Misconfigured policies fail loudly before any agents spawn.

<Note>
Until `SessionContext.nBatch` is exposed natively, the pool validates against `ContextPressure.ASSUMED_N_BATCH = 512` (llama.cpp's default `n_batch`). Once the native exposure lands, the check reads `ctx.nBatch` directly so policies running against a custom batch size validate against the actual value.
</Note>

candidates.heading: "I have many agents sharing the same tool set" — systemPrompt (shared-mode)
candidates.text:
#### "I have many agents sharing the same tool set" — `systemPrompt` (shared-mode)

When set, `agentPool` runs in shared-mode: the chat header (system + tool schemas) is prefilled onto an inner querySpine once at setup; every agent inherits via metadata-only KV prefix-share instead of re-emitting the schemas in its own suffix. See [Mixed-role pools](/under-the-hood/mixed-role-pools) for the full convention and tradeoffs (notably: in shared-mode, `extendSpine` writes to the inner spine, not the warmParent).

For chains where you want spine extensions to persist past pool exit (so post-pool synth can attend to them), put the shared catalog on the **harness's** outer `withSpine`, not on `agentPool` directly.

candidates.heading: cellsUsed tracks live pressure
candidates.text:
## `cellsUsed` tracks live pressure

<Note>
`cellsUsed` increments on every `decode_each` (COMMIT, +1 per branch) and `decode_scatter` (SETTLE prefill, +tokens per branch). It **decrements** when a branch is pruned — each branch subtracts its unique cells (`position - fork_head`) on release. It resets on bulk operations: `retainOnly` sets it to the winner's position, `drain` resets to zero.

`cellsUsed` can exceed `nCtx` — there is no pre-decode guard in the native layer. Protection is entirely in the JS layer via `ContextPressure.critical` + the `hardLimit >= nBatch` invariant, which together guarantee the next decode has a valid batch allocation.

On `llama_decode` failure, `cellsUsed` is NOT rolled back (it remains at the post-increment value), but the branch's sampler, grammar, and metrics state IS rolled back via the native snapshot/restore mechanism.
</Note>

`remaining` and `percentAvailable` reflect actual KV usage and **recover** as branches are freed. When an agent reports and its branch is pruned (`pruneOnReturn: true`), the freed cells are visible to the next `ContextPressure` snapshot. Remaining agents see increased headroom.

This has a direct impact on explore/exploit mode: `percentAvailable` can rise above the `exploreThreshold` after branches are pruned, switching surviving agents back to explore mode. The flip is intentional — it produces higher quality trajectories by allowing discovery when headroom recovers.

candidates.heading: 4. SETTLE deferral (tool result gating)
Clearing the cache
candidates.text:
### Clearing the cache

```bash
rm ~/.cache/lloyal/auth.json
```

The next authenticated command will trigger a fresh browser flow.

candidates.heading: Kill / nudge decision matrix
candidates.text:
## Kill / nudge decision matrix

The decision to kill or nudge an agent happens at multiple phases, each with its own inputs and side-effect chain. Reading the code site-by-site makes the behavior hard to reason about — the following matrix enumerates every decision point.

candidates.heading: Distribution
candidates.text:
## Distribution

First-party Apps that ship inside the HDK reference harness (`reasoning.run`) are loaded as build-time imports (the harness `import { createWebApp } from '@lloyal-labs/web-app'` and hands the factory to `createAppRegistry`). Third-party Apps — and any App reaching a harness it doesn't own — flow through the canonical **signed-bundle channel** at `apps.lloyal.ai`. The harness resolves Apps by name (`yield* loadBundle('jira', { semver: '^1.2.0' })`); the catalog URL and the trust roots used to verify signatures are **compile-time constants in `@lloyal-labs/rig`** — the harness does not configure them.

The runtime primitives live in `@lloyal-labs/rig` (`loadBundle`, `verifyBundle`, `CHANNEL_CATALOG_URL`, `CHANNEL_TRUST_ROOTS`). The `harness.dev` CLI wraps them for scaffolding, publishing, and installing Apps. [Distribution](/build-an-app/distribution) covers the full flow.

---

candidates.heading: Diagnostic
candidates.text:
#### Diagnostic

- **`trace`** — when true, emits per-token entropy and surprisal on `agent:produce` events. The TraceWriter itself is set globally via `initAgents`.

candidates.heading: Typical workflows
candidates.text:
## Typical workflows

**As an app developer:**

```bash
# One-time setup
harness.dev publishers register --handle acme

# Scaffold a new app
harness.dev app jira
cd jira
# ... write your app ...

# Publish it
harness.dev publish
harness.dev publish status <submission-id>   # poll until approved
```

**As a harness developer (consumer of apps):**

```bash
# Scaffold a new harness
harness.dev my-harness
cd my-harness

# Install signed apps from the channel
harness.dev install lloyal/web
harness.dev install acme/jira

# Then in your harness code:
# import { createWebApp } from '@lloyal-labs/web-app';
# import { createJiraApp } from '@acme/jira-app';
```

candidates.heading: Tool anatomy
Budget-aware recovery prompt
candidates.text:
### Budget-aware recovery prompt

The recovery prompt is an eta template. `DefaultAgentPolicy.onRecovery` renders it with the current `budget` before returning to the pool. A minimal recovery prompt:

```eta
You are a research reporter. The agent has been terminated due to budget
constraints and must deliver findings now.

Call the report tool with the most important findings from the research above.
Prioritize in this order:
1. Direct quotes and specific evidence that answer the task
2. Key conclusions with source URLs / line references
3. Supporting context (only if budget allows)

IMPORTANT: You have approximately <%= it.budget %> words for your report. Be concise —
if you exceed the budget your report will be cut off.
---
Report your findings.
```

The `<%= it.budget %>` tag is substituted at `onRecovery` call time with the numeric budget. Prompts that don't use the tag render identically (backward compatible).

candidates.heading: Install a signed app
candidates.text:
## Install a signed app

```bash
npx harness.dev install <publisher>/<short>[@<semver>] [--allow-scripts]
```

**Examples:**

```bash
# Install the latest lloyal/web
npx harness.dev install lloyal/web

# Install at a specific version
npx harness.dev install lloyal/web@1.0.0

# Install with a semver range (highest matching is picked)
npx harness.dev install acme/jira@^2.0.0
```

The CLI:

1. Fetches `apps.lloyal.ai/v1/catalog.json`; Ed25519-verifies its signature against `CHANNEL_TRUST_ROOTS` (vendored in `@lloyal-labs/rig`).
2. Resolves `<publisher>/<short>[@<semver>]` to a specific catalog version entry. The entry carries `importName` — the npm package name to install.
3. Fetches the entry's `manifestUrl`; signature-verifies it; cross-checks `name` / `version` / `sizeBytes` against the catalog entry.
4. Fetches the tarball at `entry.tarballUrl`; Ed25519-verifies against the manifest's signature.
5. Computes the sha512 integrity locally for the post-install lockfile audit.
6. Caches the verified tarball at `$XDG_CACHE_HOME/lloyal/apps/<publisher>__<short>-<version>.tgz`.
7. Seeds npm's HTTP cache so step 8 resolves from local cache (no second download).
8. Runs `npm install --ignore-scripts --save <canonical-tarball-URL>` in `process.cwd()`. The lockfile records the canonical channel URL + sha512.
9. Re-reads the lockfile and confirms npm-recorded integrity matches what we computed pre-install. If they diverge, the package is uninstalled and the command errors (defense against an MITM between our verify-fetch and npm's install-fetch).

**Successful output:**

```bash
$ npx harness.dev install lloyal/web
# harness.dev install: resolved lloyal/web → lloyal/web@1.0.0
# harness.dev install: running `npm install --ignore-scripts --save https://apps.lloyal.ai/v1/bundles/lloyal__web-1.0.0.tgz` in /Users/dev/my-harness...
# (npm output)
# installed lloyal/web@1.0.0
#   package:   @lloyal-labs/web-app
#   tarball:   https://apps.lloyal.ai/v1/bundles/lloyal__web-1.0.0.tgz
#   integrity: sha512-GpJXpc+qHA6SI...
#   cached:    /Users/dev/.cache/lloyal/apps/lloyal__web-1.0.0.tgz
```

candidates.heading: Boot set vs dynamic enable
candidates.text:
## Boot set vs dynamic enable

Two ways an App enters the registry:

**Build-time imports (the boot set).** The harness `import`s factories and hands them to `createAppRegistry({ apps: [...] })`. This is the path the reasoning.run harness uses today — `createWebApp` and `createCorpusApp` are build-time deps.

**Dynamic enable.** `yield* registry.enable(factory)` mid-session. This is the path channel-distributed Apps use: `yield* loadBundle('jira', { semver: '^1.2.0' })` resolves against `apps.lloyal.ai` (URL and trust roots are framework-locked constants), verifies, and returns an `AppFactory`. `enable` then runs it in its own scope. See [Distribution](/build-an-app/distribution).

Both paths converge on the same invariant: the App enters the registry only if (a) the factory body completes without throwing, (b) `defineApp` validation passes, (c) the App protocol version is supported, and (d) an existing entry with the same `name` doesn't already occupy the slot.

queries.text: ContextPressure partition KV cache hardLimit nBatch

candidates.heading: pressureThresholds
candidates.text:
### `pressureThresholds`

The pool reads `policy.pressureThresholds` once at setup to configure `ContextPressure` snapshots. Returns `{ softLimit, hardLimit }` from `budget.context` or the class defaults (1024, 512). The pool also validates `hardLimit >= nBatch` at this point and throws `Invariant Violation` if not satisfied.

candidates.heading: The hardLimit >= nBatch invariant
candidates.text:
## The `hardLimit >= nBatch` invariant

`hardLimit` isn't an arbitrary safety margin — it's the **parallel report budget** for the recovery path. When `pressure.critical` fires and the kill proceeds, `recoverInline` prefills its extraction prompt and runs a single-agent produce/commit loop *inside* the reserved `hardLimit` cells. If that reserve is smaller than the context's `nBatch`, the first recovery decode fails with `llama_decode: failed to find a memory slot for batch of size N` — native OOM.

The pool enforces this at `useAgentPool` entry. If `hardLimit < nBatch`, the pool throws:

```
useAgentPool: Invariant Violation — hardLimit (128) must be >= nBatch (512).
Recovery reserves hardLimit cells for its own decode; if smaller than nBatch,
the next batch allocation will OOM. Increase policy.budget.context.hardLimit
to at least 512.
```

This is a compile-time/startup check, not a runtime surprise. Misconfigured policies fail loudly before any agents spawn.

<Note>
Until `SessionContext.nBatch` is exposed natively, the pool validates against `ContextPressure.ASSUMED_N_BATCH = 512` (llama.cpp's default `n_batch`). Once the native exposure lands, the check reads `ctx.nBatch` directly so policies running against a custom batch size validate against the actual value.
</Note>

candidates.heading: The budget model
candidates.text:
## The budget model

`ContextPressure` reads from `SessionContext._storeKvPressure()`, which returns `{ nCtx, cellsUsed, remaining }` where `remaining = nCtx - cellsUsed`. Two thresholds partition `remaining` into three zones:

```
                nCtx
 ┌──────────┬───────────────────┬──────────┬────────┐
 │cellsUsed │    headroom > 0   │ softLimit│hardLim │
 │ (in use) │   (new work OK)   │(reserved)│(recov) │
 └──────────┴───────────────────┴──────────┴────────┘
              <- remaining ->
                                |          |
 headroom = remaining - softLimit          |
 critical = remaining < hardLimit ---------┘
```

- **headroom > 0** — room for new work. Tool results can be prefilled, agents can continue generating.
- **headroom ≤ 0** — over budget. SETTLE defers tool results. PRODUCE nudges the model to wrap up.
- **critical** — `remaining` has dropped below `hardLimit`. Agents are killed before `produceSync()` to prevent `llama_decode` crashes. The `hardLimit` reserve is the recovery extraction budget.

candidates.heading: ContextPressure API
candidates.text:
## `ContextPressure` API

The class is constructed from a `SessionContext` and thresholds from the policy. It captures an immutable snapshot of KV state at construction time — frozen at phase boundaries so that all decisions within a phase are evaluated against the same baseline. Without this, items processed earlier in a loop would see different pressure than items processed later, making reject/nudge/kill decisions order-dependent and nondeterministic.

The pool reads thresholds from `policy.pressureThresholds` (which comes from `DefaultAgentPolicyOpts.budget.context`):

```typescript
const policy = new DefaultAgentPolicy({
  budget: { context: { softLimit: 1024, hardLimit: 1024 } },
});

pressure.nCtx              // total KV cache capacity (0 when no limit)
pressure.cellsUsed         // KV cells currently in use (monotonic within a pool run)
pressure.remaining         // nCtx - cellsUsed (Infinity when nCtx <= 0)
pressure.headroom          // remaining - softLimit
pressure.critical          // remaining < hardLimit
pressure.canFit(n)         // n <= headroom
pressure.percentAvailable  // 0–100 integer: remaining / nCtx * 100
```

`percentAvailable` is the single source of truth for the KV availability percentage. It is used by `policy.shouldExplore()` to decide explore vs exploit mode, recorded on `ToolHistoryEntry.contextAfterPercent` after each tool result settles, and injected into tool results as `_contextAvailablePercent` so agents can make pressure-aware decisions.

**Defaults:** `softLimit = 1024`, `hardLimit = 512` (matches llama.cpp's default `n_batch`). Pools that use recovery with a larger expected report body should raise `hardLimit` accordingly — e.g. `hardLimit: 1024` to fit a ~400-token recovery extraction.

candidates.heading:
title: "KV Pressure"
description: "The pressure budget model — how ContextPressure partitions the KV cache and where enforcement happens in the tick loop."
candidates.text:
title: "KV Pressure"
description: "The pressure budget model — how `ContextPressure` partitions the KV cache and where enforcement happens in the tick loop."
---

Agent pools share a single KV cache. As agents generate tokens and prefill tool results, the cache fills. `ContextPressure` tracks how much room remains and enforces thresholds at multiple points in the tick loop to prevent out-of-memory crashes. When an agent is killed by pressure, an inline recovery protocol extracts its findings before the pool continues, using the `hardLimit` reserve as a parallel report budget.

> **See also.** This page documents the *budget model and enforcement mechanics*. For the policy hooks that act on pressure events (`onProduced`, `shouldExit`, `onRecovery`), see [Agent Policy](/under-the-hood/agent-policy). For picking `nCtx` / `softLimit` / KV quantization values for a given deployment, see [Deployment](/guides/deployment).

candidates.heading: ContextPressure Across Nested Pools
candidates.text:
### `ContextPressure` Across Nested Pools

Both inner and outer pools create `ContextPressure` snapshots from the same `SessionContext`. Each snapshot captures `nCtx`, `cellsUsed`, `remaining`, and derives `percentAvailable` (0–100 integer). `cellsUsed` increments on every decode and prefill, and **decrements** when branches are pruned (each branch subtracts its unique cells: `position - fork_head`). This means pressure is live — when an inner pool completes and its branches are pruned, `remaining` and `percentAvailable` recover. The outer pool's next DISPATCH sees the freed headroom. `percentAvailable` drives `policy.shouldExplore()` — the explore/exploit decision at each DISPATCH, which can flip back to explore as pressure recovers. Two thresholds govern behavior:

- **`softLimit`** (default 1024) — remaining KV floor for new work. SETTLE defers tool results that would cross this floor. PRODUCE nudges non-terminal tool calls with a word-budget message ("within N words") when headroom is negative. Terminal tools pass.

- **`hardLimit`** (default 512) — crash-prevention floor AND recovery-extraction reserve. Agents killed immediately before `produceSync()` when `remaining < hardLimit`; `recoverInline` runs in the reserved cells. Must be >= context's `nBatch` — the pool validates at startup and throws `Invariant Violation` if not.

<Tip>
For recursive pipelines, the outer pool's `softLimit` must account for the inner pool's KV consumption. An inner pool with 3 agents, each consuming ~500 tokens of shared prefix + tool results, needs ~1500 tokens of headroom. Set the outer pool's `softLimit` accordingly.
</Tip>

candidates.heading: KV Cache
candidates.text:
### KV Cache

Inner and outer agents share a single `BranchStore` and a single KV cache. `ContextPressure` snapshots `remaining = nCtx - cellsUsed` on every tick. Inner agent prefills consume the same budget as outer agent prefills. When the inner pool runs, it reduces headroom for the outer pool's remaining agents.

KV pressure governs agent lifecycle at four points:

1. **Prefill gate** — at pool setup, the pool computes the total suffix cost (formatted chat tokens) for all agents. If the total exceeds headroom, agents are dropped from the end until the remaining suffixes fit. Dropped agents are marked `done` before generating a single token. This is the primary mechanism that limits sub-agent count in nested pools — outer agents' live branches consume headroom that the inner pool's prefill gate measures against.

2. **PRODUCE hard-cut** — agents are killed before `produceSync()` when remaining drops below `hardLimit`. The kill reserves the `hardLimit` cells for `recoverInline` to extract findings via the eager-report grammar. Terminal-tool protection (`agent.currentTool === terminalToolName`) yields to critical — mid-report agents get killed if they push past the threshold, and recovery takes over with a budget-aware prompt.

3. **PRODUCE soft-cut** — non-terminal tool calls are denied when headroom is negative. Terminal tools (e.g. `report`) always pass.

4. **SETTLE deferral** — tool results that would cross the `softLimit` floor are deferred to the next tick, giving siblings a chance to free KV (parallel) or the spine to accept the next turn (chain). If `deferred` items have no active siblings, the stall-breaker consults `policy.onSettleReject` — which returns a budget-aware nudge (replacing the oversized result) or idle (drop + recovery). Distinct drop reasons: `pressure_settle_reject` (policy-driven) vs `settle_stall_break` (hook absent).

candidates.heading: Configure budget and recovery
shouldExit — pre-generate kill gate
candidates.text:
## `shouldExit` — pre-generate kill gate

Called before `produceSync()` — before the agent generates any tokens. Returns `true` to kill the agent immediately. There's no nudge possible here — there's no tool call to attach an error message to. The branch stays alive for recovery via `onRecovery`.

```typescript
// PRODUCE: before each agent generates
if (policy.shouldExit?.(agent, pressure) ?? pressure.critical) {
  // transition to idle, eligible for recovery
}
```

Optional — defaults to `pressure.critical` when absent. `DefaultAgentPolicy` also checks the time budget:

- `pressure.critical` (remaining < hardLimit) → exit
- `budget.time.hardLimit` exceeded → exit
- **Terminal-tool protection (narrowed to the graceful zone)**: if `terminalToolName` is configured, `agent.currentTool` matches, AND `!pressure.critical`, `shouldExit` returns `false`. An agent actively generating its report is protected from arbitrary kills (time limits, custom policy decisions). Once `pressure.critical` fires, protection yields — the kill proceeds so `recoverInline` can extract findings before native decode OOMs. This narrowing prevents runaway free-text-into-terminal-tool patterns from exhausting the context.

`agent.currentTool` is populated by `Agent.observe(ctx)`, which runs a partial parse (`parseChatOutput` with `isPartial: true`) on each generated token until the tool name is detected. The partial parser is format-agnostic — it uses the same PEG parser that handles the full output at `isStop`, just in lenient mode.

<Note>
The pool validates `hardLimit >= nBatch` at startup. If `policy.pressureThresholds.hardLimit` is smaller than the context's `nBatch` (the native batch allocation size, default 512), `useAgentPool` throws with `Invariant Violation: hardLimit (N) must be >= nBatch (M)`. The reserve below `hardLimit` is recovery's parallel report budget — smaller than `nBatch` means the first recovery decode can't allocate its batch.
</Note>

candidates.heading: DefaultAgentPolicy configuration
Branch.create -- cold start
cellsUsed tracks live pressure
candidates.text:
## `cellsUsed` tracks live pressure

<Note>
`cellsUsed` increments on every `decode_each` (COMMIT, +1 per branch) and `decode_scatter` (SETTLE prefill, +tokens per branch). It **decrements** when a branch is pruned — each branch subtracts its unique cells (`position - fork_head`) on release. It resets on bulk operations: `retainOnly` sets it to the winner's position, `drain` resets to zero.

`cellsUsed` can exceed `nCtx` — there is no pre-decode guard in the native layer. Protection is entirely in the JS layer via `ContextPressure.critical` + the `hardLimit >= nBatch` invariant, which together guarantee the next decode has a valid batch allocation.

On `llama_decode` failure, `cellsUsed` is NOT rolled back (it remains at the post-increment value), but the branch's sampler, grammar, and metrics state IS rolled back via the native snapshot/restore mechanism.
</Note>

`remaining` and `percentAvailable` reflect actual KV usage and **recover** as branches are freed. When an agent reports and its branch is pruned (`pruneOnReturn: true`), the freed cells are visible to the next `ContextPressure` snapshot. Remaining agents see increased headroom.

This has a direct impact on explore/exploit mode: `percentAvailable` can rise above the `exploreThreshold` after branches are pruned, switching surviving agents back to explore mode. The flip is intentional — it produces higher quality trajectories by allowing discovery when headroom recovers.

candidates.heading: Context pressure tuning
candidates.text:
## Context pressure tuning

Context pressure controls when agents are shut down as KV fills. Two thresholds:

```typescript
const researchPolicy = new DefaultAgentPolicy({
  budget: { context: { softLimit: 1024, hardLimit: 128 } },
  recovery: { prompt: REPORT },
});
```

**softLimit** (default 1024) -- remaining KV floor for new work. When remaining tokens drop below this:
- SETTLE rejects tool results (primary enforcement point)
- PRODUCE nudges agents to report (terminal tools like `report` still pass)
- INIT drops agents that don't fit during setup

Set this to account for downstream work. If synthesis needs ~1000 tokens of headroom, set research pool softLimit to at least 1024.

**hardLimit** (default 128) -- crash-prevention floor. When remaining drops below this, `shouldExit` kills agents immediately before `produceSync()`. This is a safety net, not a tuning knob.

For reporter sub-pools that only make one terminal tool call, use the default policy (softLimit 1024, hardLimit 128).

candidates.heading: Recovery and hardLimit
candidates.text:
### Recovery and hardLimit

The `hardLimit` reserve exists specifically for recovery. Agents are killed when `remaining < hardLimit`. Recovery needs to fit:

- Extraction prompt prefill: ~100–200 tokens (short system + user turn).
- JSON `{"result": "..."}` generation: 200–500 tokens for a typical recovery report.
- Native batch workspace: ~512 cells (the `nBatch` floor).

Default `hardLimit = 512` satisfies the invariant but is tight for verbose reports. Policies expecting longer extractions should bump to 1024+.

<Note>
The recovery pressure check uses `remaining < promptTokens` (raw capacity), not `canFit` or `critical`. The `hardLimit` reserve was set aside precisely for this moment — checking `critical` would always fail because agents were just killed at the critical threshold.
</Note>

candidates.heading: The interface
Locked by tests
candidates.text:
### Locked by tests

The following cells have dedicated scenario tests in `packages/agents/test/invariants/scenarios/`:

| Cell | Scenario |
|---|---|
| PRODUCE top: critical + narrowed protection yields | `critical-kill-despite-protection` |
| PRODUCE `isStop`: nudge path | (covered via `nudge-message-includes-budget`) |
| SETTLE stall-break: nudge fits → no drop | `pressure-exit-via-settle-policy-nudge` |
| SETTLE stall-break: nudge infeasible → drop | `pressure-exit-via-stall-break` |
| Parallel staggered-exit defers across ticks | `parallel-staggered-exit` |
| Recovery diagnostic events fire | `recovery-fails` |
| Recovery template rendering | `recovery-prompt-budget-substitution` |
| `hardLimit >= nBatch` invariant throw | `hardLimit-nBatch-invariant` |
| End-to-end decision matrix | `decision-matrix` |

candidates.heading: Sizing thresholds
candidates.text:
## Sizing thresholds

For **single-level pools** with default `nBatch=512`:

| Policy | softLimit | hardLimit | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Baseline (default) | 1024 | 512 | Minimum invariant-satisfying config |
| Research with recovery | 1024–2048 | 1024 | Generous recovery budget for recovery extraction |
| Chain pipeline (4+ tasks) | 2048 | 1024 | Spine accumulates across tasks — size for the last task's constraints |
| Nested / recursive pools | 3072 | 1024 | Outer pool reserves enough for inner pool's full lifecycle |

```typescript
const policy = new DefaultAgentPolicy({
  budget: { context: { softLimit: 2048, hardLimit: 1024 } },
});

yield* useAgentPool({
  root,
  orchestrate: chain(tasks, (task, i) => ({ /* ... */ })),
  tools: toolMap,
  maxTurns: 8,
  policy,
});
```

<Tip>
For recursive pipelines, the outer pool's `softLimit` must reserve enough for the inner pool's KV consumption. An inner pool with 3 agents consuming ~500 tokens each of shared prefix + tool results needs ~1500 tokens of headroom. Set the outer pool's `softLimit` accordingly.
</Tip>

Monitor with `trace: true` — the `[SETTLE]` and `[PRODUCE]` pressure logs print `remaining`, `headroom`, `cellsUsed`, and `nCtx` at each tick. Recovery logs `pool:recoveryProduce` / `pool:recoveryReturn` / `pool:recoveryFailed`:

```
[PRODUCE] SOFT_LIMIT remaining=2200 headroom=-104 cellsUsed=14184 nCtx=16384
[SETTLE] DEFER read_file:1059 > headroom=-104
[SETTLE] NUDGE read_file: 33tok (replacing 1059)
[RECOVERY] prefilling agent=3, 143 tokens, remaining=1024
pool:recoveryProduce agent=3 tokenCount=218 outputLength=1204
pool:recoveryReturn  agent=3 resultLength=1180
```

candidates.heading: Drop reasons
candidates.text:
## Drop reasons

The `pool:agentDrop` trace event records the specific enforcement point:

| Reason | Origin | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `pressure_init` | `ctx.spawn` prefill gate | Agent suffix doesn't fit under softLimit |
| `pressure_critical` | PRODUCE `shouldExit` | `remaining < hardLimit`; recovery runs in reserve |
| `pressure_softcut` | PRODUCE `_handleOverBudget` | Non-terminal over-budget + no terminal tool available |
| `pressure_settle_reject` | SETTLE stall-break (policy-driven) | Policy returned `idle` or nudge that can't fit |
| `settle_stall_break` | SETTLE stall-break (legacy fallback) | `onSettleReject` hook absent |
| `time_exceeded` | PRODUCE shouldExit (time hardLimit) | Wall-clock hardLimit crossed |
| `policy_exit` | PRODUCE shouldExit (custom policy) | Policy returned `true` not driven by pressure |
| `maxTurns` | PRODUCE (policy `_handleOverBudget`) | Turn count ≥ maxTurns, no terminal tool |
| `tool_error` | DISPATCH | Tool's `execute()` threw |

Every drop reason (except `pressure_init`, which is pre-spawn) is followed by a recovery attempt whose outcome is visible in `pool:recoveryReturn` or `pool:recoveryFailed` — no silent data loss.

candidates.heading: "How do I tune budgets and recovery?" — policy
Writing a custom policy
shouldExplore — explore vs exploit mode
candidates.text:
## `shouldExplore` — explore vs exploit mode

Called at DISPATCH before each tool executes. Returns `true` for explore mode (agent-local scoring at content boundaries) or `false` for exploit mode (dual scoring via [scoreRelevanceBatch](/under-the-hood/search-strategy#content-boundaries-explore-vs-exploit)).

Optional — defaults to `true` (explore) when absent. `DefaultAgentPolicy` checks two independent axes:

- **Context axis**: `pressure.percentAvailable / 100 > shouldExplore.context` (default 0.4). Below this fraction of KV capacity remaining → exploit.
- **Time axis**: `elapsed / timeSoftLimit >= shouldExplore.time` (default 0.5). Above this fraction of the time soft limit consumed → exploit. Only applies when `budget.time.softLimit` is set.

Both axes must agree on explore; either falling below its threshold flips the policy into exploit mode.

<Note>
`percentAvailable` is live — it recovers when branches are pruned. An agent in exploit mode can flip back to explore if other agents report and free KV. This is intentional. Explore/exploit is a non-monotonic quality knob, separate from the lifecycle (nudge/kill/recover). See [KV Pressure — cellsUsed tracks live pressure](/under-the-hood/kv-pressure#cellsused-tracks-live-pressure).
</Note>

candidates.heading: Context size (nCtx)
Pressure-driven agent lifecycle
candidates.text:
## Pressure-driven agent lifecycle

<Steps>
  <Step title="Pool setup">
    `hardLimit >= nBatch` invariant validated. Misconfigured policies throw at `useAgentPool` entry.
  </Step>
  <Step title="ctx.spawn">
    Prefill gate rejects agents whose suffix doesn't fit under softLimit. Agent dropped silently with reason `pressure_init`.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Each tick: PRODUCE critical">
    `pressure.critical` fires → `shouldExit` kills one agent → `recoverInline` extracts in the `hardLimit` reserve. Terminal-tool protection yields in critical.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Each tick: PRODUCE soft-cut">
    Non-terminal tool call with `headroom < 0` → policy nudges with "within N words" budget. Terminal tools pass.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Each tick: SETTLE deferral">
    Oversized tool results defer to next tick. Siblings freeing KV (parallel) or spine extension (chain) restores headroom.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Stall-break">
    If deferred items have no active siblings to free KV, policy's `onSettleReject` is consulted. Nudge → replace result with compact payload. Idle → drop with `pressure_settle_reject`. No hook → `settle_stall_break`.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Recovery + diagnostics">
    Every drop path runs `recoverInline` (unless `onRecovery` returns skip). Diagnostic events: `pool:recoveryProduce`, then exactly one of `pool:recoveryReturn` or `pool:recoveryFailed`.
  </Step>
</Steps>

candidates.heading: Sequence count (nSeqMax)
3. GPU batch uses inherited positions
candidates.text:
### 3. GPU batch uses inherited positions

```cpp
// decode.hpp — building the llama_batch
const int32_t pos = n_past + i;  // n_past = 866, i = 0,1,2,...
```

The suffix tokens are placed at positions 866, 867, 868, etc. The GPU attends to prior KV entries at positions 0-865 (already in cache from the spine) and computes new KV entries only for the suffix positions. The spine's tokens are never re-processed.

candidates.heading: Kill / nudge decision matrix
candidates.text:
## Kill / nudge decision matrix

The decision to kill or nudge an agent happens at multiple phases, each with its own inputs and side-effect chain. Reading the code site-by-site makes the behavior hard to reason about — the following matrix enumerates every decision point.

candidates.heading: Enforcement points
candidates.text:
## Enforcement points

`ContextPressure` is instantiated at multiple points in the tick loop. Each snapshot is fresh — constructed from current `cellsUsed` at that moment.

candidates.heading: The nudge mechanism
candidates.text:
## The nudge mechanism

Nudging is stateless — no escalation, no tracking of past nudges. When the agent is over budget and calls a non-terminal tool, the policy returns `nudge` with a message. The pool:

1. Serializes the message as a tool result: `{ "error": "..." }`
2. Prefills the error into the agent's branch
3. Resets the grammar so the agent generates a fresh response

The agent sees the error in its context and may call the terminal tool on its next turn. If it tries another non-terminal tool while still over budget, the policy nudges again. If headroom recovers (branches pruned, KV freed), the agent is no longer over budget and can continue working.

There is no "second offense" kill. The hard kill is `shouldExit`'s job — triggered by `pressure.critical` or time `hardLimit`, not by repeated nudges.

Nudge messages carry the model's remaining token budget — `pressure.remaining - pressure.hardLimit` — interpolated into the string. The model reads this and can self-cap its response length:

| Source | Message |
|--------|---------|
| KV pressure (PRODUCE) | "KV memory pressure — report your findings now within `{budget}` words." |
| Turn limit (PRODUCE) | "Turn limit reached — report your findings now within `{budget}` words." |
| Time budget (PRODUCE) | "Time limit reached — report your findings now within `{budget}` words." |
| SETTLE stall-break | "Tool result too large for remaining KV. Report your findings now within `{budget}` words." |
| Insufficient work | "You must use tools before submitting results." |
| Tool guard | The guard's custom `message` field |

The `{budget}` substituted into the message is derived as `floor((pressure.remaining - pressure.hardLimit) * 0.7 / 10) * 10` (minimum 10) — **words, not tokens**. Tokenizers vary across models but words are universal in training data, and the 0.7 ratio + floor-to-10 conservatively under-advertise the ceiling so the model stays within native decode headroom. Overshoot → `pressure.critical` fires → kill → recovery takes over with its own word budget inside the `hardLimit` reserve.

candidates.heading: KV quantization
candidates.text:
## KV quantization

KV cache quantization reduces memory per token at the cost of some precision. The two options:

| Type | Memory per token | Quality | Use case |
|------|-----------------|---------|----------|
| `q8_0` | ~2 bytes | Near-lossless | Small contexts, quality-critical |
| `q4_0` | ~1 byte | Slight degradation | Large contexts, recommended default |

```typescript
const ctx = yield* call(() =>
  createContext({
    modelPath,
    nCtx: 32768,
    nSeqMax: 15,
    typeK: 'q4_0',
    typeV: 'q4_0',
  }),
);
```

The reference pipeline uses `q4_0` for both K and V. At `nCtx=32768` with a 4B parameter model:

- **q8_0**: ~64MB KV cache
- **q4_0**: ~32MB KV cache

The quality difference is negligible for research pipelines where the primary bottleneck is tool-call quality, not raw generation precision. Use `q8_0` if you observe degraded output quality with long contexts.

You can mix K and V quantization (`typeK: 'q8_0', typeV: 'q4_0'`), but this is rarely needed. Both `q4_0` is the practical default.

candidates.heading: Defaults to start with
candidates.text:
## Defaults to start with

Most pipelines work with these defaults:

- **3 research agents** in parallel, **20 max tool turns** per agent.
- **3 verify branches** in `Eval` if used.
- **Context size 16384 tokens** (configurable per-harness).
- `DefaultAgentPolicy` with `softLimit: 1024`, `_minToolCalls: 2`, `echoThreshold: 0.8`, `_exploreContext: 0.4`.

Tighten or loosen these as your harness matures. The framework's defaults err on the side of agents reporting earlier rather than running out of KV.

candidates.heading: Sizing guidelines
candidates.text:
### Sizing guidelines

| Pipeline | Recommended nCtx | Reasoning |
|----------|-----------------|-----------|
| Single agent + tools | 8192 | One branch, moderate tool results |
| Simple research (1-2 sources) | 16384 | Default. 3 agents + synthesis + eval |
| Deep research (multi-source) | 32768 | Bridge stages, multiple source rounds |
| Large corpus research | 32768-65536 | Long documents inflate search/read results |

The budget divides across concurrent branches. With 3 research agents sharing a root:

```
Spine:     ~200 tokens (system prompt + tools)
Agent suffix:    ~300 tokens each (user message + generation prompt)
Tool results:    ~500-2000 tokens per call (varies by tool)
Generation:      ~200-500 tokens per turn
```

At `nCtx=16384` with 3 agents doing 4 tool calls each, rough budget:
- Spine: 200
- 3 agents x (300 suffix + 4 x 1000 tool results + 4 x 300 generation): ~15,900
- Remaining for synthesis + eval: ~300

This is tight. If agents hit large tool results (web pages, long search results), context pressure kicks in and starts cutting agents. For comfortable headroom, use 32768.

candidates.heading: Agent
candidates.text:
## Agent

A branch with intent. An **Agent** is a forked KV-cache sequence with a task, a system prompt, a tool set, and a policy that decides when it's done. Agents don't loop on their own — the **Pool**'s tick loop drives them.

- **Owns:** its branch, its system prompt, its toolkit, its result.
- **Go deeper:** [Agents & pools](/build-a-harness/agents-and-pools)

candidates.heading: Acquiring grants
candidates.text:
### Acquiring grants

Two mechanisms ship:

- **Harness consent prompts.** The harness asks the user before calling `grantStore.grant("send_email")`. The trivial case — a CLI tool prompts the user to confirm; a desktop app shows a system dialog.
- **App `ConfigFlow`.** An App that owns an OAuth-style flow (`web_research` connecting to a paid search backend, a Gmail App handing off through Google's OAuth) exposes a `configFlow` on its spec. The harness drives the flow, captures the credential into a secret store, and grants the relevant tools.

Both paths share the protocol: **consent moves from user → grant store; credential moves from user → secret store**. The model triggers; the runtime executes.

candidates.heading: Grammar on Branch
candidates.text:
## Grammar on `Branch`

The `Branch` class exposes three grammar methods:

**`setGrammar(grammarStr)`** -- Eager mode. Activates immediately. Every subsequent token is constrained. Pass an empty string to remove the grammar.

```typescript
branch.setGrammar(jsonGrammar);  // constrain from now
branch.setGrammar('');           // remove constraint
```

**`setGrammarLazy(grammar, triggers)`** -- Lazy mode. Free generation until a trigger fires, then constrained. Used by agent pools for tool-calling agents.

```typescript
branch.setGrammarLazy(grammar, [
  { type: GrammarTriggerType.WORD, value: '<tool_call>' },
]);
```

**Grammar state cloning on fork.** When a branch is forked, the grammar state is cloned. Sibling branches diverge independently -- one can be in constrained mode while another is still generating free text. This is a property of `forkSync()`, not a separate API call.

candidates.heading: Diagnostic
candidates.text:
#### Diagnostic

- **`trace`** — when true, emits per-token entropy and surprisal on `agent:produce` events. The TraceWriter itself is set globally via `initAgents`.

candidates.heading: A2 — progressive section coverage
candidates.text:
### A2 — progressive section coverage

```
1. grep(/\bDOJ\b.*\bApple\b.*\bIPod\b/)     → 0 matches
2. search("DOJ Apple iPod monopoly...")       → Super Apps section
3. read_file(L83-100)                         → 2812b, section A
4. read_file(L101-112)                        → 1612b, section B (third-party investment)
5. read_file(L133-146)                        → 1974b, Super Apps
6. read_file(L241-248)                        → 723b, First Claim
7. read_file(L253-256)                        → 313b, Fourth Claim
8. read_file(L125-132)                        → 1270b, contractual restrictions
→ report
```

A2 is the most systematic — eight reads spanning the document's full argumentative arc from historical narrative (section A) through current practices (Super Apps, contractual restrictions) to legal claims. No step-4 grep, but every read returns substantive content and the progression follows the document's logical structure.
  </Tab>
</Tabs>

<Accordion title="Divergences from the predicted pattern">
The predicted pattern (lines 68-76) shows 6 tool calls in a specific order. Actual agents deviate in predictable ways:

**More reads before broadening.** The predicted pattern shows one read, then immediately broadening. Agents consistently read 2-4 sections before constructing their hypothesis grep. The agent builds a thicker interpretive frame before committing to a hypothesis — a conservative strategy that produces better-informed grep patterns.

**Grep as hypothesis test, not search.** The predicted pattern uses `search("specific hypothesis formed from reading")` as the final step. Agents prefer `grep(/discovered concept/)` — a stricter test (exact pattern matching vs semantic similarity) that maps a concept's full extent across the document rather than finding a single best match.

**No final search in some trajectories.** When the broadening grep returns sufficient matches (26 matches across 11 lines), agents report directly. A search would be redundant — the grep already confirmed the hypothesis.

**Backward reasoning.** A1 reads conclusions before evidence, then tests whether the connecting concept exists as an explicit phrase. The decision boundary mechanism is the same (form hypothesis from reads, test it), but the traversal order is inverted.
</Accordion>

<Accordion title="Cross-run stability">
A0's trajectory is stable across 4+ independent runs with the same query:

| Run | Sections read | Step-4 grep pattern | Hypothesis source |
|-----|--------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | L83-100, L25-27, L97-99 | `/Apple\s+success\|market\s+position/` | market position concepts |
| 2 | L25-30, L55-58, L97-99 | `/market position\|success\|iPod\|iTunes\|platform\|ecosystem/` | platform/ecosystem terms |
| 3 | L25-30, L97-102, L55-56 | `/Apple.*(iPod\|iTunes\|music\|store\|platform)/` | iPod ecosystem terms |
| 4 | L83-100, L25-27, L55-57 | `/Microsoft\|United States v. Microsoft\|antitrust enforcement\|consent decree/` | Microsoft antitrust mechanism |

Same three document regions every run (L25-30, L55-5x, L83-100/L97-9x). The step-4 grep pattern varies — each run discovers a different facet of the connecting mechanism — but the pattern always targets concepts absent from the original question and discovered during reads. The exploration structure is deterministic; the specific hypothesis varies.
</Accordion>

candidates.heading:
title: What is an App
description: A packaged capability — Source + Tools + skill + manifest — wired with defineApp.
candidates.text:
title: What is an App
description: A packaged capability — Source + Tools + skill + manifest — wired with defineApp.
---

An **App** is the HDK's unit of capability packaging. A harness consumes Apps; a third party builds one. Same person can wear both hats on day one — the split scales when other teams start shipping Apps for harnesses they didn't write.

candidates.heading:
title: Sources & retrieval
description: The Source abstract base, the reranker-backed EntailmentScorer, and how RIG inverts classical RAG.
candidates.text:
title: Sources & retrieval
description: The Source abstract base, the reranker-backed EntailmentScorer, and how RIG inverts classical RAG.
---

A **Source** is the data-access half of an App. It wraps a live system (a corpus, the web, a vector store, a database) and exposes the tools an agent uses to read it — plus the reranker-backed scorer that decides whether what came back is relevant.

This page is the deep dive on the `Source` abstract base, the four scoring roles of the cross-encoder reranker, and how Retrieval Interleaved Generation (**RIG**) puts retrieval *inside* the model's decode loop rather than before it.

candidates.heading: Continuous Context
candidates.text: ## Continuous Context Most agent frameworks pass strings between stages. A transcript of what one agent generated becomes a prompt for the next; every step re-encodes the full context from text. The model's prior attention state — the specific weighted relationships it found between tokens — is discarded and rebuilt each call. **lloyal agents share live attention state.** When stage 2 starts decoding, stage 1's findings are already in its KV at their original positions; its first generated token attends over them directly. No re-tokenization. No re-encoding. No "summarize, paste, re-prompt" information bottleneck. When the model computes Q·Kᵀ for its next token, it attends over all K vectors at positions 0..N — including those written during prior tool result prefills. A fork is metadata-only: O(1) cost, zero tensor copy. Child branches share the same physical key-value pairs at inherited positions — not a re-encoding, the same vectors. **What this changes about agent behavior.** Watching real pipelines, agents form and test hypotheses through iterative retrieval — narrowing, discovering, hypothesizing, verifying — emergent from the attention mechanics, not from prompted instruction. Later searches reference concepts **absent from the original question**, discovered during earlier reads and still physically present in the KV cache. At its core, **an agent is simply a [liblloyal](https://lloyal-ai.github.io/liblloyal/) branch with intent** — a forked KV slot, a system prompt, a tool set, a terminal condition. Pools are sets of agents on a shared spine; harnesses orchestrate pools. The whole stack is branches all the way down.

queries.text: KV pressure management consumer applications context budget

candidates.heading:
title: "Deployment"
description: "Production tuning checklist — model choice, nCtx, KV quantization, policy thresholds. Actionable recipes that reference the underlying mechanics."
candidates.text:
title: "Deployment"
description: "Production tuning checklist — model choice, nCtx, KV quantization, policy thresholds. Actionable recipes that reference the underlying mechanics."
---

This guide covers model selection, context sizing, KV quantization, and runtime configuration for deploying a lloyal-agents pipeline.

> **See also.** This guide is the *tuning checklist*. For the policy interface (the hooks you're tuning), see [Agent Policy](/under-the-hood/agent-policy). For why the pressure thresholds matter (the budget model), see [KV Pressure](/under-the-hood/kv-pressure).

candidates.heading: Context pressure
candidates.text:
## Context pressure

The pool monitors KV cache usage every tick and enforces two thresholds:

- **Soft limit** (default 1024 tokens) — headroom reserved for downstream work. When exhausted, non-terminal tool calls are rejected.
- **Hard limit** (default 128 tokens) — crash-prevention floor. Agents are killed before `produceSync()`.

Pressure management is automatic. You don't check headroom in your tools. The pool drops agents gracefully when the context fills — agents that reported results keep them, agents that didn't get recovered via `policy.onRecovery()`.

candidates.heading:
title: "Agent Policy"
description: "The policy interface — lifecycle hooks (produce / settle / recover), budget gating, tool guards, recovery extraction."
candidates.text:
title: "Agent Policy"
description: "The policy interface — lifecycle hooks (produce / settle / recover), budget gating, tool guards, recovery extraction."
---

Every agent lifecycle decision passes through a policy. When an agent hits a stop token, when a tool result won't fit in KV, when a tool call is about to dispatch — the pool consults the policy and executes the returned action. The pool handles mechanics (transitions, prefills, prunes). The policy handles judgment.

> **See also.** This page documents the policy *interface*. For the underlying budget model and pressure thresholds, see [KV pressure](/under-the-hood/kv-pressure). For production tuning of `softLimit` / `hardLimit` / `recovery` per-deployment, see [Deployment](/guides/deployment).

candidates.heading:
title: "KV Pressure"
description: "The pressure budget model — how ContextPressure partitions the KV cache and where enforcement happens in the tick loop."
candidates.text:
title: "KV Pressure"
description: "The pressure budget model — how `ContextPressure` partitions the KV cache and where enforcement happens in the tick loop."
---

Agent pools share a single KV cache. As agents generate tokens and prefill tool results, the cache fills. `ContextPressure` tracks how much room remains and enforces thresholds at multiple points in the tick loop to prevent out-of-memory crashes. When an agent is killed by pressure, an inline recovery protocol extracts its findings before the pool continues, using the `hardLimit` reserve as a parallel report budget.

> **See also.** This page documents the *budget model and enforcement mechanics*. For the policy hooks that act on pressure events (`onProduced`, `shouldExit`, `onRecovery`), see [Agent Policy](/under-the-hood/agent-policy). For picking `nCtx` / `softLimit` / KV quantization values for a given deployment, see [Deployment](/guides/deployment).

candidates.heading: Under the App protocol
candidates.text:
## Under the App protocol

Pressure is *per-context* (one `llama_context`, one `nCtx` budget), not per-App. Every enabled App's tools draw from the same KV budget once they're prefilled into agent branches. Two App-protocol specifics worth knowing:

- **App lifecycle teardown is independent of pressure.** Per-app detached scopes in the registry free the App's *external* allocations (corpus chunks, watchers, connection pools) via `ensure(...)` on scope halt — not via `pruneOnReturn` or pressure-driven recovery. The two systems handle different resources (KV cells vs. native/JS state) and run on different triggers (tick-loop budget vs. registry scope-exit).
- **Recon agents tighten the budget per-pool.** The reasoning.run `ReconPolicy` uses a stricter time/turn cap than the default research policy, since recon is a shallow probe rather than a deep investigation. Force-kill recovery follows the standard path: `recoverInline` with a `PREFLIGHT_RECOVER` prompt — the same recovery extraction mechanism documented below, just with a recon-shaped prompt.

The threshold-and-zones model on this page is the *substrate*; the App protocol doesn't change it, but App authors should know that protected-tool guard checks (`authGuard`) run before any pressure check on `Tool.execute`, and the SETTLE-phase pressure gate applies uniformly to results from any App's tool.

candidates.heading: The nudge mechanism
candidates.text:
## The nudge mechanism

Nudging is stateless — no escalation, no tracking of past nudges. When the agent is over budget and calls a non-terminal tool, the policy returns `nudge` with a message. The pool:

1. Serializes the message as a tool result: `{ "error": "..." }`
2. Prefills the error into the agent's branch
3. Resets the grammar so the agent generates a fresh response

The agent sees the error in its context and may call the terminal tool on its next turn. If it tries another non-terminal tool while still over budget, the policy nudges again. If headroom recovers (branches pruned, KV freed), the agent is no longer over budget and can continue working.

There is no "second offense" kill. The hard kill is `shouldExit`'s job — triggered by `pressure.critical` or time `hardLimit`, not by repeated nudges.

Nudge messages carry the model's remaining token budget — `pressure.remaining - pressure.hardLimit` — interpolated into the string. The model reads this and can self-cap its response length:

| Source | Message |
|--------|---------|
| KV pressure (PRODUCE) | "KV memory pressure — report your findings now within `{budget}` words." |
| Turn limit (PRODUCE) | "Turn limit reached — report your findings now within `{budget}` words." |
| Time budget (PRODUCE) | "Time limit reached — report your findings now within `{budget}` words." |
| SETTLE stall-break | "Tool result too large for remaining KV. Report your findings now within `{budget}` words." |
| Insufficient work | "You must use tools before submitting results." |
| Tool guard | The guard's custom `message` field |

The `{budget}` substituted into the message is derived as `floor((pressure.remaining - pressure.hardLimit) * 0.7 / 10) * 10` (minimum 10) — **words, not tokens**. Tokenizers vary across models but words are universal in training data, and the 0.7 ratio + floor-to-10 conservatively under-advertise the ceiling so the model stays within native decode headroom. Overshoot → `pressure.critical` fires → kill → recovery takes over with its own word budget inside the `hardLimit` reserve.

candidates.heading: Pressure-aware exploit/explore
candidates.text:
### Pressure-aware exploit/explore

The framework's `DefaultAgentPolicy` exposes an explore/exploit gate that flips reranker behaviour when KV runs short:

- **Explore mode** (default, headroom available). `SearchTool` ranks chunks against the *tool query* alone — agents discover bridging content adjacent to the answer even when the original query wouldn't surface it.
- **Exploit mode** (under pressure). `SearchTool` re-scores via `scoreRelevanceBatch` — `min(toolQueryScore, originalQueryScore)` — tightening focus at the cost of serendipitous discovery.

The agent doesn't know which mode it's in; the policy decides and the tool obeys. [KV pressure](/under-the-hood/kv-pressure) walks the budget model.

---

candidates.heading: 3. PRODUCE soft-cut + budget nudge
candidates.text:
### 3. PRODUCE soft-cut + budget nudge

When an agent hits a stop token and the parsed output contains a non-terminal tool call, the policy checks whether the call should be allowed:

```typescript
const overBudget = (a.turns >= maxTurns || pressure.headroom < 0)
  && (!terminalToolName || tc.name !== terminalToolName);
```

If `headroom < 0` (over the soft limit) and the tool call is not the terminal tool, the policy nudges the agent with a message *carrying the remaining token budget*:

```
KV memory pressure — report your findings now within 1100 words.
Turn limit reached — report your findings now within 1100 words.
Time limit reached — report your findings now within 1100 words.
```

The token ceiling is `pressure.remaining - pressure.hardLimit`, converted to a conservative word count (`floor(tokens * 0.7 / 10) * 10`, minimum 10) before being embedded in the message. **Words, not tokens**, because tokenizers vary across models but words are universal in training data — and under-advertising keeps the model within native decode headroom even if its word-to-token ratio differs from the 0.7 assumption. If the agent overshoots, `pressure.critical` fires on the next tick and recovery takes over (consuming the `hardLimit` reserve). If the agent obeys, the voluntary return lands cleanly.

Terminal tools always pass through without nudging — an agent that wants to report should never be blocked.

<Tip>
Model behavior varies: some small models honor the token budget closely, others produce verbose prose before their tool call. Both paths are covered. Voluntary report within budget → clean finish. Overshoot → kill + recovery report in the reserved cells. Either way, findings land on the spine. The budget-aware nudge just makes the happy path more likely.
</Tip>

candidates.heading: onRecovery — recovery extraction
candidates.text:
## `onRecovery` — recovery extraction

Called inline when the trailing stop kills an agent during PRODUCE (or at pool close for agents that ended idle without result). The policy decides whether to extract findings, and renders its extraction prompt with the live pressure snapshot so the model knows its budget.

```typescript
type RecoveryAction =
  | { type: 'extract'; prompt: { system: string; user: string } }
  | { type: 'skip' };

onRecovery?(agent: Agent, pressure: ContextPressure): RecoveryAction;
```

`DefaultAgentPolicy` checks the confabulation guard before returning `extract`:
- `agent.tokenCount >= minTokens` (default 100) — agent generated enough context
- `agent.toolCallCount >= minToolCalls` (default 2) — agent did real work

Agents that barely ran would produce fabricated findings — the guard prevents this.

**Budget-aware prompt rendering.** The policy computes `budget = max(50, pressure.remaining - prefillOverhead - batchBuffer)` and renders the prompt's eta template with that value. Prompt authors use `<%= it.budget %>` where they want the budget surfaced:

```eta
You are a research reporter. The agent has been terminated due to budget constraints.

Call the report tool with the most important findings. Prioritize:
1. Direct quotes and specific evidence
2. Key conclusions with source URLs
3. Supporting context (only if budget allows)

IMPORTANT: You have approximately <%= it.budget %> words for your report. Be concise.
---
Report your findings.
```

Prompts with no eta tags render unchanged (backward compatible). Policies that author prompts via `DefaultAgentPolicyOpts.recovery.prompt` can use the full eta feature set (conditionals, loops) for richer templating.

The pool owns the extraction grammar (`{ "result": "..." }` JSON schema). The policy owns the prompt and the decision.

**Recovery diagnostics are always visible.** After every non-skip recovery attempt, the pool emits one of:

- `pool:recoveryReturn` — JSON parse succeeded, findings captured.
- `pool:recoveryFailed` — parse failed; event carries `reason` (`parse_error`, `no_result_field`, `scope_error`) + `outputExcerpt` (first 200 chars of what was generated) so ops can diagnose without re-running.

No silent recovery failures — every recovery prefill has a matching diagnostic.

<Tip>
Recovery runs inline on each pressure-kill using the `hardLimit` reserve as a parallel report budget. `pruneOnReturn: true` (default) maximizes sibling headroom after each recovered or voluntary return.
</Tip>

candidates.heading: Configure budget and recovery
Internal enterprise use
candidates.text:
### Internal enterprise use

- A bank runs HDK internally to power their intelligent search and document
  understanding for compliance use
- A government agency deploys HDK on-premise for sovereign AI use cases
- A research org uses HDK across hundreds of internal applications

candidates.heading:
title: "Recovery Extraction"
description: "Salvaging findings from agents killed by KV pressure, in-place on the agent's own branch."
candidates.text:
title: "Recovery Extraction"
description: "Salvaging findings from agents killed by KV pressure, in-place on the agent's own branch."
---

When an agent is killed by KV pressure (its tool results overshot the soft limit, its turn budget ran out, or the policy force-cut it), the pool runs **recovery extraction** before the tick loop continues. One agent is sacrificed per critical tick, its findings are extracted, its branch is pruned, and the freed KV lets remaining agents keep researching.

Recovery is **in-place**: no fork. The agent's full KV context (system prompt, tool results, reasoning from prior turns) is already in the cache. An extraction prompt is prefilled directly onto the dying agent's own branch, eager grammar constrains output to `{"result": "..."}`, and a single-agent produce/commit loop generates the report.

candidates.heading: 2. scoreRelevanceBatch — dual gate under pressure
candidates.text:
### 2. `scoreRelevanceBatch` — dual gate under pressure

Used in **exploit mode** when KV pressure tightens focus. Scores each text twice — against the per-call tool query *and* against the original query — and returns `min(toolQueryScore, originalQueryScore)`. A chunk has to clear both bars.

**Fires at:** inside the tool (e.g. `SearchTool.execute`) when `ToolContext.scorer` is set and `explore` is `false`. The pool decides which mode the tool runs in based on `KV-pressure`; the tool obeys.

candidates.heading: Agent Policy
candidates.text:
## Agent Policy

The pool consults an **AgentPolicy** at every decision boundary — when an agent hits a stop token, when deciding whether to kill under pressure, and when checking tool guards before dispatch.

```typescript
interface AgentPolicy {
  onProduced(agent, parsed, pressure, config): ProduceAction;
  shouldExit?(agent, pressure): boolean;    // trailing stop kill gate
  shouldExplore?(agent, pressure): boolean; // optional — defaults to true
  onRecovery?(agent): RecoveryAction;       // scratchpad extraction
  resetTick?(): void;                       // per-tick flag reset
}
```

`DefaultAgentPolicy` handles all the default behavior:

- **Terminal tool interception** — when the agent calls `report()`, extract result and mark idle
- **Budget enforcement** — deny non-terminal tool calls when over budget, nudge the agent to report
- **Confabulation prevention** — reject premature reports from agents with fewer than 2 tool calls
- **Tool guard checking** — run declarative guards before dispatching any tool call
- **Explore/exploit switching** — decide per-DISPATCH whether content-boundary tools should explore (agent-local scoring) or exploit (dual scoring against original query)

Customize thresholds and guards via the constructor:

```typescript
import { DefaultAgentPolicy } from "@lloyal-labs/lloyal-agents";

const policy = new DefaultAgentPolicy({
  minToolCallsBeforeReturn: 3,
  extraGuards: [myCustomGuard],
  exploreThreshold: 40,
  budget: { context: { softLimit: 2048 }, time: { softLimit: 600_000 } },
  recovery: { prompt: REPORT_PROMPT },
});

const pool = yield* agentPool({
  orchestrate: parallel(specs),  // each spec carries its own systemPrompt
  tools,
  policy,
});
```

The policy sees the agent's full state: status, tool history, result, nudge count, pressure snapshot. It returns an action — `tool_call`, `report`, `nudge`, or `idle` — and the pool executes it.

candidates.heading: Explore vs exploit
candidates.text:
## Explore vs exploit

The pool tells the tool which mode to score in via `ToolContext.explore`:

- **Explore (default, headroom available).** `SearchTool` scores chunks against the **tool query** alone — agents discover bridging content adjacent to the answer even when the original query wouldn't surface it.
- **Exploit (under pressure).** `SearchTool` calls `context.scorer.scoreRelevanceBatch` — `min(toolQueryScore, originalQueryScore)` — tightening focus at the cost of serendipitous discovery.

The agent never knows which mode it's in; `DefaultAgentPolicy.shouldExplore` decides per dispatch. See [Agent policy](/under-the-hood/agent-policy) for the threshold and [KV pressure](/under-the-hood/kv-pressure) for what counts as "under pressure."

candidates.heading: KV Cache
candidates.text:
### KV Cache

Inner and outer agents share a single `BranchStore` and a single KV cache. `ContextPressure` snapshots `remaining = nCtx - cellsUsed` on every tick. Inner agent prefills consume the same budget as outer agent prefills. When the inner pool runs, it reduces headroom for the outer pool's remaining agents.

KV pressure governs agent lifecycle at four points:

1. **Prefill gate** — at pool setup, the pool computes the total suffix cost (formatted chat tokens) for all agents. If the total exceeds headroom, agents are dropped from the end until the remaining suffixes fit. Dropped agents are marked `done` before generating a single token. This is the primary mechanism that limits sub-agent count in nested pools — outer agents' live branches consume headroom that the inner pool's prefill gate measures against.

2. **PRODUCE hard-cut** — agents are killed before `produceSync()` when remaining drops below `hardLimit`. The kill reserves the `hardLimit` cells for `recoverInline` to extract findings via the eager-report grammar. Terminal-tool protection (`agent.currentTool === terminalToolName`) yields to critical — mid-report agents get killed if they push past the threshold, and recovery takes over with a budget-aware prompt.

3. **PRODUCE soft-cut** — non-terminal tool calls are denied when headroom is negative. Terminal tools (e.g. `report`) always pass.

4. **SETTLE deferral** — tool results that would cross the `softLimit` floor are deferred to the next tick, giving siblings a chance to free KV (parallel) or the spine to accept the next turn (chain). If `deferred` items have no active siblings, the stall-breaker consults `policy.onSettleReject` — which returns a budget-aware nudge (replacing the oversized result) or idle (drop + recovery). Distinct drop reasons: `pressure_settle_reject` (policy-driven) vs `settle_stall_break` (hook absent).

candidates.heading: Time budget scope
Context pressure tuning
candidates.text:
## Context pressure tuning

Context pressure controls when agents are shut down as KV fills. Two thresholds:

```typescript
const researchPolicy = new DefaultAgentPolicy({
  budget: { context: { softLimit: 1024, hardLimit: 128 } },
  recovery: { prompt: REPORT },
});
```

**softLimit** (default 1024) -- remaining KV floor for new work. When remaining tokens drop below this:
- SETTLE rejects tool results (primary enforcement point)
- PRODUCE nudges agents to report (terminal tools like `report` still pass)
- INIT drops agents that don't fit during setup

Set this to account for downstream work. If synthesis needs ~1000 tokens of headroom, set research pool softLimit to at least 1024.

**hardLimit** (default 128) -- crash-prevention floor. When remaining drops below this, `shouldExit` kills agents immediately before `produceSync()`. This is a safety net, not a tuning knob.

For reporter sub-pools that only make one terminal tool call, use the default policy (softLimit 1024, hardLimit 128).

candidates.heading: Branch as the foundation
candidates.text:
## Branch as the foundation

Everything in the agent framework builds on Branch:

- **Prefix sharing**: `withSpine` creates the spine, prefills the shared prompt, agents fork from it. See [Prefix Sharing](/under-the-hood/prefix-sharing).
- **Agent pools**: Each agent is a forked branch. The tick loop calls `produceSync()` and `store.commit()` on branch arrays. See [Concurrency Model](/under-the-hood/concurrency).
- **KV pressure**: `ContextPressure` reads `cellsUsed` which is incremented by branch decode operations. See [KV Pressure](/under-the-hood/kv-pressure).
- **Recovery extraction**: when an agent is killed by KV pressure, `recoverInline` prefills a nudge onto the agent's own branch, swaps to eager grammar, generates, and prunes. See [Recovery Extraction](/under-the-hood/recovery-extraction).
- **Grammar constraining**: `setGrammar()` and `setGrammarLazy()` are branch methods. Grammar state is cloned on fork. See [Grammar & Tool Ordering](/under-the-hood/grammar-and-ordering).

The produce/commit separation, O(1) fork, and scope-tree cleanup are the primitives that make multi-agent generation on shared GPU compute possible.

candidates.heading: shouldExit — pre-generate kill gate
candidates.text:
## `shouldExit` — pre-generate kill gate

Called before `produceSync()` — before the agent generates any tokens. Returns `true` to kill the agent immediately. There's no nudge possible here — there's no tool call to attach an error message to. The branch stays alive for recovery via `onRecovery`.

```typescript
// PRODUCE: before each agent generates
if (policy.shouldExit?.(agent, pressure) ?? pressure.critical) {
  // transition to idle, eligible for recovery
}
```

Optional — defaults to `pressure.critical` when absent. `DefaultAgentPolicy` also checks the time budget:

- `pressure.critical` (remaining < hardLimit) → exit
- `budget.time.hardLimit` exceeded → exit
- **Terminal-tool protection (narrowed to the graceful zone)**: if `terminalToolName` is configured, `agent.currentTool` matches, AND `!pressure.critical`, `shouldExit` returns `false`. An agent actively generating its report is protected from arbitrary kills (time limits, custom policy decisions). Once `pressure.critical` fires, protection yields — the kill proceeds so `recoverInline` can extract findings before native decode OOMs. This narrowing prevents runaway free-text-into-terminal-tool patterns from exhausting the context.

`agent.currentTool` is populated by `Agent.observe(ctx)`, which runs a partial parse (`parseChatOutput` with `isPartial: true`) on each generated token until the tool name is detected. The partial parser is format-agnostic — it uses the same PEG parser that handles the full output at `isStop`, just in lenient mode.

<Note>
The pool validates `hardLimit >= nBatch` at startup. If `policy.pressureThresholds.hardLimit` is smaller than the context's `nBatch` (the native batch allocation size, default 512), `useAgentPool` throws with `Invariant Violation: hardLimit (N) must be >= nBatch (M)`. The reserve below `hardLimit` is recovery's parallel report budget — smaller than `nBatch` means the first recovery decode can't allocate its batch.
</Note>

candidates.heading: You never manually prune branches
Budget-aware recovery prompt
candidates.text:
### Budget-aware recovery prompt

The recovery prompt is an eta template. `DefaultAgentPolicy.onRecovery` renders it with the current `budget` before returning to the pool. A minimal recovery prompt:

```eta
You are a research reporter. The agent has been terminated due to budget
constraints and must deliver findings now.

Call the report tool with the most important findings from the research above.
Prioritize in this order:
1. Direct quotes and specific evidence that answer the task
2. Key conclusions with source URLs / line references
3. Supporting context (only if budget allows)

IMPORTANT: You have approximately <%= it.budget %> words for your report. Be concise —
if you exceed the budget your report will be cut off.
---
Report your findings.
```

The `<%= it.budget %>` tag is substituted at `onRecovery` call time with the numeric budget. Prompts that don't use the tag render identically (backward compatible).

candidates.heading: Hardware footprint
candidates.text:
## Hardware footprint

liblloyal isn't infinitely portable — it needs four things from the host:

| Requirement      | Floor for a working harness                                                                                                                              |
| ---------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| RAM              | ~3 GB resident for a 4B-class model at Q4_K_M, plus ~600 MB for a small reranker, plus KV cache. 8 GB total system RAM is comfortable.                   |
| Compute backend  | GPU recommended (Metal / CUDA / Vulkan / ROCm). CPU works for tiny models or as fallback. Pure MCU-class embedded (no accelerator, no spare RAM) is out. |
| Storage          | ~3 GB for weights of a 4B-class model at Q4; varies with model size.                                                                                     |
| Binary footprint | ~150–300 MB of compiled liblloyal + binding code, depending on which compute backends are statically linked.                                             |

A Raspberry Pi 5–class single-board computer, a phone, a laptop, or a server is in scope. Microcontrollers (Cortex-M, ESP32) are not — too little memory and no inference accelerator.

**Quant floor that production RIG harnesses target today.** A reference research pipeline — three concurrent agents, web + corpus retrieval, 32K context — runs comfortably on a 16 GB consumer laptop with:

- **Weights**: any 4B-class instruction-tuned GGUF that llama.cpp loads — **Qwen 3.5** is our recommended default; Llama, Gemma, Mistral, and your own fine-tunes also work — at **`Q4_K_M`** (~2.5–3 GB on disk, ~3 GB resident). The model is a configuration choice, not a constraint.
- **KV cache**: `typeK: "q4_0"`, `typeV: "q4_0"` — Q4-quantized, **not** FP16. FP16 KV would 4× the cache footprint; Q4 KV is the difference between fitting a 32K window on consumer hardware and not.
- **Reranker**: a small cross-encoder reranker GGUF — typically 0.5–0.6B parameters at Q8_0 (~600 MB resident).
- **Bootstrap**: `nCtx: 32768` on a single `llama_context`.

Q4_K_M weights + Q4_0 KV at the 4B class is the pragmatic floor. Quality holds — modern instruction-tuned 4B GGUFs are well-behaved at Q4 — throughput is interactive, and the whole stack (model + reranker + KV + your code) fits in well under 8 GB total resident.

Architecture isn't transformer-bound either: SSM and recurrent models (Mamba, Jamba, RWKV) are first-class via llama.cpp, and inside the HDK's continuous-context model we've observed them outperform attention-only transformers — fixed-size recurrent state composes through fork-and-extend better than a KV cache that grows linearly with sequence length.
