candidates.heading:
title: "Deployment"
description: "Production tuning checklist — model choice, nCtx, KV quantization, policy thresholds. Actionable recipes that reference the underlying mechanics."
candidates.text:
title: "Deployment"
description: "Production tuning checklist — model choice, nCtx, KV quantization, policy thresholds. Actionable recipes that reference the underlying mechanics."
---

This guide covers model selection, context sizing, KV quantization, and runtime configuration for deploying a lloyal-agents pipeline.

> **See also.** This guide is the *tuning checklist*. For the policy interface (the hooks you're tuning), see [Agent Policy](/under-the-hood/agent-policy). For why the pressure thresholds matter (the budget model), see [KV Pressure](/under-the-hood/kv-pressure).

candidates.heading:
title: "Recovery Extraction"
description: "Salvaging findings from agents killed by KV pressure, in-place on the agent's own branch."
candidates.text:
title: "Recovery Extraction"
description: "Salvaging findings from agents killed by KV pressure, in-place on the agent's own branch."
---

When an agent is killed by KV pressure (its tool results overshot the soft limit, its turn budget ran out, or the policy force-cut it), the pool runs **recovery extraction** before the tick loop continues. One agent is sacrificed per critical tick, its findings are extracted, its branch is pruned, and the freed KV lets remaining agents keep researching.

Recovery is **in-place**: no fork. The agent's full KV context (system prompt, tool results, reasoning from prior turns) is already in the cache. An extraction prompt is prefilled directly onto the dying agent's own branch, eager grammar constrains output to `{"result": "..."}`, and a single-agent produce/commit loop generates the report.

candidates.heading: "What about follow-up questions?" — session
"What about sub-agents?" — parent
candidates.text:
#### "What about sub-agents?" — `parent`

For warm-path forking: `agentPool({ parent: context.branch })`. The new pool's root forks from the calling agent's branch instead of cold-starting. Sub-agents inherit the parent's full attention state — every tool call, every result, every token. `DelegateTool` from `@lloyal-labs/rig` uses this internally; you'd reach for it directly when threading roots between harness stages.

Precedence: `parent ?? session?.trunk ?? cold-start`.

candidates.heading: Session: state across turns
candidates.text:
## Session: state across turns

When the pipeline produces an answer, commit it to the Session so subsequent queries inherit the conversation context:

```typescript
yield* call(() => session.commitTurn(query, answer));
// Next agent/agentPool with `session` forks from the trunk
```

See [Sessions](/build-a-harness/sessions) for the full warm/cold lifecycle.

candidates.heading:
title: Sessions
description: Trunk lifecycle, promotion, and multi-turn conversation deltas.
candidates.text:
title: Sessions
description: Trunk lifecycle, promotion, and multi-turn conversation deltas.
---


A session owns the current conversation state. It tracks the trunk branch — the ongoing conversation — and provides methods to advance it.

```typescript
import { Session } from "@lloyal-labs/sdk";

const session = new Session({ ctx, store });
```

That's it. A session starts with no trunk. The first query creates one.

candidates.heading: Warm path via session
How they compose
candidates.text:
### How they compose

Within a task, the KV spine operates through delegation — sub-agents inherit the calling agent's KV state. Between tasks, `extendSpine` extends `querySpine` with tokenized user+assistant turns. The synthesis pool forks from the fully-extended `querySpine` and attends over the complete research chain. After synthesis, `querySpine` is pruned (scope exit frees all intermediate KV). Only the final Q&A persists on the session trunk via `session.commitTurn`.

Within-query spine compounds evidence task-by-task via KV share. Cross-query spine stays minimal (one Q&A turn per query on the trunk).

candidates.heading: Multi-turn flow
Where to put the spine: harness withSpine vs agentPool({ systemPrompt })
The building blocks
candidates.text:
## The building blocks

Three primitives implement the work inside a stage:

<CardGroup cols={3}>
  <Card title="agent()" icon="wand-magic-sparkles">
    Single agent. Plan decomposition (with `schema`), JSON extraction, evaluation. Returns an Agent with result and raw output.
  </Card>
  <Card title="agentPool()" icon="users">
    Multi-agent pool with tools and an `orchestrate` argument that picks how the pool's agents relate (parallel / chain / fanout / dag).
  </Card>
  <Card title="diverge()" icon="code-branch">
    Multi-branch perplexity selection. Fork N attempts, generate in parallel, select the lowest-perplexity output. Verification and confidence checking.
  </Card>
</CardGroup>

`reduce()` is a stage-level combinator (covered in [Multi-source pipelines](#multi-source-pipelines-with-reduce) below) — it threads accumulated state across stages rather than describing relationships within one pool.

candidates.heading: The trunk
candidates.text:
## The trunk

The trunk is the branch that holds the current conversation. It contains every token the model has seen — system prompt, user messages, assistant responses, tool results.

```typescript
session.trunk  // Branch | null
```

- **`null`** — no conversation yet (cold start)
- **`Branch`** — active conversation with tokens in KV cache (warm)

<Note>
Cold and warm are physical properties of KV cache state, not modes. A cold session has no tokens in GPU memory. A warm session has prior turns physically present in the KV cache. The model recalls the conversation because those tokens are there.
</Note>

candidates.heading: Result propagation
candidates.text:
### Result propagation

When the inner pool completes, `agentPool` exits its scope — inner branches are pruned via their `ensure()` callbacks. The inner `withSpine` exits — the inner spine is pruned via `try/finally`. Control returns to the tool's `execute()` generator, which returns the result. Back in DISPATCH, the result is JSON-stringified, wrapped in a tool result delta, and pushed to `settledBuffer`. On the next tick's SETTLE phase, the outer agent's branch is prefilled with the tool result, grammar state resets, and the outer agent resumes generating.

candidates.heading: The protected bit
How dispatch fails closed
candidates.text:
### How dispatch fails closed

When the model emits a tool call for a protected tool the session hasn't granted:

1. The pool's DISPATCH phase sees `tool.protected === true`.
2. `authGuard` reads `GrantStoreCtx.expect()` and calls `has("send_email")`.
3. `has` returns `false` → dispatch denies the call.
4. The pool emits `tool:authReject` (carrying the agent's `walkAncestors` lineage so the trace records *which* agent attempted *which* tool from *which* lineage), then *nudges* the agent — the agent sees an error string at the call position and can recover (try a different tool, report what's missing).

The model never executes the action. The session never produces the side effect. The user is never billed for an email they didn't authorize.

candidates.heading: Debugging workflow
candidates.text:
## Debugging workflow

A systematic approach for investigating unexpected behavior:

1. **Run with `--output-dir`** to produce a JSONL trace file

2. **Check pool:open events** for initial pressure state and agent count

3. **Check pool:agentDrop events** for premature agent kills

4. **Check agent:turn events** for tool call patterns -- are agents using tools? Are they alternating between search/read/grep (depth-first) or repeating the same tool (scatter)?

5. **Check tool:result events** for `prefillTokenCount` -- are tool results unusually large?

6. **Check prompt:format events** for correct `taskContent` and `tools` -- is the model seeing the right context?

7. **Check pool:close events** for aggregate statistics -- how many agents produced results? What was the total token count?

For comparing two runs (e.g., before and after a change), extract the tool call sequences and compare:

```bash
# Extract tool dispatch sequence per agent
grep '"type":"tool:dispatch"' trace.jsonl | \
  jq -r '[.agentId, .tool, (.toolIndex|tostring)] | join("\t")'
```

This shows whether agents are following the expected investigation pattern (grep -> search -> read -> return-to-grep -> report) or degenerating into shallow patterns (grep -> report).

candidates.heading: Writing a custom policy
The interface
Publishing — the App author flow
candidates.text:
### Publishing — the App author flow

```bash
harness.dev publish   # auth → build → submit to apps.lloyal.ai for review + signing
```

The CLI:

1. Authenticates the publisher against `apps.lloyal.ai`. Two modes: an interactive OAuth flow when run from a developer terminal (short-lived session, no persistent credential on disk), or long-lived service credentials provisioned by Lloyal during publisher onboarding for use in CI environments (read from environment variables, not from disk).
2. esbuilds the App into a single ESM bundle with `@lloyal-labs/*` as externals.
3. Submits the bundle bytes + an unsigned manifest stub (name, version, entry, sizeBytes, peerDependencies) to the apps.lloyal.ai publish endpoint.

The platform side then:

4. Authenticates the publisher token and runs review checks — bundle source conformance, framework-version compatibility, `skill.eta` sanitization, `protected` tool surfacing.
5. Signs the bundle bytes with the Lloyal platform Ed25519 key (the same key listed in `CHANNEL_TRUST_ROOTS`), and writes the bundle + the signed `AppBundleManifest` to R2.
6. Re-signs `catalog.json` (adding/updating the new entry) with the platform key and atomically replaces `https://apps.lloyal.ai/v1/catalog.json`.

There is no "self-hosted catalog" option, and no per-publisher signing keys: publishers authenticate to submit, but every signature on every byte in the catalog comes from the Lloyal platform key after Lloyal's review. The signature on a bundle is the cryptographic record of that review.

candidates.heading: session.prefillToolResult(resultStr, callId)
candidates.text:
### `session.prefillToolResult(resultStr, callId)`

Append a tool result turn to the trunk. Used when running tools outside the agent pool (e.g. single-agent interactive mode).

```typescript
await session.prefillToolResult(
  JSON.stringify({ results: searchResults }),
  "call_123"
);
```

candidates.heading: Agent Policy
candidates.text:
## Agent Policy

The pool consults an **AgentPolicy** at every decision boundary — when an agent hits a stop token, when deciding whether to kill under pressure, and when checking tool guards before dispatch.

```typescript
interface AgentPolicy {
  onProduced(agent, parsed, pressure, config): ProduceAction;
  shouldExit?(agent, pressure): boolean;    // trailing stop kill gate
  shouldExplore?(agent, pressure): boolean; // optional — defaults to true
  onRecovery?(agent): RecoveryAction;       // scratchpad extraction
  resetTick?(): void;                       // per-tick flag reset
}
```

`DefaultAgentPolicy` handles all the default behavior:

- **Terminal tool interception** — when the agent calls `report()`, extract result and mark idle
- **Budget enforcement** — deny non-terminal tool calls when over budget, nudge the agent to report
- **Confabulation prevention** — reject premature reports from agents with fewer than 2 tool calls
- **Tool guard checking** — run declarative guards before dispatching any tool call
- **Explore/exploit switching** — decide per-DISPATCH whether content-boundary tools should explore (agent-local scoring) or exploit (dual scoring against original query)

Customize thresholds and guards via the constructor:

```typescript
import { DefaultAgentPolicy } from "@lloyal-labs/lloyal-agents";

const policy = new DefaultAgentPolicy({
  minToolCallsBeforeReturn: 3,
  extraGuards: [myCustomGuard],
  exploreThreshold: 40,
  budget: { context: { softLimit: 2048 }, time: { softLimit: 600_000 } },
  recovery: { prompt: REPORT_PROMPT },
});

const pool = yield* agentPool({
  orchestrate: parallel(specs),  // each spec carries its own systemPrompt
  tools,
  policy,
});
```

The policy sees the agent's full state: status, tool history, result, nudge count, pressure snapshot. It returns an action — `tool_call`, `report`, `nudge`, or `idle` — and the pool executes it.

candidates.heading: Under the App protocol
candidates.text:
## Under the App protocol

The tick loop is the substrate every App's tools execute on. Two App-protocol interactions to know up front:

- **The spine is rendered from the App registry.** Pool setup calls `renderSpine({ apps: registry.enabled() })` and the resulting catalog + tool schemas decode into the spine branch once. The enabled-App set at the moment of pool creation is what every spawned agent inherits via prefix-share; mid-pool registry changes don't propagate.
- **DISPATCH gates protected tools via `authGuard` before `Tool.execute`.** Phase 4 looks up the tool by name, checks `tool.protected` against the session's `GrantStoreCtx`, denies if no grant exists (emits `tool:authReject` with agent lineage), and only then calls `execute`. Open tools (the default) bypass the check. See [App security](/build-an-app/app-security).
- **Per-app scope lifecycles are independent of the pool.** The registry runs each App's `AppFactory` in a detached Effection scope. App teardown via `ensure(...)` fires on registry scope exit, not on pool exit — so an App's external allocations survive across many pool invocations within a single session.

The five-phase tick mechanics below describe the per-pool execution loop. The App protocol sits on top of it; it doesn't change the phase ordering, but it gates one of the dispatch checks and supplies the spine content.

candidates.heading: Event Channel
candidates.text:
### Event Channel

Inner agent events flow through the same `Events` channel. The TUI or event collector sees `agent:spawn`, `agent:produce`, `agent:tool_call`, `agent:tool_result`, `agent:done` from both outer and inner agents. The `parentAgentId` field on `agent:spawn` distinguishes inner from outer — inner agents have a `parentAgentId` that matches an inner pool's root branch handle, not the outer spine.

candidates.heading: resetTick — per-tick state reset
candidates.text:
## `resetTick` — per-tick state reset

Called by the pool at the start of each tick iteration. Resets per-tick flags used by the trailing stop:

- `_killedThisTick`: At most one agent killed per critical tick
- `_nudgedThisTick`: At most one agent nudged per over-budget tick

Optional — only needed if the policy tracks per-tick state. `DefaultAgentPolicy` implements this.

candidates.heading: Events
Lifecycle
candidates.text:
## Lifecycle

<Steps>
  <Step title="Cold start — first query">
    No trunk exists. Run your pipeline. Commit the turn to create the trunk.

    ```typescript
    const answer = yield* research(query);
    yield* call(() => session.commitTurn(query, answer));
    // session.trunk now exists — cold → warm
    ```
  </Step>

  <Step title="Warm continuation — follow-up queries">
    Trunk exists. Pipeline stages that receive `session` fork from the trunk — the model sees full conversation history. After producing an answer, commit again.

    ```typescript
    const answer = yield* research(query);
    yield* call(() => session.commitTurn(query, answer));
    // Trunk extended with the new turn
    ```
  </Step>

  <Step title="Dispose">
    When the conversation ends, dispose the session to prune the trunk.

    ```typescript
    await session.dispose();
    // session.trunk is now null, KV cells freed
    ```
  </Step>
</Steps>

<Note>
`commitTurn` handles warm and cold internally. Cold: creates a branch at position 0, prefills the formatted turn, promotes to trunk. Warm: appends a turn separator + formatted delta to the existing trunk. You don't branch between the two — one call covers both.
</Note>

candidates.heading: Teardown
candidates.text:
### Teardown

Effection defines [three ways an operation exits][scope]: return (completes with a value), error (fails with an exception), or halt (stopped by a parent or related operation). In all three cases, child operations are torn down and cleanup runs.

**Normal completion.** Inner pool finishes → `scoped()` exits → inner branches pruned → inner spine pruned → tool returns → result pushed to `settledBuffer` → SETTLE prefills outer agent → outer pool eventually finishes → outer branches pruned → outer spine pruned.

**Error in inner pool.** Inner pool errors → `scoped()` error boundary catches it → inner scope tears down → outer agent marked `done` with `findings: "Tool error: ..."` → outer pool continues with remaining agents.

**Outer scope cancelled.** Parent of the outer pool exits → outer pool's resource scope tears down → `scoped()` child is halted → inner pool halted → all inner branches pruned. No dangling branches. No orphaned GPU state.

candidates.heading: Recursive Agents
candidates.text:
## Recursive Agents

Tools can spawn sub-agents. A tool's `execute()` method returns an Effection [`Operation<unknown>`][ops], which means it can [`yield*`][ops] into any framework primitive — `agentPool`, `useAgentPool`, `agent`, `diverge`. The inner pipeline shares the same GPU compute, KV cache, and event channel as the outer pool. No separate process. No serialization boundary.

candidates.heading: 1. Prefill gate (ctx.spawn)
Recursive agents (delegation)
useAgent and useAgentPool
Parallel agents: agentPool
Parameters
candidates.text:
## Parameters

| Marker | Value at instantiation |
|---|---|
| `\<Year>` | Year of release (e.g. `2026`) |
| `\<Licensor>` | The party offering the Software (`Lloyal Labs`) |
| `\<Software>` | The name of the software (`liblloyal`, `lloyal-node`, or the lloyal-sdk package name) |
| `\<Change Date>` | Two years from the version's release date (e.g. release `2026-06-01` → Change Date `2028-06-01`). **Per-release**: every new published version sets its own Change Date at release time. Do not use a single global Change Date across versions. |

candidates.heading: Register a publisher handle
candidates.text:
## Register a publisher handle

```bash
npx harness.dev publishers register --handle <handle> [--yes]
```

`<handle>` matches the grammar `[a-z][a-z0-9_-]{1,63}` (see [Scaffolding > Identifier grammar](/cli/scaffolding#identifier-grammar)).

**Example:**

```bash
npx harness.dev publishers register --handle acme

# Publisher ToS (v1): https://docs.lloyal.ai/licensing/publisher-tos
#
# By registering you attest that you have read and accept the publisher Terms of Service.
#
# Accept the publisher ToS? [y/N] y
# registered as acme
#   tosVersion: v1
#   registeredAt: 2026-06-08T02:25:37.976Z
```

Pass `--yes` to skip the interactive ToS prompt (still prints the link — required when running non-interactively):

```bash
npx harness.dev publishers register --handle acme --yes
```

candidates.heading: Retrieval calibration: softmax → logit-diff (regression + fix)
candidates.text:
### Retrieval calibration: softmax → logit-diff (regression + fix)

A pathology investigation in reasoning.run (the "TLS handshake → HDK chunk scores 0.999" failure mode) traced miscalibration to the score formula itself:

- **Rerank.\_rerankScore: `P(yes | yes_or_no)` (softmax) → `logit(yes) − logit(no)` (logit-diff).** Range becomes unbounded; sign carries meaning (positive ⇒ relevant); magnitude carries confidence. Saturation eliminated (top-5 stddev rose ~0.05–0.5 → 2.5–6.5 on the calibration fixture).
- **`Source._entailmentFloor`: default `0.25` → `0`.** Now expressed in logit-diff space ("model leans yes"). Subclasses can tighten (`2` for "confident yes") or loosen (negative for sparse data).
- **NEW: `SearchTool` envelope** (`@lloyal-labs/corpus-app/tools/search.ts`). Returns `{ hits, thresholdScore, totalScored, topRejected }` instead of a raw top-K array. Sub-floor hits drop to `topRejected` only when nothing passes — gives the agent an honest "best I could find but the model said no" signal instead of feeding garbage as evidence.
- **Calibration probe shipped** at `reasoning.run/scripts/inspect-rerank.mjs` — median(relevant) − median(irrelevant) on the fixture moved `0.0453 → 3.66` (×80).
- **Unchanged:** the `EntailmentScorer` interface (4 methods) and the three-queries model (tool query / agent task / original query).

candidates.heading: The pattern
Why eager grammar, not lazy
candidates.text:
### Why eager grammar, not lazy

During normal generation, agents use lazy grammar with tool-call triggers — the model generates freely until `<tool_call>` fires, then the grammar constrains the tool call structure. Recovery uses **eager** grammar (`setGrammar`, not `setGrammarLazy`). The JSON schema grammar constrains from token 0: the model must produce `{"result":` followed by findings text. No tool calls possible, no model choice. This is the system extracting findings, not asking the model to report.

candidates.heading: Rate limits
candidates.text:
## Rate limits

| Limit | Window | Per |
|---|---|---|
| 10 publishes | 24 hours | Per authenticated identity |
| 1 publisher registration | (forever) | Per email |

Rate limits are tracked in Workers KV with a sliding-window counter. Exceeding returns `429` with a `Retry-After` header.

candidates.heading: Framework contexts seeded by the registry
candidates.text:
## Framework contexts seeded by the registry

When the registry runs a factory, it seeds these contexts into the App's detached scope so the factory can read them:

| context | what it carries |
| --- | --- |
| `AppConfigStoreCtx` | The harness's per-app config store — call `cfgStore.get(manifest.name)` for this App's config. |
| `AppRegistryCtx` | A reference back to the registry itself — useful for an App that needs to enumerate peers. |
| `RerankerCtx` | The shared reranker, if any — for Apps whose Source needs cross-encoder scoring. |
| `GrantStoreCtx` | The session's protected-tool grants — relevant if the App's tools need to query their own grant state (rare). |

These are Effection contexts (from `agents/src/context.ts`). Set on the App's scope, accessible via `.expect()`. They don't leak across Apps — each factory's scope sees only what the registry seeded.

candidates.heading: What gets captured
candidates.text:
## What gets captured

Every trace event has a `traceId`, `parentTraceId` (for building the tree), and `ts` (high-resolution timestamp).

| Event | What it captures |
|-------|-----------------|
| `scope:open/close` | Named scope boundaries with duration — pools, tools, spines |
| `prompt:format` | The exact prompt the model sees + the raw task content before formatting |
| `agent:turn` | Raw model output + parsed content + parsed tool calls per turn |
| `tool:dispatch` | Tool name, parsed args, **toolkit position** (`toolIndex/toolkitSize`) |
| `tool:result` | Full result object, prefill token count, duration |
| `pool:open/close` | Agent count, suffix token costs, pressure snapshot, per-agent findings |
| `pool:agentDrop` | Why an agent was killed: `pressure_init`, `pressure_critical`, `pressure_softcut`, `pressure_settle_reject` (stall-breaker), `maxTurns` |
| `generate:start/end` | Single-branch generation (plan, eval, recovery) with output |
| `branch:create/prefill/prune` | KV lifecycle with token counts and roles |
| `rerank:start/end` | Query, chunk count, top scored results |
| `entailment:search` | Steering-boundary scoring of web search results against original query |
| `entailment:delegate` | Delegation entailment gate — per-question scores and keep/filter decisions |
| `entailment:delegate:echo` | Echo detection — similarity scores against agent task, threshold, rejection |
| `entailment:content:exploit` | Exploit-mode dual scoring at content boundary — pressure snapshot, `toolQueryScore` vs `combinedScore` per chunk |

candidates.heading: Spine
candidates.text:
## Spine

The shared KV-cache prefix every Pool's agents fork from. The **Spine** holds the framework intro, the catalog of enabled Apps (their protocol names + `useWhen` + tools), and the tool-selection rule — decoded **once** and inherited by every forked agent via metadata-only fork. This is what makes N parallel agents cost roughly the wall-time of one.

- **Owns:** the system prompt, the tool schemas, the per-tick batched decode.
- **Go deeper:** [Continuous-context spine](/under-the-hood/continuous-context-spine) · [Prefix sharing](/under-the-hood/prefix-sharing)

candidates.heading:
title: Search strategy
description: How the reranker scores retrieved content — logit-diff formula, four scoring roles, three queries, where each method fires in the tick loop.
candidates.text:
title: Search strategy
description: How the reranker scores retrieved content — logit-diff formula, four scoring roles, three queries, where each method fires in the tick loop.
---

The reranker is RIG infrastructure, not a fetch optimization. The same cross-encoder model drives **four distinct scoring roles** across an agent's lifetime — content entailment, relevance under pressure, similarity for echo detection, and a floor gate that decides whether a result is worth surfacing at all. This page is the reference for the scoring shape, the score formula, and where each method fires in the tick loop.

If you're authoring an App, [Sources & retrieval](/build-an-app/sources-and-retrieval) is the higher-level walkthrough — the page you're reading is for harness authors, framework debuggers, and anyone designing custom retrieval flows.

candidates.heading: Cross-request multiplex
candidates.text:
## Cross-request multiplex

A single reranker context handles concurrent scoring via `Rerank._drain` and `_fillGroup`:

- Multiple `score()` calls enqueue into `_pending` and the drain loop pulls work via `_fillGroup`, packing across requests up to `nSeqMax` slots per GPU dispatch.
- Two concurrent `scoreBatch` calls on the same context race (`scoreBatch` bypasses the multiplex layer). `EntailmentScorer.scoreRelevanceBatch` uses `scoreBatch` internally — so callers must serialize relevance scoring per reranker context.

The agent pool's serial-DISPATCH invariant covers this today by ensuring one `tool.execute` runs at a time per pool. If you write a custom scoring path that lives outside DISPATCH, route through the iterator (`reranker.score(...)`) rather than `reranker.scoreBatch(...)` to stay multiplex-safe.

candidates.heading: The four scoring roles
The reranker — four scoring roles
v3.0 — rerank: position-read scoring, BM25 first stage
candidates.text:
## v3.0 — rerank: position-read scoring, BM25 first stage

`@lloyal-labs/sdk` `Rerank` restructure (verified score-preserving by the frozen-fixture eval pack — same-pair drift below 0.02 logits, rank order τ ≥ 0.99 vs the 2.x implementation):

- Leaf scoring reads logits at the final prompt position via `Branch` position reads (`ForkOpts.cloneLogits`, `BranchSampleError` on the sdk surface); per-leaf re-decode is gone.
- `Rerank.create(ctx, opts)` enforces exclusive decode ownership of its `SessionContext` and fires three fail-loud calibration gates (single-token labels, BPE-boundary drift, boot-canary ordering gap).
- Scores are log-odds of an absolute yes/no judgment: `sigmoid(score) = P(yes)`, floor `0 ≡ P 0.5`. Docstrings previously describing a "relative ranker" were corrected — see [Rerank architecture](/under-the-hood/rerank-architecture).
- `@lloyal-labs/corpus-app` 1.1.0 adds an Okapi-BM25 first stage over reranker-token ids (top-100 lexical gate ahead of the cross-encoder) and moves its TOC from per-spawn prompts to `Source.promptData()`.

candidates.heading: Related: rerank-architecture
candidates.text:
## Related: rerank-architecture

The cross-encoder reranker composes the same `Branch` + `BranchStore` primitives over a permanent warm trunk — same physics, different consumer. See [Rerank architecture](/under-the-hood/rerank-architecture) for the trunk / queryBranch / leaves topology and the BM25 first-stage that caps cross-encoder workload at top-K candidates.

candidates.heading:
title: Sources & retrieval
description: The Source abstract base, the reranker-backed EntailmentScorer, and how RIG inverts classical RAG.
candidates.text:
title: Sources & retrieval
description: The Source abstract base, the reranker-backed EntailmentScorer, and how RIG inverts classical RAG.
---

A **Source** is the data-access half of an App. It wraps a live system (a corpus, the web, a vector store, a database) and exposes the tools an agent uses to read it — plus the reranker-backed scorer that decides whether what came back is relevant.

This page is the deep dive on the `Source` abstract base, the four scoring roles of the cross-encoder reranker, and how Retrieval Interleaved Generation (**RIG**) puts retrieval *inside* the model's decode loop rather than before it.

candidates.heading: What gets captured
candidates.text:
## What gets captured

Every trace event has a `traceId`, `parentTraceId` (for building the tree), and `ts` (high-resolution timestamp).

| Event | What it captures |
|-------|-----------------|
| `scope:open/close` | Named scope boundaries with duration — pools, tools, spines |
| `prompt:format` | The exact prompt the model sees + the raw task content before formatting |
| `agent:turn` | Raw model output + parsed content + parsed tool calls per turn |
| `tool:dispatch` | Tool name, parsed args, **toolkit position** (`toolIndex/toolkitSize`) |
| `tool:result` | Full result object, prefill token count, duration |
| `pool:open/close` | Agent count, suffix token costs, pressure snapshot, per-agent findings |
| `pool:agentDrop` | Why an agent was killed: `pressure_init`, `pressure_critical`, `pressure_softcut`, `pressure_settle_reject` (stall-breaker), `maxTurns` |
| `generate:start/end` | Single-branch generation (plan, eval, recovery) with output |
| `branch:create/prefill/prune` | KV lifecycle with token counts and roles |
| `rerank:start/end` | Query, chunk count, top scored results |
| `entailment:search` | Steering-boundary scoring of web search results against original query |
| `entailment:delegate` | Delegation entailment gate — per-question scores and keep/filter decisions |
| `entailment:delegate:echo` | Echo detection — similarity scores against agent task, threshold, rejection |
| `entailment:content:exploit` | Exploit-mode dual scoring at content boundary — pressure snapshot, `toolQueryScore` vs `combinedScore` per chunk |

candidates.heading:
title: "Rerank Architecture"
description: "How the cross-encoder reranker composes Branch + BranchStore primitives over a warm trunk, and where BM25 first-stage fits."
candidates.text:
title: "Rerank Architecture"
description: "How the cross-encoder reranker composes Branch + BranchStore primitives over a warm trunk, and where BM25 first-stage fits."
---

Rerank is the cross-encoder that admits task-relevant context into every agent's KV cache. It's HDK's most load-bearing capability: every search tool result, every corpus probe, every coverage check goes through it. The architecture below is the post-3.0 shape — composed in the SDK over the same `Branch` + `BranchStore` primitives that power `useAgent`, `withSpine`, and `agentPool`. The framework's "agents are branches of a live KV cache" framing extends literally to rerank.

candidates.heading: Concurrency model
candidates.text:
## Concurrency model

Three layers of decode exist in HDK; only the third needs explicit handling in this layer:

| Decode caller | Concurrency control | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Agent-loop `store.commit(entries)` per tick | Architectural — one call carries all N agents | `decode_each` batches N branches into ONE `llama_decode` |
| Tool-decode (rerank/embed) from one agent's tool | Structural — agent blocks on tool result | No overlap with same agent's commit |
| Tool-decode from N parallel agents simultaneously | **SDK serializer (~10 LOC Promise chain)** | Prevents kernel-level concurrent decode on a single context |

**Rerank takes exclusive ownership of its SessionContext.** `createReranker` constructs a dedicated context; routing any other decode through it is undefined behavior — `llama_context::decode` carries no internal mutex. Enforcement is at construction: a `__decodeOwner` flag on the context refuses a second `Rerank.create()`. The flag is cleared by `dispose()` so test / REPL re-creation works.

Within a single Rerank instance, concurrent `score()` / `scoreBatch()` calls are serialized by a per-instance Promise chain. The kernel sees them in arrival order; consumers still get a concurrent-looking API.

candidates.heading: Three queries in play
candidates.text:
## Three queries in play

The four scoring methods aren't variants on one concept — they operate on three *distinct* queries the framework keeps separate by design:

| Concept | Scope | Field name in code |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Tool query** | Per tool call | `localQuery` param of `scoreRelevanceBatch` |
| **Agent task** | Per agent lifetime | `reference` param of `scoreSimilarityBatch` |
| **Original query** | Per research invocation | Captured in closure by `createScorer` |

Conflating these produces wrong scores. When you add a new scoring method or trace event, use the field names from this table.

candidates.heading: Where scoring happens
candidates.text:
## Where scoring happens

Scoring fires at three points in a pool's lifecycle, all inside the dispatch fiber:

1. **Search-time scoring (inside the tool).** `SearchTool.execute` calls `reranker.score(query, chunks)`. Hits below the threshold are dropped; the result envelope `{ hits, thresholdScore, totalScored, topRejected }` lets the agent see "best I could find, but the model said no to all of them" instead of getting fed garbage as honest signal.
2. **Boundary entailment at SETTLE.** When a tool result is prefilled back into the agent's KV, the framework optionally re-scores it against the *original query* via `scoreEntailmentBatch` — a content boundary check that catches off-topic drift after the per-tool scoring already passed.
3. **Echo detection at delegation.** When an agent forks a sub-agent, the parent's task is the `reference` and the candidate sub-task is compared via `scoreSimilarityBatch` — preventing the recursion from spinning on paraphrases of the same intent.

The single reranker context handles all three concurrently via its `_drain` + `_fillGroup` multiplexing — multiple requests in the pending queue get packed into shared GPU batches of `nSeqMax` slots.

---

candidates.heading: Retrieval calibration: softmax → logit-diff (regression + fix)
candidates.text:
### Retrieval calibration: softmax → logit-diff (regression + fix)

A pathology investigation in reasoning.run (the "TLS handshake → HDK chunk scores 0.999" failure mode) traced miscalibration to the score formula itself:

- **Rerank.\_rerankScore: `P(yes | yes_or_no)` (softmax) → `logit(yes) − logit(no)` (logit-diff).** Range becomes unbounded; sign carries meaning (positive ⇒ relevant); magnitude carries confidence. Saturation eliminated (top-5 stddev rose ~0.05–0.5 → 2.5–6.5 on the calibration fixture).
- **`Source._entailmentFloor`: default `0.25` → `0`.** Now expressed in logit-diff space ("model leans yes"). Subclasses can tighten (`2` for "confident yes") or loosen (negative for sparse data).
- **NEW: `SearchTool` envelope** (`@lloyal-labs/corpus-app/tools/search.ts`). Returns `{ hits, thresholdScore, totalScored, topRejected }` instead of a raw top-K array. Sub-floor hits drop to `topRejected` only when nothing passes — gives the agent an honest "best I could find but the model said no" signal instead of feeding garbage as evidence.
- **Calibration probe shipped** at `reasoning.run/scripts/inspect-rerank.mjs` — median(relevant) − median(irrelevant) on the fixture moved `0.0453 → 3.66` (×80).
- **Unchanged:** the `EntailmentScorer` interface (4 methods) and the three-queries model (tool query / agent task / original query).

candidates.heading: Reranker selection
candidates.text:
## Reranker selection

The reranker scores chunks against queries for post-research passage selection. It runs on a separate model context with its own `nCtx` and `nSeqMax`:

```typescript
import { createReranker } from '@lloyal-labs/rig/node';

const reranker = yield* call(() =>
  createReranker(rerankModelPath, { nSeqMax: 8, nCtx: 4096 }),
);
```

| Parameter | Default | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| `nSeqMax` | 8 | Parallel scoring slots. Higher = faster scoring of many chunks |
| `nCtx` | 4096 | Max tokens per chunk+query pair. 4096 is sufficient for most passages |

The reference pipeline uses `qwen3-reranker-0.6b` at Q4_K_M quantization (~400MB). The reranker is called at two points:
1. After each source's research phase (score source chunks against query)
2. Between sources (bridge reranking for discovery extraction)

Reranker memory is separate from the main model's KV cache. On a system with 16GB, the typical budget: ~4GB main model weights + ~2GB KV cache + ~400MB reranker weights + ~200MB reranker KV.

candidates.heading: Template
candidates.text:
## Template

> Copy everything below the divider into the repo's `LICENSE` file and
> substitute the four parameters above. Do not modify the operative text.

---

candidates.heading: The score formula: logit-diff, range unbounded
candidates.text:
## The score formula: logit-diff, range unbounded

The reranker returns `logit(yes) − logit(no)` per (query, document) pair — the raw logit difference, **not** a softmax probability. Sign carries meaning:

| score | interpretation |
| --- | --- |
| **positive** | the model would emit "yes" rather than "no" if forced — relevant |
| **zero** | indifferent |
| **negative** | "no" — irrelevant |
| **magnitude** | confidence |

`SearchTool` and `EntailmentScorer.shouldProceed` both threshold on this scale. The default floor on `Source._entailmentFloor` is `0` — a hit passes when the cross-encoder *leans yes*. Subclasses can tighten (`2` for "confident yes" in noisy corpora) or loosen (negative floors when sparse data means leaning-no hits are still worth investigating).

<Note>
The prior scoring form returned `P(yes | yes_or_no) ∈ (0,1)` via softmax over the yes/no logits, with a `0.25` default floor. The softmax compressed small logit gaps into extreme probabilities (gap of 5 → 0.993; 10 → 0.99995), saturating top-K ordering and erasing confidence differences in dense result sets. Calibration data (`reasoning.run/scripts/inspect-rerank.mjs`) showed:

- median(relevant) − median(irrelevant) jumped **0.0453 → 3.66** (×80)
- top-5 stddev per query **0.05–0.5 → 2.5–6.5** (saturation eliminated)
- on the canonical fixture, AUC moved from per-query 0.625-noise to consistent ≥0.85 for API-identifier queries

The logit-diff change is in `packages/sdk/src/Rerank.ts`; the floor recalibration is in `packages/agents/src/source.ts` (default `_entailmentFloor = 0`).
</Note>

candidates.heading: Why does the LICENSE list four narrow Permitted Purposes then?
candidates.text:
## Why does the LICENSE list four narrow Permitted Purposes then?

You may read the FSL LICENSE and see this section:

> Permitted Purposes specifically include using the Software:
> 1. for your internal use and access;
> 2. for non-commercial education;
> 3. for non-commercial research; and
> 4. in connection with professional services that you provide to a
>    Licensee using the Software in accordance with these Terms and
>    Conditions.

A careful first reading can mistake this list for the *exclusive* set of
permitted uses — leading to the (incorrect) conclusion that commercial
product distribution is prohibited.

It isn't. **The operative definition is broader.** Earlier in the same
section, the license states:

> A "Permitted Purpose" is any purpose other than a Competing Use.

The four enumerated items are **illustrative examples** added because those
specific cases are ones a careful reader might otherwise hesitate about
("is research permitted? is consulting permitted?"). The four items are
additive clarifications, not a closing of the open-ended definition.

Sentry, who authored FSL and uses it on their own software, [confirms this
explicitly in their FAQ](https://fsl.software):

> "You can do anything with FSL software except undermine its producer. You
> can run it for almost all purposes, study it, modify it, and distribute
> your changes…"

If the license felt restrictive on first read, that's a documented
[FSL adoption hazard](https://fair.io) — many developers hit the same wall.
The answer is to read the "any purpose other than a Competing Use" line as
the operative definition and treat the four enumerated items as examples,
not as a closed list.

candidates.heading:
title: "RIG Pipeline Reference"
description: "Reference architecture for retrieval-interleaved harnesses — Plan, Research, Bridge, Synthesize, Eval. Patterns to compose, not a fixed recipe."
candidates.text:
title: "RIG Pipeline Reference"
description: "Reference architecture for retrieval-interleaved harnesses — Plan, Research, Bridge, Synthesize, Eval. Patterns to compose, not a fixed recipe."
---

A RIG pipeline composes the HDK's primitives — `agentPool`, `useAgent`, `withSpine`, `Source`, `diverge` — into a research → synthesize → verify workflow. This reference describes the architectural patterns that consistently appear when building such pipelines, so you can pick the ones you need and skip the rest.

candidates.heading: Retrieval Interleaved Generation
candidates.text:
## Retrieval Interleaved Generation

Classical RAG is a *pre-processing* step: embed the query, run a similarity search, concatenate top chunks into the prompt, generate. One shot, static context, no feedback loop between what the model reads and what it asks for next.

**RIG inverts this.** Retrieval happens *inside* the generation loop. The agent emits a tool call → a `Source` returns chunks → a cross-encoder reranker scores them against the original query → results are prefilled into the agent's KV at the call position → the next decoded token attends over them and decides what to ask for next. Hypothesis-driven, not bag-of-keywords.

<Tabs>
  <Tab title="Classical RAG">
    <Steps>
      <Step title="Embed query">
        Query passed through an embedding model → dense vector.
      </Step>
      <Step title="ANN search">
        Vector similarity search over the index → top-K chunks by cosine distance.
      </Step>
      <Step title="Concatenate">
        Build prompt: `system + retrieved_chunks + query`.
      </Step>
      <Step title="Generate">
        One inference call. Done.
      </Step>
    </Steps>
    Static context. The model never re-shapes retrieval based on what it learned.
  </Tab>
  <Tab title="Retrieval Interleaved Generation">
    <Steps>
      <Step title="Agent generates">
        PRODUCE phase samples a tool call (`web_search`, `fetch_page`, `search`, custom).
      </Step>
      <Step title="Source returns chunks">
        The App's `Source` queries the backend (web, corpus, vector store, database).
      </Step>
      <Step title="Reranker scores">
        Cross-encoder scores chunks against the original query as a **logit-diff** (`logit(yes) − logit(no)`). Positive ⇒ relevant; the default floor is `0`.
      </Step>
      <Step title="Prefill at call position">
        SETTLE phase prefills the result into the agent's KV at the position where the call was made.
      </Step>
      <Step title="Attend and refine">
        Next PRODUCE attends over the prefilled result. The agent's next query is shaped by what it just read.
      </Step>
    </Steps>
    Dynamic. Each call is shaped by everything that came before in attention — including chunks the model has already cross-encoded against the original intent.
  </Tab>
</Tabs>

The kit ships:

<AccordionGroup>
  <Accordion title="Apps as the unit of capability" icon="puzzle-piece">
    A `Source` + a `Tool`-map + a `skill.eta` + an `app.json` manifest, wired
    through `defineApp`. The two reference apps that ship with the HDK are
    **`web_research`** (Tavily / keyless web search + page fetch) and
    **`corpus_research`** (filesystem-backed local docs with reranker-scored
    chunk search). Both are full HDK 3.0 Apps — read them as worked examples.
    Custom Apps for vector stores, databases, JIRA, email, anything — same
    protocol, same registry, same agent surface.
  </Accordion>
  <Accordion title="Cross-encoder reranker — four scoring roles" icon="layer-group">
    The reranker (Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B by default, auto-downloaded by the reference harness) drives an `EntailmentScorer` with four distinct methods:

    - **`scoreEntailmentBatch`** — texts vs. the original query. Boundary entailment for retrieved content.
    - **`scoreRelevanceBatch`** — dual-score `min(toolQueryScore, originalQueryScore)`. Used in exploit mode when KV pressure tightens focus.
    - **`scoreSimilarityBatch`** — texts vs. an arbitrary reference. Powers echo detection at delegation boundaries (the agent's own task as reference).
    - **`shouldProceed`** — floor gate. Scores are **logit-diffs** (`logit(yes) − logit(no)`); the default floor is `0` (model leans toward "yes").

    One model, four roles. The reranker is RIG infrastructure, not a fetch optimization.
  </Accordion>
  <Accordion title="Dynamic prompt composition" icon="code">
    Eta templates parameterize on harness state. Each App's `skill.eta` adapts on `it.agentCount > 1` (sibling-task awareness for parallel mode) and `it.taskIndex > 0` (build-on-prior-research for chain mode). Synthesis prompts adapt on convergence state and source presence. Prompts are functions of the harness's current situation, not fixed strings.
  </Accordion>
  <Accordion title="Pressure-aware tool gating" icon="gauge">
    `DefaultAgentPolicy` denies non-terminal tool calls when KV is over budget. The terminal tool (typically a harness-defined `report`) always passes — the agent can finish even under pressure. The explore/exploit gate flips the reranker's chunk-filtering mode when context tightens. Retrieval is bounded by the available compute, not by guesswork.
  </Accordion>
  <Accordion title="Protected tools + authGuard" icon="shield-halved">
    Tools carry a `protected` flag. Read tools (search, grep, fetch_page) are
    open; **protected** tools (writes, sensitive actions) are gated at
    DISPATCH by `authGuard` against a per-session grant store. App
    metadata that lands in the shared prompt is grammar-sanitized at
    `defineApp` time so an app-supplied string can't break out of the
    catalog block.
  </Accordion>
  <Accordion title="Continuous Context across delegation" icon="diagram-project">
    Sub-agents fork from a calling agent's branch and inherit its full KV state — system prompt, tool schemas, prior search results, prior page reads — at zero marginal cost. The deeper an investigation goes, the richer the attention state at depth.
  </Accordion>
</AccordionGroup>

candidates.heading: Lifetime and cleanup
candidates.text:
## Lifetime and cleanup

`Rerank.dispose()` is idempotent. It:

1. Calls `pruneSubtree()` (CASCADE) on the trunk — `RESTRICT prune()` would throw if any leaf or queryBranch leaked from a swallowed abandonment, masking the original error.
2. Clears the `__decodeOwner` flag so the SessionContext can be re-bound.
3. Disposes the underlying SessionContext.

For consumers that wire Rerank into the rig's `createReranker` (the canonical path), the `resource()` shape handles dispose automatically when the surrounding scope tears down. The contract above is what allows test/REPL re-creation patterns: dispose, immediately re-create on the same ctx, get a fresh instance.

candidates.heading: KV spine — within a task's delegation
candidates.text:
### KV spine — within a task's delegation

When an agent calls `web_research`/`research`, DelegateTool creates an inner pool with `parent: context.branch`. Sub-agents fork from the calling agent's branch and inherit its full KV state.

```
Position 0-100:    Spine (system prompt + tool schemas)
Position 100-300:  Agent suffix (system+user prompt with task)
Position 300-500:  web_search result (prefilled via SETTLE)
Position 500-900:  fetch_page result (survey article content)
Position 900-950:  Agent reasoning + web_research call

  Agent calls web_research(["Moshi architecture", "Sesame prosody"])
  ↓ fork (warm path — only turn separator prefilled)

  Inner spine (forkHead=950, position=955)
    ↓ fork × 2

    Sub-agent B (task: "Moshi architecture")
      Attends over positions 0-955 (entire KV spine)
      Searches with vocabulary from the survey already in attention

    Sub-agent C (task: "Sesame prosody")
      Attends over positions 0-955 (same spine)
```

The KV spine is the chain of decoded state within a single agent's branch. Sub-agents inherit it at zero cost. Delegation results are JSON-serialized and prefilled back into the calling agent's branch during SETTLE, extending the spine for subsequent tool calls.

candidates.heading: The Source base
Run it
candidates.text:
## Run it

```bash
export TAVILY_API_KEY=tvly-…

npx tsx examples/compare/main.ts \
  --x "Rust's ownership model" \
  --y "Swift's automatic reference counting" \
  --corpus ~/Documents/swift-docs \
  --reranker ~/.cache/lloyal/models/Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B-Q8_0.gguf \
  --axes "memory safety,runtime cost,ergonomics" \
  ~/.cache/lloyal/models/Qwen3.5-4B-Q4_K_M.gguf
```

Or via the workspace script: `npm run examples:compare -- --x "…" --y "…" …`

Required flags: `--x`, `--y`, `--corpus`, `--reranker`, `TAVILY_API_KEY`. Optional: `--axes` (default `accuracy,performance,complexity` — must be exactly three), `--max-turns`, `--n-ctx`, `--output-dir`, `--jsonl`.

candidates.heading: Calibration gates
candidates.text:
## Calibration gates

`Rerank.create()` fires three fail-loud gates at boot. If any fails, the consumer gets a typed `RerankCalibrationError` with diagnostic context; no Rerank instance is returned and the SessionContext is released so the next attempt can re-bind.

1. **Single-token yes / no** — the score formula assumes single-token labels. Multi-token labels (some non-English tokenizers) need a log-sum-exp generalization (3.x ticket).
2. **BPE-boundary drift** — re-tokenizing the whole canary prompt is compared to the concat of the segments (prefix, query, mid, doc, suffix). Most chat templates produce 1-5 tokens of drift from BOS/EOS handling; the gate trips on >5% drift, which would indicate a sentinel-induced multi-token BPE merge (catastrophic) rather than benign chrome.
3. **Boot canary** — two known pairs (Paris/Paris-doc relevant, Paris/Photosynthesis-doc irrelevant); the relevant pair must outscore the irrelevant by >1 logit unit. The gate asserts the *gap*, not absolute signs — quantization shifts calibration enough that sign assertions are brittle, while the ordering gap still catches model swaps, template drift, and yes/no token reversal.

The canary fixtures are hardcoded to Qwen3-reranker semantics. If you swap reranker models, re-run the calibration probe and update the fixtures.

candidates.heading: FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 — canonical template
candidates.text:
# FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 — canonical template

This is the canonical FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 LICENSE text used across the lloyal
runtime stack (`liblloyal`, `lloyal-node`, `lloyal-sdk` and its FSL packages).
Each repo's `LICENSE` file is this template with the four parameters below
substituted in.

candidates.heading: Three queries in play
candidates.text:
### Three queries in play

| Concept | Scope | Field name in code |
|---|---|---|
| **Tool query** | Per tool call | `localQuery` param of `scoreRelevanceBatch` |
| **Agent task** | Per agent lifetime | `reference` param of `scoreSimilarityBatch` |
| **Original query** | Per research invocation | Captured in closure by `createScorer` |

Conflating these produces wrong scores. The four methods correspond to four boundaries:

- **`scoreEntailmentBatch`** — does this retrieved content stay on the *original* research intent? Used at content-prefill boundaries.
- **`scoreRelevanceBatch`** — combine the agent's per-tool query with the original query via `min(local, original)`. Used in exploit mode when KV pressure tightens focus.
- **`scoreSimilarityBatch`** — compare texts against an arbitrary reference (the agent's own task, typically). Powers echo detection at delegation boundaries — preventing an agent from re-issuing a search that paraphrases its own task.
- **`shouldProceed`** — the floor gate. Below it, the content is dropped or the agent stalls.

candidates.heading: When to use
candidates.text:
## When to use

- **Mixed-role parallel pools.** A DAG with multiple agent kinds drawing from one tool set. Tools amortized at root; per-agent roles preserved.
- **Tool-heavy chains.** A 5-step research chain re-emitting tool schemas per spawn wastes ~700 tokens per step. Catalog at root drops that to zero past step 1.
- **You want the model to enforce protocol discipline soft-side.** The catalog's `useWhen` lines + the tool-selection rule teach the boundary. The model can still call any tool (grammar permits all), but the role assignment pushes it toward the right subset.

candidates.heading:
title: Quick Start
description: Build a daily news digest agent that researches, drafts, and emails itself to you — in five minutes.
candidates.text:
title: Quick Start
description: Build a daily news digest agent that researches, drafts, and emails itself to you — in five minutes.
---

Tomorrow morning, an email lands in your inbox. Subject: *Daily Digest — 2026-04-30*. Body: a one-line headline summarizing the day, three or four stories with sources, a one-line "watching" closer.

You wrote ~100 lines of TypeScript on Monday. Now it runs on a cron, on a local model, costing $0 in inference. This page is how you build it.

candidates.heading: When not to use
candidates.text:
## When not to use

- **Single-role pools where every spawn has the exact same role.** The protocol-catalog convention helps when roles differ; if they don't, just put the role in `agentPool({ systemPrompt })` without the catalog scoping (or skip the convention entirely — re-emitting tools per spawn is fine for small pools).
- **Hard tool gating.** This is a soft enforcement — the model can ignore the rule. If you need to *prevent* certain tool calls per agent, you need either per-agent grammar restriction (a future evolution; not currently exposed) or the `protected` + `authGuard` gate for sensitive actions (see [App security](/build-an-app/app-security)).

candidates.heading: See also
candidates.text:
## See also

- [Mixed-role pools](/under-the-hood/mixed-role-pools) — the shared-catalog convention for pools with multiple roles
- [Continuous-context spine](/under-the-hood/continuous-context-spine) — how `extendSpine` and prefix sharing compose
- [Concurrency](/under-the-hood/concurrency) — the five-phase tick loop and DAG orchestration
- [Sources & retrieval](/build-an-app/sources-and-retrieval) — the `Source<TCtx, TChunk>` contract
- [Bridges](/build-a-harness/bridges) — multi-source pipelines
- [Compare](/examples/compare) — a worked DAG harness using these patterns

candidates.heading: The trace tree
candidates.text:
## The trace tree

Events form a tree via `parentTraceId`. Reconstruct it to see the full execution structure:

```
scope:open(pool, outer)
  ├── tool:dispatch(research, A0)          ← toolIndex: 4/5
  │   ├── scope:open(withSpine)       ← inner pool
  │   │   └── scope:open(pool, inner)
  │   │       ├── tool:dispatch(grep)      ← toolIndex: 2/5
  │   │       └── tool:result(grep)
  │   └── scope:close(withSpine)
  └── tool:result(research, A0)
```

The `TraceParent` Effection context automatically connects inner pools to the outer tool dispatch that spawned them. No manual wiring needed.

candidates.heading: LLAMA_CTX_SIZE environment variable
candidates.text:
### LLAMA_CTX_SIZE environment variable

Examples read `LLAMA_CTX_SIZE` from the environment when no explicit `--n-ctx` flag is passed:

```bash
LLAMA_CTX_SIZE=32768 npx reasoning.run --output-dir ./runs
```

If unset, defaults to 16384.

candidates.heading: Tool Guards
Sizing guidelines
candidates.text:
### Sizing guidelines

| Pipeline | Recommended nCtx | Reasoning |
|----------|-----------------|-----------|
| Single agent + tools | 8192 | One branch, moderate tool results |
| Simple research (1-2 sources) | 16384 | Default. 3 agents + synthesis + eval |
| Deep research (multi-source) | 32768 | Bridge stages, multiple source rounds |
| Large corpus research | 32768-65536 | Long documents inflate search/read results |

The budget divides across concurrent branches. With 3 research agents sharing a root:

```
Spine:     ~200 tokens (system prompt + tools)
Agent suffix:    ~300 tokens each (user message + generation prompt)
Tool results:    ~500-2000 tokens per call (varies by tool)
Generation:      ~200-500 tokens per turn
```

At `nCtx=16384` with 3 agents doing 4 tool calls each, rough budget:
- Spine: 200
- 3 agents x (300 suffix + 4 x 1000 tool results + 4 x 300 generation): ~15,900
- Remaining for synthesis + eval: ~300

This is tight. If agents hit large tool results (web pages, long search results), context pressure kicks in and starts cutting agents. For comfortable headroom, use 32768.

candidates.heading: Inspect your account
candidates.text:
## Inspect your account

```bash
npx harness.dev publishers me
```

Returns the publisher record for your current authenticated identity:

```
acme
  status:         active
  published:      3
  tosVersion:     v1
  tosAcceptedAt:  2026-06-08T02:25:37.976Z
  createdAt:      2026-06-08T02:25:37.976Z
```

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| (top line) | Your registered handle |
| `status` | `active` or `suspended` (suspended publishers can't submit; contact support to appeal) |
| `published` | Count of approved versions across all your apps |
| `tosVersion` | Which ToS version you attested to. If you see `tos-version-stale` on subsequent publish, re-run `publishers register` to attest the latest version |
| `tosAcceptedAt` / `createdAt` | Timestamps of your initial registration |

If no record exists for your identity, the command exits non-zero with a hint:

```
harness.dev publishers me: no publisher account for this identity.
Run `harness.dev publishers register --handle <handle>` first.
```

candidates.heading: How it works
candidates.text:
### How it works

When `shouldExit` (or stall-break, or post-loop cleanup) kills an agent:

1. **Policy check**: Call `policy.onRecovery(agent, pressure)`. If it returns `{type: 'skip'}`, prune the branch and continue.
2. **Budget computation**: Policy computes `budget = max(50, pressure.remaining - prefillOverhead - batchBuffer)` and renders the recovery prompt's eta templates with `{budget}` context. The model receives its actual token budget.
3. **Grammar**: Create an eager JSON grammar from `jsonSchemaToGrammar({ result: string })`. Constrains from token 0 — the model MUST produce `{"result": "..."}`.
4. **Prefill**: Prefill the rendered extraction prompt into the agent's own branch. No fork — the agent's full KV context (system prompt, tool results, reasoning) is physically present.
5. **Set grammar**: `branch.setGrammar(reportGrammar)`. Eager mode — active from the first generated token.
6. **Generate**: Single-agent produce/commit loop until stop token.
7. **Parse + diagnostics**: Parse the JSON output. Emit `pool:recoveryProduce` with token count + output length, then one of:
   - `pool:recoveryReturn` — parse succeeded, findings captured via `setResult(parsed.result, 'recovery')`.
   - `pool:recoveryFailed` — parse failed. Event carries `reason` (`parse_error`, `no_result_field`, `scope_error`) and `outputExcerpt` (first 200 chars) so ops can diagnose without re-running.
8. **Prune**: Free the agent's branch. The freed KV cells give remaining agents headroom.

Recovery runs inside `scoped()` with error containment — if `llama_decode` fails during the produce/commit loop, the scope tears down cleanly and `pool:recoveryFailed` fires with the captured failure reason.
