candidates.heading:
title: "Agent Policy"
description: "The policy interface — lifecycle hooks (produce / settle / recover), budget gating, tool guards, recovery extraction."
candidates.text:
title: "Agent Policy"
description: "The policy interface — lifecycle hooks (produce / settle / recover), budget gating, tool guards, recovery extraction."
---

Every agent lifecycle decision passes through a policy. When an agent hits a stop token, when a tool result won't fit in KV, when a tool call is about to dispatch — the pool consults the policy and executes the returned action. The pool handles mechanics (transitions, prefills, prunes). The policy handles judgment.

> **See also.** This page documents the policy *interface*. For the underlying budget model and pressure thresholds, see [KV pressure](/under-the-hood/kv-pressure). For production tuning of `softLimit` / `hardLimit` / `recovery` per-deployment, see [Deployment](/guides/deployment).

candidates.heading: Pressure decision flow
candidates.text: ## Pressure decision flow

candidates.heading: Lifecycle — no hooks, just scopes
candidates.text:
## Lifecycle — no hooks, just scopes

There are no `install`/`uninstall`/`enable`/`disable` hooks on the App. Lifecycle is Effection scope:

- **Factory body = setup.** An `AppFactory` is `() => Operation<App>`. Read config + reranker, construct, return.
- **`ensure(...) = teardown`.** Anything the factory allocates that needs cleanup (a connection pool, a watcher) goes inside `ensure(...)` so the registry's scope-halt fires it.
- **Per-app detached scope.** The registry runs each factory in its own detached scope and tears them down in reverse register-order on registry-scope exit — best-effort, so a throwing teardown can never strand a sibling or crash the harness.

[Lifecycle & registry](/build-an-app/lifecycle-and-registry) walks the registry's scope model in detail.

candidates.heading: The interface
2. PRODUCE critical (the kill threshold)
Drop reasons
candidates.text:
## Drop reasons

The `pool:agentDrop` trace event records the specific enforcement point:

| Reason | Origin | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `pressure_init` | `ctx.spawn` prefill gate | Agent suffix doesn't fit under softLimit |
| `pressure_critical` | PRODUCE `shouldExit` | `remaining < hardLimit`; recovery runs in reserve |
| `pressure_softcut` | PRODUCE `_handleOverBudget` | Non-terminal over-budget + no terminal tool available |
| `pressure_settle_reject` | SETTLE stall-break (policy-driven) | Policy returned `idle` or nudge that can't fit |
| `settle_stall_break` | SETTLE stall-break (legacy fallback) | `onSettleReject` hook absent |
| `time_exceeded` | PRODUCE shouldExit (time hardLimit) | Wall-clock hardLimit crossed |
| `policy_exit` | PRODUCE shouldExit (custom policy) | Policy returned `true` not driven by pressure |
| `maxTurns` | PRODUCE (policy `_handleOverBudget`) | Turn count ≥ maxTurns, no terminal tool |
| `tool_error` | DISPATCH | Tool's `execute()` threw |

Every drop reason (except `pressure_init`, which is pre-spawn) is followed by a recovery attempt whose outcome is visible in `pool:recoveryReturn` or `pool:recoveryFailed` — no silent data loss.

candidates.heading: Problem: Agents killed by context pressure
candidates.text:
## Problem: Agents killed by context pressure

**Symptom:** `pool:agentDrop` events appear in the trace. Some agents produce no findings.

**Diagnosis steps:**

1. Collect all `pool:agentDrop` events and group by `reason`:

| Reason | Meaning | When |
|--------|---------|------|
| `pressure_init` | Agent didn't fit during pool setup | Before any generation |
| `pressure_softcut` | Agent wanted a non-terminal tool but headroom was negative | At stop token during PRODUCE |
| `pressure_settle_reject` | Policy-driven drop: nudge unavailable, drop + recover | During SETTLE |
| `settle_stall_break` | All agents deferred and no hook to nudge — one sacrificed to free KV | During SETTLE |
| `policy_exit` | Policy `shouldExit` returned a stop (e.g. turn cap in a subclass) | At stop token during PRODUCE |
| `maxTurns` | Agent exhausted its turn budget | At stop token during PRODUCE |

2. Check `pool:open` for the initial pressure state:

```jsonl
{"type":"pool:open","pressure":{"remaining":8000,"softLimit":1024,"headroom":6976}}
```

If `headroom` is tight at pool open, subsequent tool results will exhaust it quickly.

3. Look at `tool:result` events for `prefillTokenCount`. Large tool results (web pages at 2000+ tokens, search results at 500+ tokens) are the primary KV consumers:

```jsonl
{"type":"tool:result","tool":"fetch_page","prefillTokenCount":1847,"durationMs":342}
{"type":"tool:result","tool":"web_search","prefillTokenCount":623,"durationMs":1205}
```

**Solutions:**
- Increase `nCtx` (see [Deployment](./deployment.md))
- Reduce `AGENT_COUNT` to give each agent more KV budget
- Truncate tool results in tool implementations (cap response size)
- Lower `softLimit` in pressure options (trades downstream headroom for agent longevity)
- Use `q4_0` KV quantization to fit more tokens in the same memory

candidates.heading: Writing a custom policy
Why
candidates.text:
### Why

The internal vocabulary echoed the model's tool-call mental model when it should have described what was happening. "Report" carries a domain assumption (research findings); "return" describes the *semantic* (function exit with value) and works for any agent regardless of what it produces. The split between voluntary and recovery makes the framework honest about *how* a value was obtained — both paths produce `agent.result` but only one is the model's own work.

candidates.heading:
title: "KV Pressure"
description: "The pressure budget model — how ContextPressure partitions the KV cache and where enforcement happens in the tick loop."
candidates.text:
title: "KV Pressure"
description: "The pressure budget model — how `ContextPressure` partitions the KV cache and where enforcement happens in the tick loop."
---

Agent pools share a single KV cache. As agents generate tokens and prefill tool results, the cache fills. `ContextPressure` tracks how much room remains and enforces thresholds at multiple points in the tick loop to prevent out-of-memory crashes. When an agent is killed by pressure, an inline recovery protocol extracts its findings before the pool continues, using the `hardLimit` reserve as a parallel report budget.

> **See also.** This page documents the *budget model and enforcement mechanics*. For the policy hooks that act on pressure events (`onProduced`, `shouldExit`, `onRecovery`), see [Agent Policy](/under-the-hood/agent-policy). For picking `nCtx` / `softLimit` / KV quantization values for a given deployment, see [Deployment](/guides/deployment).

candidates.heading: DefaultAgentPolicy configuration
Reasoning probes
candidates.text:
## Reasoning probes

Override the `probe` getter to prefill text after the tool result settles. The probe is injected between the tool result and the grammar reset, giving the model unconstrained prose space to reason about the result before deciding the next action.

```typescript
class SearchTool extends Tool<{ query: string }> {
  readonly name = 'search';
  // ...

  get probe() { return 'Wait, '; }
}
```

The pool handles the lifecycle:
1. Tool result prefilled into agent's KV cache
2. Probe text prefilled immediately after
3. Lazy grammar reset — model generates freely until a trigger fires
4. Model reasons about the result, then produces the next tool call or reports

Probes only fire after real tool dispatches. Nudges, settle rejects, and terminal tool interceptions are unaffected. When `probe` returns `null` (the default), no extra prefill happens — zero cost.

<Note>
Most tools don't need probes. Use them when a tool returns dense results that benefit from explicit intermediate reasoning — semantic search results, large document reads, complex API responses where the model should synthesize before acting.
</Note>

candidates.heading: Agent Policy
candidates.text:
## Agent Policy

The pool consults an **AgentPolicy** at every decision boundary — when an agent hits a stop token, when deciding whether to kill under pressure, and when checking tool guards before dispatch.

```typescript
interface AgentPolicy {
  onProduced(agent, parsed, pressure, config): ProduceAction;
  shouldExit?(agent, pressure): boolean;    // trailing stop kill gate
  shouldExplore?(agent, pressure): boolean; // optional — defaults to true
  onRecovery?(agent): RecoveryAction;       // scratchpad extraction
  resetTick?(): void;                       // per-tick flag reset
}
```

`DefaultAgentPolicy` handles all the default behavior:

- **Terminal tool interception** — when the agent calls `report()`, extract result and mark idle
- **Budget enforcement** — deny non-terminal tool calls when over budget, nudge the agent to report
- **Confabulation prevention** — reject premature reports from agents with fewer than 2 tool calls
- **Tool guard checking** — run declarative guards before dispatching any tool call
- **Explore/exploit switching** — decide per-DISPATCH whether content-boundary tools should explore (agent-local scoring) or exploit (dual scoring against original query)

Customize thresholds and guards via the constructor:

```typescript
import { DefaultAgentPolicy } from "@lloyal-labs/lloyal-agents";

const policy = new DefaultAgentPolicy({
  minToolCallsBeforeReturn: 3,
  extraGuards: [myCustomGuard],
  exploreThreshold: 40,
  budget: { context: { softLimit: 2048 }, time: { softLimit: 600_000 } },
  recovery: { prompt: REPORT_PROMPT },
});

const pool = yield* agentPool({
  orchestrate: parallel(specs),  // each spec carries its own systemPrompt
  tools,
  policy,
});
```

The policy sees the agent's full state: status, tool history, result, nudge count, pressure snapshot. It returns an action — `tool_call`, `report`, `nudge`, or `idle` — and the pool executes it.

candidates.heading: How it works
candidates.text:
### How it works

When `shouldExit` (or stall-break, or post-loop cleanup) kills an agent:

1. **Policy check**: Call `policy.onRecovery(agent, pressure)`. If it returns `{type: 'skip'}`, prune the branch and continue.
2. **Budget computation**: Policy computes `budget = max(50, pressure.remaining - prefillOverhead - batchBuffer)` and renders the recovery prompt's eta templates with `{budget}` context. The model receives its actual token budget.
3. **Grammar**: Create an eager JSON grammar from `jsonSchemaToGrammar({ result: string })`. Constrains from token 0 — the model MUST produce `{"result": "..."}`.
4. **Prefill**: Prefill the rendered extraction prompt into the agent's own branch. No fork — the agent's full KV context (system prompt, tool results, reasoning) is physically present.
5. **Set grammar**: `branch.setGrammar(reportGrammar)`. Eager mode — active from the first generated token.
6. **Generate**: Single-agent produce/commit loop until stop token.
7. **Parse + diagnostics**: Parse the JSON output. Emit `pool:recoveryProduce` with token count + output length, then one of:
   - `pool:recoveryReturn` — parse succeeded, findings captured via `setResult(parsed.result, 'recovery')`.
   - `pool:recoveryFailed` — parse failed. Event carries `reason` (`parse_error`, `no_result_field`, `scope_error`) and `outputExcerpt` (first 200 chars) so ops can diagnose without re-running.
8. **Prune**: Free the agent's branch. The freed KV cells give remaining agents headroom.

Recovery runs inside `scoped()` with error containment — if `llama_decode` fails during the produce/commit loop, the scope tears down cleanly and `pool:recoveryFailed` fires with the captured failure reason.

candidates.heading: Structured output with schema
Spine bloat from large tool results
candidates.text:
### Spine bloat from large tool results

Each tool result prefilled into an agent's branch permanently extends its KV spine. A fetch_page returning 2000 tokens consumes 2000 cells. Sub-agents at deeper delegation levels inherit the cost. Tools that return large payloads should compress server-side (the `lloyal/web` app's `fetch_page` does this via reranker-based chunk selection) before returning into the spine.

candidates.heading: "I have many concurrent agents — KV is filling up" — pruneOnReturn
candidates.text:
#### "I have many concurrent agents — KV is filling up" — `pruneOnReturn`

Set `true` for parallel pools where each agent's branch grows large and you want sibling agents to recover headroom mid-flight. The agent's branch is pruned immediately after the terminal tool fires. Spine extensions (which live on a separate root) are unaffected.

candidates.heading: Agent lifecycle
candidates.text: ## Agent lifecycle

candidates.heading: Problem: Scratchpad extraction fails
Problem: Agents not using tools
candidates.text:
## Problem: Agents not using tools

**Symptom:** Agents generate text but never call any tools. They produce raw text output and are marked done without tool calls.

**Diagnosis steps:**

1. Check `prompt:format` events with `role: 'agentSuffix'`. The `tools` field must contain the JSON-serialized tool schemas. If `tools` is missing or empty, the model has no awareness of available tools.

```jsonl
{"type":"prompt:format","role":"agentSuffix","tools":"[{\"type\":\"function\",...}]",...}
```

2. Check `agent:turn` events. If `parsedToolCalls` is an empty array, the model generated text but no tool-call syntax:

```jsonl
{"type":"agent:turn","agentId":5,"parsedContent":"The answer is...","parsedToolCalls":[]}
```

3. Verify the model supports tool calling. If the model's chat template does not handle tools, `formatChatSync` will produce `CHAT_FORMAT_CONTENT_ONLY` or `CHAT_FORMAT_GENERIC`, and agent setup will throw. If you catch/suppress this error, agents will run without tool awareness.

**Common causes:**
- Missing `tools` on the pool: `agentPool({ orchestrate, tools: [], ... })` — empty array means no tools available to the agent
- Wrong model: base model instead of instruction-tuned, or instruction-tuned without tool-call training
- Empty tools array: `tools: []` means no tools available to the agent

candidates.heading: Under the App protocol
candidates.text:
## Under the App protocol

The policy interface is App-agnostic — it doesn't know which App spawned the agent, only that the agent is in the pool. Three App-protocol interactions to keep in mind:

- **`authGuard` runs BEFORE the policy's tool guards.** When the model emits a tool call for a `protected` tool, the dispatch path checks the session's `GrantStoreCtx` first. A denied call emits `tool:authReject` and the policy never sees a dispatchable action for it. The policy's `onProduced` only fires for tools the grant gate already cleared (or open tools, where the gate is a no-op).
- **`shouldExplore` flips retrieval mode for App `Source` calls.** When `false` (exploit), `SearchTool` re-scores via `EntailmentScorer.scoreRelevanceBatch` — `min(toolQueryScore, originalQueryScore)`. When `true` (explore, the default with headroom), scoring uses the tool query alone. The App's `Source` reads `ToolContext.scorer` + `ToolContext.explore` to obey. See [Search strategy](/under-the-hood/search-strategy).
- **`ReconPolicy` is the production subclass for preflight recon.** It overrides `shouldExit` with a hard turn cap (preempts the soft-nudge loop) and is paired with a `PREFLIGHT_RECOVER` prompt on force-kill. The reasoning.run harness uses it via `runPreflight`; custom harnesses with their own recon stage can mirror the pattern.

The hooks below are the substrate. The App protocol adds the auth-gate and the recon-policy variant on top.

candidates.heading: Hidden state interactions
candidates.text:
### Hidden state interactions

- **`_killedThisTick`** gates `shouldExit` — one kill per tick in critical. Does NOT apply to SETTLE stall-break, which can drop multiple agents on the same tick.
- **`_nudgedThisTick`** gates `_handleOverBudget` — one nudge per tick in PRODUCE. Does NOT apply to SETTLE stall-break, which can emit multiple nudges per tick (one per deferred item).
- **Terminal-tool protection (`currentTool === terminalToolName`)** is only checked in `DefaultAgentPolicy.shouldExit`. SETTLE stall-break drops and pool-close cleanups do NOT check `currentTool`.
- **`handleIdleDrop` (PRODUCE `isStop` kill) does NOT call `recoverInline`**. Agents dropped via this path stay idle; their findings are only extracted by the pool-close cleanup loop.

candidates.heading: 3. PRODUCE soft-cut + budget nudge
candidates.text:
### 3. PRODUCE soft-cut + budget nudge

When an agent hits a stop token and the parsed output contains a non-terminal tool call, the policy checks whether the call should be allowed:

```typescript
const overBudget = (a.turns >= maxTurns || pressure.headroom < 0)
  && (!terminalToolName || tc.name !== terminalToolName);
```

If `headroom < 0` (over the soft limit) and the tool call is not the terminal tool, the policy nudges the agent with a message *carrying the remaining token budget*:

```
KV memory pressure — report your findings now within 1100 words.
Turn limit reached — report your findings now within 1100 words.
Time limit reached — report your findings now within 1100 words.
```

The token ceiling is `pressure.remaining - pressure.hardLimit`, converted to a conservative word count (`floor(tokens * 0.7 / 10) * 10`, minimum 10) before being embedded in the message. **Words, not tokens**, because tokenizers vary across models but words are universal in training data — and under-advertising keeps the model within native decode headroom even if its word-to-token ratio differs from the 0.7 assumption. If the agent overshoots, `pressure.critical` fires on the next tick and recovery takes over (consuming the `hardLimit` reserve). If the agent obeys, the voluntary return lands cleanly.

Terminal tools always pass through without nudging — an agent that wants to report should never be blocked.

<Tip>
Model behavior varies: some small models honor the token budget closely, others produce verbose prose before their tool call. Both paths are covered. Voluntary report within budget → clean finish. Overshoot → kill + recovery report in the reserved cells. Either way, findings land on the spine. The budget-aware nudge just makes the happy path more likely.
</Tip>

candidates.heading: Trailing stop behavior
candidates.text:
### Trailing stop behavior

The policy's `shouldExit` kills at most one agent per tick when pressure is critical. The `_killedThisTick` flag prevents mass kills. After recovery extracts and prunes, the freed KV gives remaining agents headroom to continue. If pressure stays critical, another agent is killed next tick.

candidates.heading: 4. SETTLE deferral (tool result gating)
What's not on this page (and where it lives)
candidates.text:
## What's not on this page (and where it lives)

The concept spine deliberately stops at *names and relationships*. Everything below is mechanism — read it when you're building or debugging the corresponding piece:

| Mechanism | Lives in |
| --- | --- |
| Tick phases (SPAWN+EXTEND · PRODUCE · COMMIT · SETTLE · DISPATCH) | [Concurrency](/under-the-hood/concurrency) |
| KV pressure, recovery, force-kill | [KV pressure](/under-the-hood/kv-pressure) |
| Branch fork / prefill / prune mechanics | [Branch lifecycle](/under-the-hood/branch-lifecycle) |
| How the Spine is rendered (catalog, boundary marker, tool-selection rule) | [Continuous-context spine](/under-the-hood/continuous-context-spine) |
| GBNF grammar, tool ordering, eager vs lazy | [Grammar & ordering](/under-the-hood/grammar-and-ordering) |
| Reranker scoring (logit-diff, floor) | [Sources & retrieval](/build-an-app/sources-and-retrieval) |
| App registry scope model, factory/teardown | [Lifecycle & registry](/build-an-app/lifecycle-and-registry) |
| The App protocol (boundary marker, intro, catalog format) | [What is an App](/build-an-app/what-is-an-app#the-app-protocol) |

candidates.heading: Effection-native lifecycle
candidates.text:
### Effection-native lifecycle

- **`createReranker` migrated to `resource()` shape.** Tied to scope; teardown deterministic via `provide` + `ensure`.
- **Per-app detached scopes** in the registry. `createScope()` + `scope.run()` per factory + a `suspend()` between construction and `destroy()`. App teardown errors never propagate to the caller's scope.
- **No lifecycle hooks on `App`.** Setup is the factory body. Teardown is `ensure(...)`. The registry handles the rest via scope-halt on its own scope exit.

candidates.heading: Related
candidates.text:
## Related

- [Continuous Context Spine](/under-the-hood/continuous-context-spine) — the same `Branch` + `BranchStore` primitives, used by the agent runtime instead of rerank.
- [Branch lifecycle](/under-the-hood/branch-lifecycle) — multi-tag KV survival, fork-prune topology.
- [KV pressure](/under-the-hood/kv-pressure) — how `nSeqMax` interacts with per-sequence token budgets.
- [Search strategy](/under-the-hood/search-strategy) — how the SearchTool envelope feeds into agent planning.

candidates.heading: Customization
candidates.text:
## Customization

- **Different axes** — `--axes "axis1,axis2,axis3"` (must be exactly three).
- **Different research sides** — both research nodes use the same source contract; swap `WebSource` for another implementation in `main.ts` if you want corpus-vs-corpus or web-vs-web.
- **More compare siblings** — adjust the axes array length and the `dependsOn` of `synthesize`. The DAG structure scales arbitrarily.
- **Replace `dagWithEvents`** with the framework's `dag()` if you don't need the TUI's per-node hooks.

candidates.heading:
title: "Deployment"
description: "Production tuning checklist — model choice, nCtx, KV quantization, policy thresholds. Actionable recipes that reference the underlying mechanics."
candidates.text:
title: "Deployment"
description: "Production tuning checklist — model choice, nCtx, KV quantization, policy thresholds. Actionable recipes that reference the underlying mechanics."
---

This guide covers model selection, context sizing, KV quantization, and runtime configuration for deploying a lloyal-agents pipeline.

> **See also.** This guide is the *tuning checklist*. For the policy interface (the hooks you're tuning), see [Agent Policy](/under-the-hood/agent-policy). For why the pressure thresholds matter (the budget model), see [KV Pressure](/under-the-hood/kv-pressure).

candidates.heading: Create a harness
candidates.text:
## Create a harness

```bash
npx harness.dev <name> [--dir <path>]
```

Generates a new harness in a directory named `<name>` in your current working directory (or in `<path>` if `--dir` is given). `<name>` must match `[a-z][a-z0-9_-]{1,63}`.

```bash
npx harness.dev my-research-harness
cd my-research-harness
npm install
# edit harness.json — point model.path at a local .gguf
npm run dev "What was the Cuban missile crisis?"
```

The generated harness ships with the `lloyal/wikipedia` reference app preinstalled (via signed-channel canonical URL) so it answers a research question on first run with no extra setup beyond a local model file.

The generated harness includes:

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `package.json` | Standard npm metadata + scripts (`dev`, `build`, `start`). Depends on `@lloyal-labs/lloyal-agents`, `@lloyal-labs/sdk`, `@lloyal-labs/rig`, `@lloyal-labs/lloyal.node`, `effection`, and `@lloyal-labs/wikipedia-app` (pinned to the signed-channel canonical tarball URL). |
| `harness.json` | Per-harness config — model path and context size. |
| `bin/run.js` | Node shim that requires `dist/main.js`. |
| `src/main.ts` | Boot — parses argv, loads the model, builds the `AppRegistry`, enables the Wikipedia app, runs the query. |
| `src/harness.ts` | The orchestrator — `runQuery` plans the query into tasks via `PlanTool`, fans them out as parallel agents via `agentPool` + `parallel`, then reduces the reports into an answer via a synth `useAgent` call. The harness IS the orchestrator. |
| `prompts/plan.eta` | Planner prompt (Eta-templated). |
| `prompts/synth.eta` | Synthesizer prompt. |
| `LICENSE` | Apache 2.0. |
| `tsconfig.json` | Standard TS config (CommonJS, ES2022). |

The harness is yours — extend it freely. Add the corpus reference app with `npx harness.dev install lloyal/corpus`, or scaffold your own app with `npx harness.dev app`. When you're ready to ship a derivative harness, the same Apache 2.0 license applies.

candidates.heading: onRecovery — recovery extraction
candidates.text:
## `onRecovery` — recovery extraction

Called inline when the trailing stop kills an agent during PRODUCE (or at pool close for agents that ended idle without result). The policy decides whether to extract findings, and renders its extraction prompt with the live pressure snapshot so the model knows its budget.

```typescript
type RecoveryAction =
  | { type: 'extract'; prompt: { system: string; user: string } }
  | { type: 'skip' };

onRecovery?(agent: Agent, pressure: ContextPressure): RecoveryAction;
```

`DefaultAgentPolicy` checks the confabulation guard before returning `extract`:
- `agent.tokenCount >= minTokens` (default 100) — agent generated enough context
- `agent.toolCallCount >= minToolCalls` (default 2) — agent did real work

Agents that barely ran would produce fabricated findings — the guard prevents this.

**Budget-aware prompt rendering.** The policy computes `budget = max(50, pressure.remaining - prefillOverhead - batchBuffer)` and renders the prompt's eta template with that value. Prompt authors use `<%= it.budget %>` where they want the budget surfaced:

```eta
You are a research reporter. The agent has been terminated due to budget constraints.

Call the report tool with the most important findings. Prioritize:
1. Direct quotes and specific evidence
2. Key conclusions with source URLs
3. Supporting context (only if budget allows)

IMPORTANT: You have approximately <%= it.budget %> words for your report. Be concise.
---
Report your findings.
```

Prompts with no eta tags render unchanged (backward compatible). Policies that author prompts via `DefaultAgentPolicyOpts.recovery.prompt` can use the full eta feature set (conditionals, loops) for richer templating.

The pool owns the extraction grammar (`{ "result": "..." }` JSON schema). The policy owns the prompt and the decision.

**Recovery diagnostics are always visible.** After every non-skip recovery attempt, the pool emits one of:

- `pool:recoveryReturn` — JSON parse succeeded, findings captured.
- `pool:recoveryFailed` — parse failed; event carries `reason` (`parse_error`, `no_result_field`, `scope_error`) + `outputExcerpt` (first 200 chars of what was generated) so ops can diagnose without re-running.

No silent recovery failures — every recovery prefill has a matching diagnostic.

<Tip>
Recovery runs inline on each pressure-kill using the `hardLimit` reserve as a parallel report budget. `pruneOnReturn: true` (default) maximizes sibling headroom after each recovered or voluntary return.
</Tip>

candidates.heading: Model selection
candidates.text:
## Model selection

lloyal-agents requires an instruction-tuned model with native tool-calling support. The runtime uses `formatChat()` to embed tool schemas into the system prompt and `parseChatOutput()` to extract structured tool calls from generation output. Models without tool support will fail at agent setup with:

```
Error: Model does not support tool calling. Please use a model with native tool support
```

Confirmed compatible model families:

| Family | Notes |
|--------|-------|
| Qwen 3 | Recommended. Strong tool-call compliance, good depth-first behavior |
| Llama 3.x | Works well with instruction-tuned variants |
| Mistral | Tool-calling variants supported |

The reference pipeline uses `Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507` quantized to Q4_K_M. Larger models improve reasoning depth (more hypothesis-driven tool calls, better synthesis) but require proportionally more KV memory.

candidates.heading: Per-role system prompts
candidates.text:
## Per-role system prompts

Each `DAGNode`'s `task.systemPrompt` is the role assignment for that agent. In compare, per-role prompts live in `prompts/research-web.eta`, `compare.eta`, `synthesize.eta` etc., and each opens by stating which subset of tools is in scope for that role. The agent then forks from a shared spine that holds the full tool catalog, but only uses what its role authorizes.

When you adopt the App protocol, this convention moves up a level: each App ships its own `skill.eta` describing how an agent should use that App's tools, and the per-role prompt collapses into "use App `X`". See [What is an App](/build-an-app/what-is-an-app#skill) for the protocol.

candidates.heading: Internal enterprise use
candidates.text:
### Internal enterprise use

- A bank runs HDK internally to power their intelligent search and document
  understanding for compliance use
- A government agency deploys HDK on-premise for sovereign AI use cases
- A research org uses HDK across hundreds of internal applications

candidates.heading: createAppRegistry
Is this OSI-approved open source?
candidates.text:
## Is this OSI-approved open source?

**No, and we want to be honest about that.** FSL is not OSI-approved
because the OSI definition of open source (clause 6, "No Discrimination
Against Fields of Endeavor") does not permit restrictions on specific
use cases. FSL restricts Competing Use. That restriction takes it out of
strict OSI compliance.

FSL falls under the [Fair Source](https://fair.io) classification —
source-available licenses that are explicitly developer-friendly:
commercial use permitted, free redistribution, eventual open-source
conversion. Fair Source is a more developer-friendly framing than the
generic "source-available" label, which has been tainted by the
SSPL / Elastic / MongoDB relicensing trauma cycles.

What this means practically:

- **You can read the source.** ✅
- **You can modify it.** ✅
- **You can sell products built with it.** ✅
- **You can redistribute it (with the same FSL terms).** ✅
- **It will be Apache 2.0 in two years.** ✅
- Some enterprise procurement policies that strictly require OSI-approved
  licenses will require an exception for this. We're working on making
  that exception easy to grant.

candidates.heading: Tool retry parking
candidates.text:
## Tool retry parking

Tools own their backend semantics: when a backend is transiently unavailable (rate limit, brief outage), the tool throws `ToolRetryError` with a `retryAfterMs` hint instead of returning an error result. The pool parks the agent — it stays `awaiting_tool`, costing zero in PRODUCE — and re-dispatches the **same call, same callId** after the delay. The model's context never records the failure: by the Continuous Context argument, a transient condition the model can't act on doesn't belong in its KV.

Policy decides the budget, the pool provides the mechanism:

```ts
onToolRetry(agent, tool, error, attempt): ToolRetryAction
// { type: 'retry', afterMs? }  — park and re-dispatch (afterMs overrides the tool's hint)
// { type: 'fail', message? }   — deliver a directive tool result; the model pivots
```

`DefaultAgentPolicy` retries while `attempt <= maxToolRetries` (default 1). Exhaustion is **not** a `tool_error` — the agent receives a normal tool result saying the source is unavailable, and decides its next move with its evidence intact. Observability: the pool emits `agent:tool_retry` (with `retryAfterMs` and `attempt`) and traces `tool:retry`, so a harness can render "rate-limited — retrying in ~90s" instead of appearing stuck.

candidates.heading: Vertical AI products built on HDK
candidates.text:
### Vertical AI products built on HDK

- "Clay for legal" — a sales-intelligence app for law firms, built on HDK
- "Cowork for healthcare" — a workspace-AI app for clinicians, built on HDK
- A workflow-automation product that lets users describe pipelines and
  watches HDK agents execute them
- An intelligent IDE extension that uses HDK for local code understanding

The framing test: are you building *with* HDK, or are you offering HDK
itself? Building with — permitted. Offering HDK — restricted.

candidates.heading: Cross-agent dedup pattern
Entailment scoring
candidates.text:
### Entailment scoring

When agents recurse, their local hypotheses can drift from the original query. The reranker (a 0.6B cross-encoder with its own context, zero inference KV cost) acts as a real-time entailment oracle:

| Method | Scores against | Purpose |
|--------|---------------|---------|
| `scoreEntailmentBatch(texts)` | Original query | Filter drifted delegation questions |
| `scoreRelevanceBatch(texts, localQuery)` | min(tool query, original query) | Tighten focus in exploit mode |
| `scoreSimilarityBatch(reference, texts)` | Arbitrary reference | Echo detection — reject paraphrased delegations |

The scorer propagates through the entire recursion tree — every depth scores against the same original query.

candidates.heading: KV spine — between task stages (extendSpine)
candidates.text:
### KV spine — between task stages (`extendSpine`)

The harness sequences tasks via a `reduce` combinator inside a `withSpine` scope. A query-scoped `querySpine` branch is created once; all tasks fork from it, and between tasks, findings are prefilled directly into `querySpine` as user+assistant turns via the `extendSpine` helper.

```
querySpine (position 0)
  └─ Task 1 pool forks from querySpine (position 0)
       → Agent searches, fetches, reports → findings A
       → extendSpine: prefill [user: "Task 1", assistant: findings A] into querySpine
         querySpine advances to position 500

  └─ Task 2 pool forks from querySpine (position 500)
       → Agent inherits Task 1's findings via KV share (zero re-encoding)
       → Searches for entities from Task 1 → findings B
       → extendSpine: prefill [user: "Task 2", assistant: findings B] into querySpine
         querySpine advances to position 1100

  └─ Task 3 pool forks from querySpine (position 1100)
       → Agent inherits Tasks 1+2 findings via KV share
       → Deepens specific entities → findings C
```

Each task's pool forks from the EXTENDED `querySpine`. Findings are decoded once into `querySpine`; every subsequent fork shares them at zero marginal cost. No text re-encoding per agent.

candidates.heading: Reasoning Modes
candidates.text:
## Reasoning Modes

For the Research stage specifically, two orchestration shapes are common:

- **Sequential (chain)** — each task forks `querySpine` at its current extended position; on report, `extendSpine` writes the curated `[user, asst]` turn onto `querySpine`; the next task sees prior findings via KV attention. Worker prompts can adapt on `taskIndex > 0` to target gaps and named entities from earlier findings rather than re-investigating. Synth runs as a post-pool `useAgent` that forks the fully-extended `querySpine`.

- **Parallel (flat)** — all tasks fork `querySpine` simultaneously; no spine extension; each agent runs independently with sibling-task awareness in its prompt. Findings are concatenated into the synth's user message as text. Synth runs as a `useAgent` but consumes findings via text injection, not via attention.

Pick sequential when later tasks should genuinely build on earlier ones. Pick parallel when sub-questions are independent and time-budget headroom is plentiful.

candidates.heading: What it teaches
candidates.text:
## What it teaches

- **Multi-parent DAG dependencies** — each `compare_axis_*` node depends on **two** research nodes simultaneously. `chain` and `fanout` cannot express this.
- **Sibling parallelism with shared deps** — the three compare nodes fire the moment both research nodes complete, then run concurrently.
- **Multi-child convergence** — `synthesize` waits on all three siblings before spawning.
- **Spine extension is causal, not just sequential** — each node's `userContent` is prefilled onto the spine via `ctx.extendSpine`. The compare nodes don't merely *follow* the research nodes — they *attend to* them. The edge in the diagram is the spine.
- **Shared catalog, mixed roles** — researcher, comparer, synthesizer all draw from one tool catalog amortized at the root. Per-spec system prompts say which subset to use. (Under the App protocol, this collapses into per-App `skill.eta` — see [What is an App](/build-an-app/what-is-an-app#skill).)

candidates.heading: Warm vs Cold
candidates.text:
## Warm vs Cold

Pipelines typically handle both cold starts (first query) and warm continuations (follow-up questions) automatically:

<Tabs>
  <Tab title="Cold (first query)">
    Research runs inside `withSpine`. A shared prefix branch is created, agents fork from it. After synthesis, `session.commitTurn(query, answer)` creates the trunk with the query-response pair for future warm turns.
  </Tab>
  <Tab title="Warm (follow-up)">
    Prior conversation tokens are physically present in the trunk's KV cache. Research runs against the existing trunk via a warm path — sources bind and execute without creating a new spine. After synthesis, the same `session.commitTurn(query, answer)` extends the existing trunk with the new query-response pair.
  </Tab>
</Tabs>

candidates.heading: Three queries in play
candidates.text:
### Three queries in play

| Concept | Scope | Field name in code |
|---|---|---|
| **Tool query** | Per tool call | `localQuery` param of `scoreRelevanceBatch` |
| **Agent task** | Per agent lifetime | `reference` param of `scoreSimilarityBatch` |
| **Original query** | Per research invocation | Captured in closure by `createScorer` |

Conflating these produces wrong scores. The four methods correspond to four boundaries:

- **`scoreEntailmentBatch`** — does this retrieved content stay on the *original* research intent? Used at content-prefill boundaries.
- **`scoreRelevanceBatch`** — combine the agent's per-tool query with the original query via `min(local, original)`. Used in exploit mode when KV pressure tightens focus.
- **`scoreSimilarityBatch`** — compare texts against an arbitrary reference (the agent's own task, typically). Powers echo detection at delegation boundaries — preventing an agent from re-issuing a search that paraphrases its own task.
- **`shouldProceed`** — the floor gate. Below it, the content is dropped or the agent stalls.

candidates.heading: Where to put the spine: harness withSpine vs agentPool({ systemPrompt })
What this produces
candidates.text:
### What this produces

Agents with a prescribed process like "grep first, then search, then read" naturally iterate within those steps:

```
grep(/narrow pattern/)        → 0 matches
search("broad query")         → finds relevant section
read_file(L83-100)            → reads section, identifies a connection
grep(/broader|OR|pattern/)    → 20 matches across 7 lines
read_file(L25-55)             → reads 30-line span for full context
search("specific hypothesis formed from reading")  → confirms thesis
report(findings)
```

The last search query is the signature behavior. The agent read a section, identified a structural connection the original query didn't mention, and constructed a natural-language search specifically targeting that connection. This is multi-hop reasoning — not "search and report" but "search, form hypothesis, search for confirmation."

An agent that greps with a narrow pattern and gets 0 matches will broaden the pattern on its next grep — not because it's prompted to retry, but because the 0-match result is in its context and the model's next-token prediction naturally adjusts. An agent that reads a section and discovers an unexpected connection will construct a search query targeting that specific connection — the read result is in context, and the model forms a hypothesis from it.

candidates.heading: Make it yours
candidates.text:
## Make it yours

The whole orchestration is one parameter — `orchestrate`. Drop in any of these to reshape the harness:

```typescript
// Independent topics — fan out, no spine extension. Each agent runs on the same GPU.
orchestrate: parallel(topics.map(t => ({ content: t, systemPrompt: RESEARCH_PROMPT, seed })))

// A landscape pass that informs N parallel domain agents
orchestrate: fanout(landscapeStep, domainSteps)

// Arbitrary dependency graph — research two topics in parallel, compare, synth
orchestrate: dag([
  { id: 'research_A', task: { content: topicA, systemPrompt: RESEARCH_PROMPT } },
  { id: 'research_B', task: { content: topicB, systemPrompt: RESEARCH_PROMPT } },
  { id: 'compare',    dependsOn: ['research_A', 'research_B'],
    task: { content: 'Compare A vs B', systemPrompt: COMPARE_PROMPT } },
  { id: 'synth',      dependsOn: ['compare'],
    task: { content: 'Synthesize',     systemPrompt: SYNTH_PROMPT } },
])
```

The `dag` shape is what `examples/compare/` uses — a real harness that compares two subjects across multiple axes in 250 LOC. Read it after this quick-start.

candidates.heading: The reranker — four scoring roles
Research pipeline
1. scoreEntailmentBatch — content vs. original query
candidates.text:
### 1. `scoreEntailmentBatch` — content vs. original query

Used at **content boundaries** to keep retrieved content on the original research intent. Each text is scored against the **original query** (captured in closure when the harness called `Source.createScorer(originalQuery)`).

**Fires at:** SETTLE phase, when a tool result lands and the framework is about to prefill it into the agent's KV. A failing entailment gate causes the result to be summarized or dropped before it pollutes the KV at the call position.

candidates.heading: Search deduplication
candidates.text:
### Search deduplication

Rejects `web_search` if the same query (case-insensitive) was already searched in the lineage.

candidates.heading: Session Trunk as Long-Term Spine
candidates.text:
## Session Trunk as Long-Term Spine

The Session trunk persists across queries in a multi-turn conversation:

1. `handleQuery` runs — agent pools fork from trunk, investigate, prune
2. All pools complete — branches pruned, trunk survives
3. `session.commitTurn(query, answer)` appends to trunk
4. Next query — pools fork from updated trunk, inherit prior conversation

The trunk is the long-term spine across queries. The textual spine is the medium-term spine across task stages within a single query. The KV spine is the short-term spine within a single task's delegation chain.

candidates.heading: 2. scoreRelevanceBatch — dual gate under pressure
candidates.text:
### 2. `scoreRelevanceBatch` — dual gate under pressure

Used in **exploit mode** when KV pressure tightens focus. Scores each text twice — against the per-call tool query *and* against the original query — and returns `min(toolQueryScore, originalQueryScore)`. A chunk has to clear both bars.

**Fires at:** inside the tool (e.g. `SearchTool.execute`) when `ToolContext.scorer` is set and `explore` is `false`. The pool decides which mode the tool runs in based on `KV-pressure`; the tool obeys.

candidates.heading: Problem: Plan produces poor sub-questions
candidates.text:
## Problem: Plan produces poor sub-questions

**Symptom:** Research agents investigate tangential topics. Sub-questions are too broad or too keyword-dense, leading to scattered rather than focused investigation.

**Diagnosis steps:**

1. Check the plan output. Find the `generate:end` event from the planning pass:

```jsonl
{"type":"generate:end","output":"{\"questions\":[{\"text\":\"...\",\"intent\":\"research\"},...]"}
```

2. Compare sub-question quality: Good sub-questions are focused and decompose the query into independent investigable units. Poor sub-questions restate the original query with different keywords or are overly broad.

3. Check whether the plan produced an empty array (passthrough). An empty array means the query goes to research as-is, which is correct for focused queries but wrong for complex multi-part queries.

**Solutions:**
- Revise `tasks/plan.md` to be more specific about decomposition criteria
- Adjust `maxQuestions` to constrain the planner
- Add examples to the plan prompt showing good vs bad decompositions

candidates.heading: Three queries in play
candidates.text:
## Three queries in play

The four scoring methods aren't variants on one concept — they operate on three *distinct* queries the framework keeps separate by design:

| Concept | Scope | Field name in code |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Tool query** | Per tool call | `localQuery` param of `scoreRelevanceBatch` |
| **Agent task** | Per agent lifetime | `reference` param of `scoreSimilarityBatch` |
| **Original query** | Per research invocation | Captured in closure by `createScorer` |

Conflating these produces wrong scores. When you add a new scoring method or trace event, use the field names from this table.

candidates.heading: 5. The tools — src/tools/
The pattern
Pressure-aware exploit/explore
candidates.text:
### Pressure-aware exploit/explore

The framework's `DefaultAgentPolicy` exposes an explore/exploit gate that flips reranker behaviour when KV runs short:

- **Explore mode** (default, headroom available). `SearchTool` ranks chunks against the *tool query* alone — agents discover bridging content adjacent to the answer even when the original query wouldn't surface it.
- **Exploit mode** (under pressure). `SearchTool` re-scores via `scoreRelevanceBatch` — `min(toolQueryScore, originalQueryScore)` — tightening focus at the cost of serendipitous discovery.

The agent doesn't know which mode it's in; the policy decides and the tool obeys. [KV pressure](/under-the-hood/kv-pressure) walks the budget model.

---

candidates.heading: Walkthrough: a search tool
Explore vs exploit
candidates.text:
## Explore vs exploit

The pool tells the tool which mode to score in via `ToolContext.explore`:

- **Explore (default, headroom available).** `SearchTool` scores chunks against the **tool query** alone — agents discover bridging content adjacent to the answer even when the original query wouldn't surface it.
- **Exploit (under pressure).** `SearchTool` calls `context.scorer.scoreRelevanceBatch` — `min(toolQueryScore, originalQueryScore)` — tightening focus at the cost of serendipitous discovery.

The agent never knows which mode it's in; `DefaultAgentPolicy.shouldExplore` decides per dispatch. See [Agent policy](/under-the-hood/agent-policy) for the threshold and [KV pressure](/under-the-hood/kv-pressure) for what counts as "under pressure."

candidates.heading: Pressure-driven agent lifecycle
candidates.text:
## Pressure-driven agent lifecycle

<Steps>
  <Step title="Pool setup">
    `hardLimit >= nBatch` invariant validated. Misconfigured policies throw at `useAgentPool` entry.
  </Step>
  <Step title="ctx.spawn">
    Prefill gate rejects agents whose suffix doesn't fit under softLimit. Agent dropped silently with reason `pressure_init`.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Each tick: PRODUCE critical">
    `pressure.critical` fires → `shouldExit` kills one agent → `recoverInline` extracts in the `hardLimit` reserve. Terminal-tool protection yields in critical.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Each tick: PRODUCE soft-cut">
    Non-terminal tool call with `headroom < 0` → policy nudges with "within N words" budget. Terminal tools pass.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Each tick: SETTLE deferral">
    Oversized tool results defer to next tick. Siblings freeing KV (parallel) or spine extension (chain) restores headroom.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Stall-break">
    If deferred items have no active siblings to free KV, policy's `onSettleReject` is consulted. Nudge → replace result with compact payload. Idle → drop with `pressure_settle_reject`. No hook → `settle_stall_break`.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Recovery + diagnostics">
    Every drop path runs `recoverInline` (unless `onRecovery` returns skip). Diagnostic events: `pool:recoveryProduce`, then exactly one of `pool:recoveryReturn` or `pool:recoveryFailed`.
  </Step>
</Steps>

candidates.heading: Spine compatibility
candidates.text:
## Spine compatibility

Putting the catalog on the harness's outer `withSpine` (rather than on `agentPool` itself) is load-bearing for any harness whose synth runs *post-pool* via a separate `useAgent`. `agentPool({ systemPrompt })` would create a *nested* `withSpine` whose root is pruned at pool exit — chain extensions land on that transient inner spine, and a post-pool `useAgent({ parent: querySpine })` would fork an empty branch.

When the catalog lives on the harness's outer `withSpine`, `agentPool` runs in non-shared mode internally; `extendSpine` writes onto the outer querySpine; synth's `useAgent` forks the same root and sees the chain extensions via attention. See [Continuous-context spine](/under-the-hood/continuous-context-spine) for the spine mechanics.

For pools whose synth runs as a *node inside the same `agentPool`* (a DAG with synth as the terminal node), this distinction doesn't matter — synth's branch forks from the same spineRoot the chain extensions land on.

candidates.heading: Related: rerank-architecture
candidates.text:
## Related: rerank-architecture

The cross-encoder reranker composes the same `Branch` + `BranchStore` primitives over a permanent warm trunk — same physics, different consumer. See [Rerank architecture](/under-the-hood/rerank-architecture) for the trunk / queryBranch / leaves topology and the BM25 first-stage that caps cross-encoder workload at top-K candidates.

candidates.heading: dagWithEvents — orchestrator with TUI hooks

title: Sessions
description: Trunk lifecycle, promotion, and multi-turn conversation deltas.
candidates.text:
title: Sessions
description: Trunk lifecycle, promotion, and multi-turn conversation deltas.
---


A session owns the current conversation state. It tracks the trunk branch — the ongoing conversation — and provides methods to advance it.

```typescript
import { Session } from "@lloyal-labs/sdk";

const session = new Session({ ctx, store });
```

That's it. A session starts with no trunk. The first query creates one.

candidates.heading: Pool setup — shared catalog at querySpine
Why Bridges Exist
candidates.text:
## Why Bridges Exist

Without a bridge, source N+1 re-investigates everything source N already covered. The web source searches for the same topics the corpus source already established. You waste KV budget on redundant retrieval.

A bridge solves this by extracting what was found, what is incomplete, and what is missing. The next source targets gaps instead of re-covering established ground.

candidates.heading: What you grant Lloyal Labs
candidates.text:
## What you grant Lloyal Labs

By submitting a tarball through `harness.dev publish`, you grant Lloyal
Labs a non-exclusive, worldwide, royalty-free license to:

1. **Inspect the tarball** during review — including extracting and
   reading `package.json`, the bundled source, the manifest, and the
   `app.json` metadata.
2. **Sign the approved tarball bytes** with the Lloyal platform Ed25519
   key, producing a manifest carrying that signature.
3. **Distribute the signed tarball + manifest + catalog entry** via the
   `apps.lloyal.ai` channel and any successor channel under the same
   trust roots.
4. **Cache and serve** the signed tarball through Cloudflare R2 and any
   CDN layer in front of it.
5. **Display your handle and the catalog name** publicly in the catalog
   and in any portal Lloyal Labs operates over the channel.

The signature is over the exact bytes you submitted; Lloyal Labs does
not modify your tarball before signing. If a revision is needed, you
submit a new version under a new semver.

candidates.heading: The hardLimit >= nBatch invariant
candidates.text:
## The `hardLimit >= nBatch` invariant

`hardLimit` isn't an arbitrary safety margin — it's the **parallel report budget** for the recovery path. When `pressure.critical` fires and the kill proceeds, `recoverInline` prefills its extraction prompt and runs a single-agent produce/commit loop *inside* the reserved `hardLimit` cells. If that reserve is smaller than the context's `nBatch`, the first recovery decode fails with `llama_decode: failed to find a memory slot for batch of size N` — native OOM.

The pool enforces this at `useAgentPool` entry. If `hardLimit < nBatch`, the pool throws:

```
useAgentPool: Invariant Violation — hardLimit (128) must be >= nBatch (512).
Recovery reserves hardLimit cells for its own decode; if smaller than nBatch,
the next batch allocation will OOM. Increase policy.budget.context.hardLimit
to at least 512.
```

This is a compile-time/startup check, not a runtime surprise. Misconfigured policies fail loudly before any agents spawn.

<Note>
Until `SessionContext.nBatch` is exposed natively, the pool validates against `ContextPressure.ASSUMED_N_BATCH = 512` (llama.cpp's default `n_batch`). Once the native exposure lands, the check reads `ctx.nBatch` directly so policies running against a custom batch size validate against the actual value.
</Note>

candidates.heading: Reserved handles
candidates.text:
### Reserved handles

Currently only `lloyal` is reserved (for Lloyal Labs first-party apps). Every other handle is first-come-first-served, subject to good-faith use.

If you try to register a reserved handle from a non-authorized identity, the server returns `handle-reserved`:

```bash
$ npx harness.dev publishers register --handle lloyal --yes
# harness.dev publishers register: HTTP 409 Conflict
#   error: handle-reserved
```

candidates.heading: Pipeline Shape
candidates.text:
## Pipeline Shape

A representative pipeline:

```
Plan → Research → [Bridge →] Findings Eval → Synthesize → Eval
```

Not every pipeline needs every stage. A simple harness might be `Research → Synthesize`. A multi-source pipeline adds `Bridge` between sources. A high-stakes pipeline adds `Findings Eval` to detect agent disagreement and `Eval` to detect synthesis instability.

Each stage runs inside the [tick loop](/under-the-hood/concurrency). Stages share two coordination patterns:

1. **Single `withSpine` at the harness level.** The shared catalog (system prompt + tool schemas) is amortized at `querySpine` once. All pools and post-pool `useAgent` calls inherit it via prefix-share. See [Mixed-role pools](/under-the-hood/mixed-role-pools).
2. **Spine accumulation via `extendSpine`.** Per-stage findings are prefilled onto `querySpine` as user+assistant turns. Downstream agents fork `querySpine` and attend to the accumulated spine via KV — no re-encoding, no message threading.

candidates.heading: v0.1
candidates.text:
## v0.1

- **`Source` decoupled from orchestration.** Sources now expose data-access tools and chunks (`tools`, `bind`, `getChunks`); the harness drives orchestration via `agentPool()`. A source no longer owns prompts, recursion shape, or policy.
- **`Agent` stabilized as first-class API.** Internal `AgentState` shape is no longer exported; introspect via the `Agent` class (`status`, `result`, `branch`, `traceBuffer`, `currentTool`).
- **`useAgent` / `useAgentPool` introduced** as the dual resource layers; `agent()` is a convenience alias for `useAgent()` with N=1.

candidates.heading: Poll review state
candidates.text:
## Poll review state

```bash
npx harness.dev publish status <submissionId>
```

Returns the current review state for a submission you own. Other publishers' submissions are not visible.

**While pending:**

```
c5eb4751-019d-4930-a59d-1eff795de7f6
  status:    pending
  submitted: 2026-06-08T05:37:43.823Z
```

**After approval:**

```
c5eb4751-019d-4930-a59d-1eff795de7f6
  status:    approved
  submitted: 2026-06-08T05:37:43.823Z
  approved:  2026-06-08T05:38:52.603Z
  reviewer:  review@lloyal.ai
  tarball:   https://apps.lloyal.ai/v1/bundles/lloyal__web-1.0.0.tgz
```

The signed tarball is now live in the catalog. Anyone can `npx harness.dev install lloyal/web` against it.

**After rejection:**

```
c5eb4751-019d-4930-a59d-1eff795de7f6
  status:    rejected
  submitted: 2026-06-08T05:37:43.823Z
  rejected:  2026-06-08T05:38:52.603Z
  reviewer:  review@lloyal.ai
  reason:    Manifest claims tools=[search,fetch] but tarball ships only [search].
             Resubmit with consistent declarations.
```

Address the reviewer's feedback and resubmit (a fresh `harness.dev publish` produces a new `submissionId`). Rejected pending artifacts are retained for 30 days as an appeals window.

candidates.heading: Suspension
candidates.text:
## Suspension

Lloyal Labs may suspend a publisher account — preventing further
submissions, without removing previously approved apps — for:

1. Repeated submission of apps that violate these terms.
2. Abuse of the review pipeline (e.g., spam submissions to exhaust
   reviewer capacity).
3. Behavior that misrepresents Lloyal Labs or another publisher.

Suspended publishers may appeal by emailing
[`support@lloyal.ai`](mailto:support@lloyal.ai).

candidates.heading: Register a publisher handle
candidates.text:
## Register a publisher handle

```bash
npx harness.dev publishers register --handle <handle> [--yes]
```

`<handle>` matches the grammar `[a-z][a-z0-9_-]{1,63}` (see [Scaffolding > Identifier grammar](/cli/scaffolding#identifier-grammar)).

**Example:**

```bash
npx harness.dev publishers register --handle acme

# Publisher ToS (v1): https://docs.lloyal.ai/licensing/publisher-tos
#
# By registering you attest that you have read and accept the publisher Terms of Service.
#
# Accept the publisher ToS? [y/N] y
# registered as acme
#   tosVersion: v1
#   registeredAt: 2026-06-08T02:25:37.976Z
```

Pass `--yes` to skip the interactive ToS prompt (still prints the link — required when running non-interactively):

```bash
npx harness.dev publishers register --handle acme --yes
```

candidates.heading: The canonical channel — apps.lloyal.ai
candidates.text:
## The canonical channel — `apps.lloyal.ai`

Every App published to `apps.lloyal.ai` is reviewed by Lloyal Labs for Source-contract conformance, tool-safety review (which tools are `protected`, what data they touch, what consent they require), manifest validation, and signature provenance before listing. The catalog at `apps.lloyal.ai` is the verified registry that consumers rely on.

The framework points at this verified registry by default. `loadBundle` takes a name, not a URL; the catalog URL (`CHANNEL_CATALOG_URL`) and trust roots (`CHANNEL_TRUST_ROOTS`) are compile-time constants in `@lloyal-labs/rig/src/protocol.ts`. This way, every harness built on HDK loads Apps through the same verified channel by default — Apps interoperate across harnesses, consumers see one coherent ecosystem, and the App protocol does not fragment into incompatible sub-catalogs.

```
                 ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                 │  App author                  │
                 │  harness.dev publish         │
                 │   ↓ build + Ed25519-sign     │
                 │   ↓ upload to apps.lloyal.ai │
                 └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                                │
                ┌───────────────▼────────────────┐
                │  apps.lloyal.ai                │
                │  (Cloudflare R2 + signed       │
                │   catalog.json at /v1/)        │
                └───────────────┬────────────────┘
                                │
                ┌───────────────▼────────────────┐
                │  Harness                       │
                │  yield* loadBundle('jira',     │
                │                    { semver }) │
                │   ↓ resolve via CHANNEL_       │
                │     CATALOG_URL + verify       │
                │     against CHANNEL_TRUST_     │
                │     ROOTS (framework-locked)   │
                │   ↓ AppFactory                 │
                │  registry.enable()             │
                └────────────────────────────────┘
```

candidates.heading: Boot set vs dynamic enable
candidates.text:
## Boot set vs dynamic enable

Two ways an App enters the registry:

**Build-time imports (the boot set).** The harness `import`s factories and hands them to `createAppRegistry({ apps: [...] })`. This is the path the reasoning.run harness uses today — `createWebApp` and `createCorpusApp` are build-time deps.

**Dynamic enable.** `yield* registry.enable(factory)` mid-session. This is the path channel-distributed Apps use: `yield* loadBundle('jira', { semver: '^1.2.0' })` resolves against `apps.lloyal.ai` (URL and trust roots are framework-locked constants), verifies, and returns an `AppFactory`. `enable` then runs it in its own scope. See [Distribution](/build-an-app/distribution).

Both paths converge on the same invariant: the App enters the registry only if (a) the factory body completes without throwing, (b) `defineApp` validation passes, (c) the App protocol version is supported, and (d) an existing entry with the same `name` doesn't already occupy the slot.

candidates.heading: Read a trace
candidates.text:
## Read a trace

Trace files are newline-delimited JSON. Use Python one-liners:

<Tabs>
  <Tab title="Agent trajectory">
```bash
cat trace.jsonl | python3 -c "
import json, sys
for e in (json.loads(l) for l in sys.stdin):
    if e['type'] == 'tool:dispatch' and e['agentId'] == 3:
        print(f\"{e['tool']}({json.dumps(e['args'])[:80]}) [{e['toolIndex']}/{e['toolkitSize']}]\")
"
```
  </Tab>
  <Tab title="KV pressure timeline">
```bash
cat trace.jsonl | python3 -c "
import json, sys
for e in (json.loads(l) for l in sys.stdin):
    if e['type'] == 'pool:open':
        print(f\"OPEN agents={e['agentCount']} headroom={e['pressure']['headroom']}\")
    if e['type'] == 'pool:agentDrop':
        print(f\"DROP agent={e['agentId']} reason={e['reason']}\")
"
```
  </Tab>
  <Tab title="Prefill budget">
```bash
cat trace.jsonl | python3 -c "
import json, sys
for e in (json.loads(l) for l in sys.stdin):
    if e['type'] == 'tool:result':
        print(f\"agent={e['agentId']} {e['tool']:15s} prefill={e['prefillTokenCount']:>5d}\")
"
```
  </Tab>
</Tabs>

candidates.heading: Publishing — the App author flow
candidates.text:
### Publishing — the App author flow

```bash
harness.dev publish   # auth → build → submit to apps.lloyal.ai for review + signing
```

The CLI:

1. Authenticates the publisher against `apps.lloyal.ai`. Two modes: an interactive OAuth flow when run from a developer terminal (short-lived session, no persistent credential on disk), or long-lived service credentials provisioned by Lloyal during publisher onboarding for use in CI environments (read from environment variables, not from disk).
2. esbuilds the App into a single ESM bundle with `@lloyal-labs/*` as externals.
3. Submits the bundle bytes + an unsigned manifest stub (name, version, entry, sizeBytes, peerDependencies) to the apps.lloyal.ai publish endpoint.

The platform side then:

4. Authenticates the publisher token and runs review checks — bundle source conformance, framework-version compatibility, `skill.eta` sanitization, `protected` tool surfacing.
5. Signs the bundle bytes with the Lloyal platform Ed25519 key (the same key listed in `CHANNEL_TRUST_ROOTS`), and writes the bundle + the signed `AppBundleManifest` to R2.
6. Re-signs `catalog.json` (adding/updating the new entry) with the platform key and atomically replaces `https://apps.lloyal.ai/v1/catalog.json`.

There is no "self-hosted catalog" option, and no per-publisher signing keys: publishers authenticate to submit, but every signature on every byte in the catalog comes from the Lloyal platform key after Lloyal's review. The signature on a bundle is the cryptographic record of that review.

candidates.heading: prompts/
candidates.text:
### `prompts/`

The pre-App-protocol source ships these eta templates verbatim:

- `playbooks.eta` — the shared-catalog body rendered onto `querySpine`. (This is the pre-3.0 name for what would now be a registered App's catalog block.)
- `research-web.eta`, `research-corpus.eta` — per-role researcher prompts.
- `compare.eta` — per-axis comparison prompt.
- `synthesize.eta` — final synthesis prompt.

candidates.heading: What loadBundle does
candidates.text:
### What `loadBundle` does

```ts
import { loadBundle } from "@lloyal-labs/rig";

const factory = yield* loadBundle('jira', { semver: '^1.2.0' });
yield* registry.enable(factory);
```

Internally `loadBundle`:

1. Fetches `CHANNEL_CATALOG_URL` (currently `https://apps.lloyal.ai/v1/catalog.json`).
2. Verifies the catalog's Ed25519 signature against `CHANNEL_TRUST_ROOTS`. If verification fails the call rejects — no further fetching, no trust path through a tampered catalog.
3. Looks up `'jira'` in the verified catalog; resolves `^1.2.0` against the published versions; picks the highest match (or rejects with `AppNotFoundError`).
4. Fetches the resolved bundle's manifest, verifies it against `CHANNEL_TRUST_ROOTS`, then fetches the bundle bytes and verifies them against the manifest's signature.
5. Dynamic-`import`s the verified ESM module and returns its default-exported `AppFactory`.

The harness sees one call. Every URL and every verification key is framework-vendored.

candidates.heading: v3.0 — loadBundle resolves by name (pre-launch breaking change)
candidates.text:
## v3.0 — `loadBundle` resolves by name (pre-launch breaking change)

`loadBundle`'s signature changed from `(url, manifest, { trustRoots })` to `(name, { semver })`. Trust roots and catalog URL moved from harness-supplied runtime arguments into compile-time constants in `@lloyal-labs/rig/src/protocol.ts`: `CHANNEL_CATALOG_URL` (`https://apps.lloyal.ai/v1/catalog.json`) and `CHANNEL_TRUST_ROOTS` (Lloyal platform Ed25519 keys — single active key with additive rotation slots for compromise recovery).

What changed:

- `loadBundle(name, { semver })` resolves the requested name against the canonical catalog at `apps.lloyal.ai`, verifies catalog and bundle signatures against `CHANNEL_TRUST_ROOTS`, and returns an `AppFactory`.
- §8.2 first-party build-time inclusion is unchanged: harness vendors bundling Apps they own from a private source still hand factories directly to `createAppRegistry({ apps: [...] })`.
- Catalog format at `https://apps.lloyal.ai/v1/catalog.json`: `{ signedAt, entries: [{ name, versions: [{ version, manifestUrl, bundleUrl, appProtocolVersion, sizeBytes }] }], publisherKeyId, signature }`. Catalog signature (canonical JSON over `{ signedAt, entries, publisherKeyId }`) verified before name resolution; per-bundle signatures verified before dynamic import. Both signatures are produced by the Lloyal platform key — publishers do not sign; Lloyal signs after review.
- `LoadBundleOptions` interface removed from the public surface. Tests inject test values via internal `setTestTrustRoot()` / `setTestCatalogUrl()` helpers in `bundle.ts`, active only when `NODE_ENV === 'test'`.

Migration:

- Any harness calling `loadBundle(url, manifest, { trustRoots })` now calls `loadBundle(name, { semver })`.
- [`harness.dev install <publisher>/<short>[@<semver>]`](/cli/installing) (in `harness.dev@0.4.0`) fetches, signature-verifies, and `npm install`s a signed app from the channel.
- [`harness.dev publish`](/cli/publishing) (in `harness.dev@0.4.0`) packages an app via `npm pack` and submits it to the channel for review + signing. See [`harness.dev publishers register`](/cli/publishers) for the one-time handle claim.

See [Distribution](/build-an-app/distribution) for the mechanics.

candidates.heading: Distribution primitives (channel-canonical)
candidates.text:
### Distribution primitives (channel-canonical)

- **NEW: `loadBundle(name, { semver }): AppFactory`** + **`verifyBundle(bytes, sig64, publicKey)`** + **`BundleVerificationError`** (`@lloyal-labs/rig/bundle.ts`). Resolves names against the canonical catalog at `apps.lloyal.ai`; verifies catalog + bundle signatures against framework-vendored `CHANNEL_TRUST_ROOTS`; dynamic ESM import; returns the bundle's factory ready for `registry.enable`. URLs and trust roots are compile-time constants in `protocol.ts` — the harness cannot override.
- **NEW: `CHANNEL_CATALOG_URL` + `CHANNEL_TRUST_ROOTS` constants** in `@lloyal-labs/rig/src/protocol.ts`. The structural-binding mechanism: to use a different channel, fork rig and patch the constants.
- **NEW: `harness.dev` CLI package + dispatcher.** Released as `harness.dev@0.4.0`. Commands: default-scaffolder (`harness.dev <name>`), `app` (app scaffolder), `publishers register` + `me` (publisher account claim + inspection), `publish` (submit app for review + signing via `npm pack` → quarantine queue), `publish status <id>` (poll submission state), `install <publisher>/<short>[@<semver>]` (fetch + signature-verify + `npm install`). Full reference: [CLI](/cli).

## Contact

For questions about these terms or to report a suspected violation by

candidates.heading: Distribution
candidates.text:
## Distribution

First-party Apps that ship inside the HDK reference harness (`reasoning.run`) are loaded as build-time imports (the harness `import { createWebApp } from '@lloyal-labs/web-app'` and hands the factory to `createAppRegistry`). Third-party Apps — and any App reaching a harness it doesn't own — flow through the canonical **signed-bundle channel** at `apps.lloyal.ai`. The harness resolves Apps by name (`yield* loadBundle('jira', { semver: '^1.2.0' })`); the catalog URL and the trust roots used to verify signatures are **compile-time constants in `@lloyal-labs/rig`** — the harness does not configure them.

The runtime primitives live in `@lloyal-labs/rig` (`loadBundle`, `verifyBundle`, `CHANNEL_CATALOG_URL`, `CHANNEL_TRUST_ROOTS`). The `harness.dev` CLI wraps them for scaffolding, publishing, and installing Apps. [Distribution](/build-an-app/distribution) covers the full flow.

---

candidates.heading: What the ToS covers
candidates.text:
## What the ToS covers

The [Publisher ToS](/licensing/publisher-tos) is short — read it once. The high-level points:

- You retain ownership of your code; you grant Lloyal Labs a license to inspect, sign, and distribute approved tarballs via the channel.
- You agree to submit only non-malicious code that complies with the App protocol and respects third-party licenses.
- Lloyal Labs commits to reviewing every submission, communicating outcomes, and preserving rejected artifacts for 30 days (appeals window).
- The ToS is versioned (`v1`, `v2`, ...). Material revisions require re-attestation (re-run `publishers register`).
